# Automotive Body Materials & Geometry R&D Master v5

**Version:** 5.0 — 15 July 2026  
**Research cut-off:** 15 July 2026  
**Blender production baseline:** 5.0.1  
**Forward-reference environment:** Blender 5.2 LTS  
**Authority:** Single-file, append-only Automotive Content research source  
**Embedded foundation:** Car Paint Research Compendium v12, preserved verbatim

## Governing purpose

This source expands the car-paint R&D system into the complete visible automotive body:

- paint;
- exposed composites;
- metallic and plated accents;
- plastics and elastomers;
- glazing;
- headlamps and taillamps;
- grilles and meshes;
- badges and graphics;
- wheels;
- brakes;
- exhausts and heat-affected metals;
- protective films and wraps;
- weathering, damage and repair;
- game-asset forensics;
- mesh rehabilitation;
- normals, tangents, UVs and material recovery;
- reflection-based geometry validation;
- Blender modifiers, Geometry Nodes and automation.

The complete embedded paint corpus remains active. It is not summarized away.

## Evidence policy

The status vocabulary from V12 remains binding:

- `DOC-V`: official documentation verified;
- `SRC-V`: implementation source located;
- `PAPER-V`: primary research verified;
- `STD-V`: standard/specification verified;
- `OEM-V`: official manufacturer disclosure;
- `COMM`: community observation;
- `INFERENCE`: direct technical consequence;
- `HYP`: original project proposal;
- `LIVE-5.0.1`: executed in Blender 5.0.1;
- `OBS`: visually observed;
- `MOTION-V`: motion validated;
- `PROD`: production accepted for a declared scope.

No new material or modifier recommendation is considered `LIVE-5.0.1` merely because it is plausible.

## Scope boundary

“Automotive body” includes every exterior or externally visible material that materially affects the vehicle’s rendered identity. It also includes the geometry and shading systems needed to represent those materials correctly.

Mechanical simulation, crash engineering, aerodynamic CFD and full interior upholstery are outside the default scope unless they directly affect visible body geometry or materials.

# Part I — Embedded car-paint foundation

The following Car Paint Research Compendium v12 is preserved verbatim.

# Car Paint Research Compendium v12

**Scope:** Physically grounded automotive paint research for Blender 5.x, with emphasis on Blender 5.0.1 compatibility, EEVEE/Cycles workflows, material architecture, and validation.

**Version:** 12.0 - 15 July 2026

## V10 canonical topic map

V10 is a single-file, append-only research and implementation source. Nothing in this map replaces the full text. It exists only to improve retrieval across the complete corpus.

| Canonical ID | Domain | Search terms and connected subjects |
|---|---|---|
| CP10-ARCH | Layer architecture | basecoat, effect pigment, clearcoat, tinted clear, tri-coat, candy, PPF |
| CP10-BULK | Finite-thickness pigment transport | Kubelka–Munk, radiative transfer, hiding power, substrate, opacity, K/S |
| CP10-FLAKE | Effect-pigment field | position-normal, orientation, morphology, coverage, sparkle, grain, mottle |
| CP10-FILTER | Filtering and LOD | Bump Filter Width, LEAN, LEADR, NDF moments, derivatives, mipmaps |
| CP10-COAT | Coat optics and topography | Fresnel, roughness, haze, DOI, orange peel, attenuation, absorption |
| CP10-PROCESS | Manufacturing causality | atomization, spray overlap, rheology, drying, shrinkage, flake alignment |
| CP10-METRO | Surface and appearance metrology | ASTM, CIE, ISO 25178, PSD, autocorrelation, wavelets, gloss, RIQ |
| CP10-GEOM | Geometry and reflection quality | Class-A, G0–G3, zebra, reflection lines, true normal, curvature |
| CP10-SPECTRAL | Spectral and polarimetric appearance | hyperspectral, hpBRDF, Mueller, Stokes, metamerism, fluorescence |
| CP10-CAPTURE | Reference capture and inverse rendering | RTI, near-field lighting, polarization, HDR, PBIR, optimization |
| CP10-CAMERA | Camera, temporal and display pipeline | PSF, bloom, clipping, shutter, motion blur, denoise, TAA, tone mapping |
| CP10-CONDITION | Surface condition and ageing | scratches, swirls, oxidation, chips, PPF, ceramic coating, wax |
| CP10-NODES | Blender node behaviour | Gabor, Voronoi, White Noise, Bump, AOV, Light Path, Principled |
| CP10-GN | Geometry Nodes preprocessing | attributes, blur, raycast, proximity, Poisson placement, bake |
| CP10-OSL | OSL and custom shading | Dx, Dy, filterwidth, area(P), filtered Gabor, stochastic flakes |
| CP10-AUTO | Automation and regression | version guard, graph audit, render matrix, AOV ledger, headless tests |
| CP10-RND | Research governance | provenance, contradictions, uncertainty, evidence grades, negative results |
| CP10-SPECIAL | Special and future finishes | structural colour, fluorescent, photochromic, thermochromic, electrochromic |

### Authority rule

The complete V10 text remains authoritative. The map, experiment ledgers, accepted groups, summaries and indexes are navigation structures only. They must never be used to justify ignoring adjacent or contradictory evidence.

### Final-version baseline

- Production implementation remains constrained to Blender 5.0.1 unless a project decision explicitly changes it.
- Blender 5.2 LTS is a forward-reference environment, not permission to introduce incompatible nodes or APIs.
- Every external addon, standard, website, marketplace product and compatibility claim is a point-in-time record and must be rechecked when acted upon.

## Core conclusions

1. Automotive paint is a layered dielectric coating system, not simply a metallic surface with a clearcoat checkbox.
2. Visible flakes and distant face/flop behaviour are different scale regimes. A fixed procedural noise pattern cannot represent both correctly.
3. The body/panel normal, buried flake orientation, and clearcoat topography are separate phenomena and should not share one normal input.
4. Pearlescent colour travel is not the same as Fresnel tinting or coloured glitter.
5. Lighting geometry is part of material validation. One HDRI cannot prove a shader is physically credible.

## Recommended Blender layer architecture

### Layer 0 - Geometry and panel normal
Macro body shape, smoothing and manufactured panel continuity. Weighted normals should only compensate for topology limitations, not conceal broken shading indefinitely.

### Layer 1 - Basecoat/body response
The main colour and broad scattering response. A solid paint may stop here before the coat. Metallic and pearl paints add a separate effect-pigment response.

### Layer 2 - Buried effect pigment
Sparse, oriented, scale-aware metallic or interference flakes beneath the clearcoat. Density, physical scale, orientation variance and lobe width should be independent controls.

### Layer 3 - Clearcoat
A dielectric outer interface with its own IOR, roughness and normal. Ordinary clearcoat reflection should be mostly neutral.

### Layer 4 - Clearcoat topography
Orange peel, polishing waviness and very fine clearcoat defects. These belong primarily in Coat Normal, not merely in roughness.

### Layer 5 - Surface condition
Dust, water, fingerprints, scratches and contamination sit above the clearcoat and should remain optional.

# Selected research sources

## 1. Real-Time Image-Based Lighting of Metallic and Pearlescent Car Paints

**Authors:** Tom Kneiphof and Reinhard Klein  
**Year:** 2021  
**Source:** https://ssrn.com/abstract=3988576  
**DOI:** 10.2139/ssrn.3988576

### Why retained
The strongest direct treatment of the transition from visible sparkle to smooth angular colour variation under image-based lighting.

### Research findings
- Simple spatially varying BRDFs do not capture metallic and pearlescent paint adequately.
- Sparkle is high-frequency in both spatial and angular domains.
- Individual sparkle tends to vanish with distance and with larger light sources.
- The remaining homogeneous response still carries angle-dependent brightness and colour.
- A useful real-time decomposition separates brightness response from angular colour variation.

### Blender implications
- Do not keep one procedural flake pattern equally visible at every shot scale.
- Build a close-range sparkle regime and a distant smooth face/flop regime.
- Judge the shader under both small sharp emitters and broad studio sources.
- Treat the environment map as part of the material evaluation, not merely a background.

## 2. Efficient Acquisition and Realistic Rendering of Car Paint

**Authors:** Johannes Guenther, Tongbo Chen, Michael Goesele, Ingo Wald and Hans-Peter Seidel  
**Year:** 2005  
**Source:** https://www.sci.utah.edu/~wald/Publications/2005/CarPaint/carpaint.pdf

### Why retained
Foundational measured-car-paint work showing why ad-hoc reflection tuning often produces plastic-looking cars.

### Research findings
- Automotive paint identity is defined by directional response, not one RGB colour.
- Measured paint can be fitted to a compact BRDF for efficient rendering.
- A physically motivated fit outperforms arbitrary Phong-style parameter tweaking.

### Blender implications
- Match face, mid-angle and grazing behaviour separately.
- Never approve a paint from one hero angle.
- Use a repeatable turntable and controlled light rig for fitting.

## 3. Discrete Stochastic Microfacet Models

**Authors:** Wenzel Jakob, Milos Hasan, Ling-Qi Yan, Jason Lawrence, Ravi Ramamoorthi and Steve Marschner  
**Year:** 2014  
**Source:** https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~ravir/stochastic.pdf  
**DOI:** 10.1145/2601097.2601186

### Why retained
The clearest explanation of why resolvable glints cannot be represented faithfully by a conventional smooth microfacet BRDF or ordinary bump noise.

### Research findings
- Conventional microfacet models assume many unresolved facets per pixel and therefore produce smooth highlights.
- When facets become individually resolvable, the response becomes stochastic and glinty.
- The visible pattern depends on pixel footprint, light direction and view direction.
- Proper filtering is required to avoid aliasing and unstable temporal sparkle.

### Blender implications
- Noise-to-bump is not equivalent to discrete metallic flakes.
- Hero close-ups need sparse orientation-varying glints rather than dense white grain.
- Animation tests are mandatory because a still can hide temporal crawling.
- Projected flake size must influence visibility and filtering.

## 4. Procedural Physically Based BRDF for Real-Time Rendering of Glints

**Authors:** Xavier Chermain, Basile Sauvage, Jean-Michel Dischler and Carsten Dachsbacher  
**Year:** 2020  
**Source:** https://igg.unistra.fr/People/chermain/assets/pdf/Chermain2020Procedural.pdf  
**DOI:** 10.1111/cgf.14141

### Why retained
A practical bridge from glint theory to a procedural and energy-aware model suitable for interactive rendering.

### Research findings
- Glittering appearance can be generated procedurally without explicitly modelling every facet.
- A physically based model should conserve energy and preserve facet-distribution statistics.
- Multiscale evaluation is central to correct sparkle across distance.

### Blender implications
- Separate flake density, orientation distribution and lobe width.
- Do not use one texture to drive colour, roughness and normal together.
- A procedural flake group should expose scale-aware controls and a stable random seed.

## 5. A General Micro-Flake Model for Predicting the Appearance of Car Paint

**Authors:** Serkan Ergun, Sermet Onel and Aydin Ozturk  
**Year:** 2016  
**Source:** https://diglib.cgv.tugraz.at/bitstreams/5bffc42d-b5e8-4374-8c0b-259a42eb0b11/download

### Why retained
Useful physical model of layered anisotropic scattering media with translucent microflakes.

### Research findings
- Car paint is a layered scattering structure rather than an exposed metallic surface.
- Flake orientation and transmittance matter.
- Multiple scattering changes the observed response and cannot be reduced to one metallic slider.

### Blender implications
- Treat flakes as buried in a coloured medium beneath the coat.
- Use the Metallic control cautiously: paint is often a dielectric coating containing metallic inclusions, not bulk coloured metal.
- Reserve spectral or volumetric approximations for hero paints where colour travel justifies the cost.

## 6. A General Framework for Pearlescent Materials

**Authors:** Inigo Guillen et al.  
**Year:** 2020  
**Source:** https://giga.cps.unizar.es/~iguillen/publications/Guillen_TOG2020_Pearlescence.pdf  
**DOI:** 10.1145/3414685.3417782

### Why retained
The best broad reference here for pearlescence, interference pigments and angular chromatic behaviour.

### Research findings
- Pearlescence is not merely coloured metallic sparkle.
- Interference pigments create angular hue travel as well as brightness change.
- Response depends on pigment structure, orientation and layer composition.

### Blender implications
- Give pearl paint its own mode rather than treating it as metallic paint with coloured noise.
- Separate neutral clearcoat Fresnel from pigment-driven colour travel.
- Approximate colour travel with a calibrated angular function when spectral rendering is unnecessary.

## 7. Simulation of Light Scattering in Automotive Paints

**Authors:** Sergey Ershov et al.  
**Year:** 2023  
**Source:** https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/11/11/2429  
**DOI:** 10.3390/math11112429

### Why retained
Modern open-access treatment of pigment particles and metallic or pearlescent flakes dispersed inside a transparent varnish.

### Research findings
- Automotive coatings contain colourant particles and thin flat flakes inside a clear medium.
- Continuous-medium approximations are faster but less exact than particle-level models.
- Layer composition influences the final BRDF.

### Blender implications
- Build a coherent base medium before adding sparkle.
- Dark metallic paints should retain body colour outside direct glints.
- Absorption and scattering can be artistically approximated before the flake layer.

## 8. A Computer Aided Color Appearance Design System for Metallic Car Paint

**Authors:** Clement Shimizu and Gary W. Meyer  
**Year:** 2015  
**Source:** https://www.clementshimizu.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/10/shimizu_meyer-JIST2015.pdf  
**DOI:** 10.2352/J.ImagingSci.Technol.2015.59.3.030403

### Why retained
Connects CG paint design to automotive multi-angle measurement practice and the vocabulary of face, flop and hue travel.

### Research findings
- Automotive colour is commonly measured at multiple aspecular angles.
- Face-to-flop colour travel can be represented with compact angle-dependent functions.
- Several CIE Lab readings are more informative than one RGB swatch.
- Interference finishes require broader angular treatment than solids.

### Blender implications
- Build reference boards from several viewing and lighting angles.
- Expose face colour, flop colour and transition shape as deliberate controls.
- Use measured or photographed multi-angle references where possible.

## 9. A Method for Fitting Measured Car Paints to a Game Engine

**Authors:** Tom Kneiphof and Reinhard Klein  
**Year:** 2018  
**Source:** https://diglib.eg.org/bitstream/handle/10.2312/mam20181196/027-031.pdf  
**DOI:** 10.2312/mam.20181196

### Why retained
Production-oriented reference for compressing measured behaviour into a constrained real-time shading model.

### Blender implications
- In EEVEE, prioritise face/flop, coat response and scale-aware sparkle before exotic effects.
- Maintain separate Cycles and EEVEE variants only when the visual gain justifies divergence.

## 10. Blender Manual - Principled BSDF

**Publisher:** Blender Foundation  
**Source:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/latest/render/shader_nodes/shader/principled.html

### Why retained
Official definition of the current coat layer, including coat weight, roughness, IOR, tint and independent Coat Normal.

### Blender implications
- Use Coat Normal for orange peel or clearcoat waviness instead of corrupting every lobe with one global bump.
- Keep ordinary clearcoat reflection mostly neutral.
- Confirm exact socket names and behaviour against the installed Blender version before scripting.

## 11. MaterialX Physically Based Shading Nodes Specification

**Publisher:** Academy Software Foundation / MaterialX contributors  
**Source:** https://materialx.org/assets/MaterialX.v1.38.PBRSpec.pdf

### Why retained
Strong production reference for vertical layering, energy accounting and separate normals across BSDF layers.

### Research findings
- Vertical coating is physically different from horizontally mixing two materials.
- A coat changes how much light reaches and exits the substrate.
- Separate layers can require separate normals.
- Blindly adding reflection lobes can violate energy conservation.

### Blender implications
- Prefer Principled coat or an energy-aware layered design over Add Shader stacks.
- Do not use arbitrary Mix Shader factors as a substitute for clearcoat Fresnel.
- Document which imperfections belong above, within or below the coat.

# Common failure modes

- **Paint looks like coloured aluminium:** The base is being treated as bulk metal. Use a dielectric clearcoat over a coloured base medium with a separate flake response.
- **Paint looks like plastic:** Face/flop response is absent or weak, or lighting lacks broad reflected shapes.
- **Flakes look like sand or dust:** Noise is too dense, too diffuse, or changing colour instead of producing sparse directional glints.
- **Sparkle crawls in animation:** The pattern is undersampled, screen-space unstable or not filtered by projected scale.
- **Orange peel looks like rough plastic:** Orange peel was placed in roughness instead of primarily in Coat Normal at a larger spatial scale.
- **Reflections are tinted everywhere:** Base colour is leaking into the clearcoat lobe.
- **Material only works under one HDRI:** Parameters were tuned to a lighting accident.
- **Dark paint loses all body colour:** The base medium lacks scattering and is dominated by black metal plus coat.
- **Pearl paint looks like metallic glitter:** Angular chromatic travel is missing.
- **Every panel sparkles identically:** Flake scale ignores object scale, UV scale and shot distance.

# Validation protocol

1. **Neutral softbox:** One large rectangular emitter, black surroundings and neutral colour management. Reveals coat continuity and curvature.
2. **Small-area light:** Reveals discrete sparkle and whether flakes behave directionally.
3. **Distance ladder:** Macro, close, medium and full-car renders. Sparkle should converge smoothly.
4. **Turntable:** At least 180 degrees with fixed lights. Check face/flop continuity and temporal stability.
5. **Grazing strip reflection:** Reveals orange peel, normal errors and excessive roughness.
6. **Flat and curved samples:** Flat isolates angular colour; curved verifies real body-form behaviour.
7. **Solid/metallic/pearl A-B:** Same base hue and light, ensuring categories differ for correct physical reasons.
8. **EEVEE/Cycles comparison:** Compare lobe balance and temporal behaviour, not pixel identity.

# Recommended node-group controls

- Paint mode: solid / metallic / pearl / colour-shift
- Face colour and flop colour
- Face-to-flop transition shape
- Basecoat roughness and scattering strength
- Flake physical scale, density and orientation variance
- Flake lobe width and intensity
- Sparkle distance or pixel-footprint fade
- Coat weight, IOR and roughness
- Orange-peel scale, amplitude and anisotropy
- Optional panel waviness, micro-scratches, dust and wetness

# Implementation rules for this project

- Keep body normal, flake normal and coat normal conceptually separate.
- Use Principled Coat Normal for clearcoat topography where possible.
- Avoid Add Shader stacks that double-count reflected energy.
- Do not drive several unrelated material channels from one noise texture.
- Treat EEVEE and Cycles as separately validated targets.
- Before scripting, verify exact socket names and behaviour in the installed Blender build.
- The current project requirement is Blender 5.0.1 compatibility.

# Future additions

- Measured BRDF datasets and multi-angle paint sample databases
- OEM/coatings-industry documents on orange peel, DOI and wave-scan measurement
- Blender 5.x example node groups for solid, metallic and pearl paint
- Temporal anti-aliasing tests for flake sparkle in EEVEE
- Colour-management and exposure reference tests
- Real macro photography of clearcoat, flakes, scratches and polishing defects

# Changelog

- **v1.0 - 15 July 2026:** Established research hierarchy, material architecture, detailed source cards, Blender implications, failure modes and validation protocol.


# V2 expansion: coating science, industrial appearance metrics and Blender implementation

## What changed in version 2

Version 1 established the core physically based structure. Version 2 adds the missing bridge between measured automotive appearance and a practical Blender workflow:

- a taxonomy of solid, metallic, mica/pearl and interference finishes;
- coating-stack anatomy and the location of each visible defect;
- industrial appearance quantities: gloss, haze, distinctness of image, orange peel, wet look, sparkle and graininess;
- multi-angle colour and effect measurement geometry;
- a source-to-node-to-test decision matrix;
- Blender 5.x/OpenPBR implementation notes;
- real-time versus path-traced priorities;
- acquisition and reference-photography rules;
- a formal acceptance checklist for production materials.

The aim is not to make one giant "car paint" shader. It is to define a family of paint models whose controls correspond to real appearance mechanisms.

# 1. Automotive coating anatomy

A modern body finish is better thought of as an optical stack than as a single material. A simplified stack is:

1. **Metal or composite substrate.** Normally invisible unless paint is chipped.
2. **Pretreatment/e-coat.** Primarily corrosion protection; normally not optically exposed.
3. **Primer/surfacer.** Controls adhesion, filling and substrate uniformity.
4. **Basecoat.** Carries absorption pigments and, for effect colours, aluminium, mica or interference flakes.
5. **Clearcoat.** A mostly transparent dielectric layer that supplies gloss, weather resistance and the dominant outer reflection.
6. **Post-process surface state.** Polishing marks, dust, water, fingerprints, scratches, oxidation and contamination.

This hierarchy matters in CG because every visible phenomenon belongs at a different depth. A scratch in the clearcoat should distort or break the coat reflection without necessarily changing basecoat colour. A chip may expose primer or substrate. Metallic flakes must remain beneath a continuous outer reflection.

## 1.1 Layer placement rules

| Phenomenon | Physical location | Blender placement |
|---|---|---|
| Panel waviness | Geometry/substrate | Geometry, subdivision, careful normals |
| Base hue | Basecoat | Base component of Principled or dedicated base lobe |
| Aluminium/mica flakes | Within basecoat/binder | Buried effect-pigment approximation |
| Pearl colour travel | Effect pigment within basecoat | Angle-dependent pigment layer, not coat tint |
| Clearcoat gloss | Outer dielectric interface | Principled Coat |
| Orange peel | Clearcoat topography and coating flow | Primarily Coat Normal / low-frequency bump |
| Micro-scratches | At or within coat surface | Coat roughness/normal mask, directional where appropriate |
| Dust/water | Above clearcoat | Topmost optional layer |
| Paint chip | Missing coat/basecoat | Geometry or mask revealing deeper layers |

# 2. Finish taxonomy

## 2.1 Solid colour

Solid paint uses ordinary absorption pigments without deliberate effect flakes. It still needs a clearcoat, angular Fresnel behaviour, appropriate roughness and subtle surface topography. It should not be treated as perfectly Lambertian plastic.

**Typical CG failure:** adding metallic sparkle because the artist assumes all modern paint contains visible flakes.

**Implementation priority:** base colour accuracy, neutral coat, controlled orange peel and correct reflected-shape quality.

## 2.2 Metallic paint

Metallic paint contains reflective flakes, commonly aluminium, suspended and preferentially aligned in the basecoat. The flakes create directional lightness change and visible sparkle under suitable illumination.

Two visual scales must be separated:

- **sparkle:** point-like or small-area flashes under direct illumination;
- **flop/lightness travel:** the broad change in brightness as illumination and view move relative to the panel.

**Typical CG failure:** a static bright noise texture that is visible under every light and every camera distance.

## 2.3 Mica and pearlescent paint

Mica or coated platelets can create a softer, often coloured effect. Depending on pigment construction, the paint may show chromatic travel rather than only lightness travel.

**Typical CG failure:** colouring metallic speckles while leaving the broad angular body response unchanged.

## 2.4 Interference and colour-shift paint

Interference pigments produce strong spectral changes with geometry. RGB renderers can approximate the appearance for a known shot range, but a single simple Fresnel colour ramp is not a general physical solution.

**Typical CG failure:** assigning an arbitrary rainbow ramp to Layer Weight, causing every edge to cycle through colours regardless of lighting direction.

## 2.5 Matte and satin automotive coatings

These still have a layered structure, but the top surface or additives broaden reflection. They should not be represented only by raising a single roughness value until the car becomes chalky. Surface scattering, haze and the balance between broad reflection and body colour must remain believable.

# 3. Industrial appearance quantities artists should know

Automotive appearance is not evaluated by one "roughness" number. Industrial instruments and visual assessments separate several attributes that CG workflows routinely collapse.

## 3.1 Specular gloss

Gloss describes reflected intensity around a specified specular geometry. It does not fully describe reflected-image sharpness. Two panels can have similar gloss readings yet visibly different orange peel or DOI.

**Blender consequence:** matching highlight brightness is not enough. The shape and sharpness of reflected lines must also be checked.

## 3.2 Haze

Haze is low-angle scattering around the specular reflection. It creates a milky halo or bloom around bright reflected features.

**Blender consequence:** haze is not necessarily equivalent to simply increasing coat roughness. A broad low-energy shoulder around a sharper reflection may be needed for close product work.

## 3.3 Distinctness of image (DOI)

DOI describes how sharply a reflected image is reproduced. Fine surface structures blur reflected edges even when the surface remains glossy.

**Blender consequence:** use a black-and-white stripe card or hard-edged studio reflection to judge reflected-image sharpness. Do not judge DOI using only a round softbox.

## 3.4 Orange peel

Orange peel is visible waviness in a glossy coating. It redirects reflected light according to local surface slope and produces a light/dark pattern. It spans spatial scales and changes character with viewing distance.

Industry literature distinguishes fine structures that reduce DOI at close range from broader waviness that affects the "wet look" at greater distances. BYK documentation notes that visibility depends on structure size, observing distance and image-forming quality; broad structures around the millimetre scale dominate at greater distances.

**Blender consequence:** orange peel should be multiscale:

- a very subtle finer component affecting reflected-edge sharpness;
- a broader, extremely low-amplitude waviness that bends long strip-light reflections;
- optional directional/process variation rather than isotropic noise everywhere.

It belongs primarily in coat normal/topography. Increasing roughness alone destroys the reflection rather than gently warping it.

## 3.5 Sparkle area and sparkle intensity

Under direct illumination, effect flakes produce discrete bright features. Industrial effect instruments separate how much image area is occupied by sparkle from how intense the sparkle is.

**Blender consequence:** flake density and glint intensity should be separate controls. A shader with one "flake strength" slider cannot independently match sparse bright flakes and dense weak flakes.

## 3.6 Graininess

Under diffuse illumination, effect coatings can show a coarse or fine mottled texture even without sharp glints. This is commonly called graininess.

**Blender consequence:** graininess is a low-contrast diffuse/effect variation, not the same signal as direct-light sparkle. It needs a separate, more heavily filtered component.

## 3.7 Face, flop and travel

- **Face:** appearance near the specular/face-on condition used in effect-colour evaluation.
- **Flop:** change in lightness, and sometimes colour, between face and off-angle observations.
- **Travel:** the broader angular path of colour/lightness change.

**Blender consequence:** use several fixed view/light geometries and record colour values or rendered swatches. One viewport screenshot cannot define an effect paint.

# 4. Multi-angle colour and effect measurement

Portable automotive spectrophotometers commonly evaluate colour at several aspecular angles rather than one view. Industry examples use geometries around 15, 25, 45, 75 and 110 degrees relative to specular for effect finishes. These angles are not magic Blender defaults; they are a reminder that angular appearance must be sampled across a deliberate range.

## 4.1 Blender approximation rig

Create a flat, correctly scaled paint panel with:

- a fixed directional or compact area source;
- one camera/light plane;
- cameras or turntable stops spanning near-specular to grazing observations;
- a neutral surround and fixed exposure;
- a colour chart or neutral reference outside the reflective path.

Render at least five angular states. Record:

- average body colour away from direct glints;
- highlight brightness and width;
- sparkle density and peak intensity;
- reflection sharpness;
- hue/lightness travel.

This rig is more useful for material development than immediately testing on a complex car body, because curvature continuously changes the geometry and makes diagnosis harder.

# 5. Source-to-Blender decision matrix

| Physical question | Best source class | Blender representation | Validation test |
|---|---|---|---|
| Is the coat vertically layered? | MaterialX/OpenPBR | Principled Coat or energy-aware layer | Compare coat on/off without doubling energy |
| Are flakes individually visible? | Stochastic microfacet/glint papers | Sparse, oriented, scale-filtered glints | Macro render under compact source |
| What remains when flakes disappear? | Measured car-paint/IBL papers | Smooth angular face/flop response | Distance ladder under broad source |
| Is the effect metallic or pearl? | Micro-flake/pearlescence papers | Separate lightness and chromatic travel | Multi-angle colour series |
| Is orange peel correct? | Coatings appearance literature | Multiscale Coat Normal | Stripe-card and strip-light reflections |
| Is reflected image sharp enough? | DOI/haze literature | Coat microstructure and roughness balance | Black/white edge reflection |
| Does the paint grain under diffuse light? | Effect-instrument literature | Separate filtered graininess signal | Large diffuse dome/area lighting |
| Will animation sparkle crawl? | Stochastic filtering papers | Stable object-space seed and footprint filtering | Slow camera dolly/turntable |
| Is the material portable? | OpenPBR/MaterialX | Physical control names and translation map | Export/import parameter audit |

# 6. Blender 5.x and OpenPBR implementation notes

Blender's modern Principled BSDF is based on OpenPBR and exposes a dedicated coat layer, including a separate Coat Normal. This makes it possible to keep panel shading, basecoat/effect behaviour and clearcoat topography conceptually separate.

## 6.1 Recommended node ownership

- **Global Normal:** macro panel and modelling normal only.
- **Base Normal:** normally the clean panel normal; use cautiously for broad basecoat variation.
- **Coat Normal:** orange peel, polish waviness and coat-local defects.
- **Base Roughness:** body/base scattering character, not orange peel.
- **Coat Roughness:** microscopic blur of the outer reflection.
- **Coat IOR:** dielectric interface strength; do not use it as an arbitrary brightness control.
- **Coat Tint:** reserve for genuinely tinted varnish, not ordinary body colour.

## 6.2 Why Add Shader is usually wrong

Adding a glossy lobe over an already reflective base can double-count energy. A physical coat reflects some incident energy and transmits the rest to the substrate; it also affects the light exiting the substrate. Vertical layering therefore cannot be recreated by blindly summing two full-strength shaders.

## 6.3 One texture should not drive everything

A common tutorial setup feeds one Noise Texture into colour, roughness and bump. This creates artificial correlation:

- bright flakes become rough or dented in exactly the same places;
- coat topography follows buried pigment distribution;
- graininess and sparkle share one scale;
- the material reads as procedural noise rather than a manufactured coating.

Use independent but physically related signals, with explicitly documented scales.

## 6.4 Coordinate and scale discipline

Flake and orange-peel scales must be defined in real object space. Applying scale inconsistently or using Generated coordinates on differently proportioned panels changes the material identity.

For reusable assets:

- apply object scale or compensate mathematically;
- use object-space coordinates tied to a stable reference object;
- store physical scale controls in metres/millimetres conceptually;
- test the same material on a flat coupon and a full-scale panel.

## 6.5 EEVEE priorities

For EEVEE, preserve the perceptually dominant effects first:

1. clean panel normals and continuous coat reflections;
2. broad face/flop response;
3. distance-aware sparkle visibility;
4. stable temporal behaviour;
5. subtle orange peel;
6. only then finer spectral or volumetric approximations.

A physically elaborate effect that aliases or crawls is less useful than a simplified stable model.

## 6.6 Cycles priorities

Cycles can support more complex lobe interaction and cleaner stochastic sampling, but it does not automatically make a poor material physically correct. The same layer architecture and scale discipline still apply. Sample counts should be judged specifically on glint stability and reflected-edge quality.

# 7. Proposed car-paint node-group architecture

## Group A - Paint classification and body response

Inputs:

- paint mode: solid / metallic / pearl / interference;
- face colour;
- flop colour;
- transition width and bias;
- body/base roughness;
- body scattering strength;
- optional dark-angle compensation.

Output: stable base response without discrete sparkle.

## Group B - Effect pigment

Inputs:

- physical flake scale;
- number density;
- orientation spread;
- glint lobe width;
- glint intensity;
- diffuse graininess amount and scale;
- pearl colour pair or angular palette;
- stable random seed;
- near/mid/far filtering thresholds.

Outputs:

- buried effect contribution;
- optional effect normal or mask;
- diagnostic masks for sparkle and graininess.

## Group C - Clearcoat

Inputs:

- coat weight;
- coat IOR;
- coat roughness;
- fine DOI-loss scale/strength;
- broad orange-peel scale/strength;
- directional waviness;
- polishing micro-scratch amount.

Output: Principled coat parameters and Coat Normal.

## Group D - Surface condition

Inputs:

- dust amount;
- water state;
- fingerprints;
- scratch/chip masks;
- dirt accumulation.

This group must remain removable so the underlying paint can be validated cleanly.

## Diagnostic outputs

Every production node group should expose temporary outputs for:

- base response only;
- coat only;
- sparkle mask;
- graininess mask;
- angular travel factor;
- orange-peel normal;
- final combined material.

Without diagnostics, artists often compensate for one incorrect layer by damaging another.

# 8. Reference acquisition protocol

## 8.1 Minimum reference set

For a specific paint, collect:

- neutral daylight or calibrated broad-light image;
- direct-sun or compact-source image showing sparkle;
- overcast/diffuse image showing graininess;
- close macro showing flake scale;
- grazing strip-light or workshop image showing orange peel;
- several angles around the same panel;
- one image containing a neutral object or known colour reference where possible.

## 8.2 What not to infer from dealership images

Dealership and configurator imagery may include:

- heavy grading and local contrast;
- polarisation;
- composited reflections;
- retouching of orange peel and panel defects;
- non-neutral studio walls;
- clipped highlights;
- camera white-balance differences.

Use them for design intent and angular character, not as absolute colour measurement.

## 8.3 Macro image scale calibration

A macro photograph is useful only when flake size can be estimated. Seek a known feature, panel gap, dust particle scale, measurement target or manufacturer pigment specification. Otherwise use it qualitatively rather than assigning exact millimetres.

# 9. Lighting rigs for paint development

## 9.1 Neutral softbox rig

Purpose: coat continuity, body curvature and broad face/flop.

- black or dark neutral environment;
- one large rectangular source;
- optional black flags;
- fixed exposure;
- no decorative HDRI.

## 9.2 Strip-light rig

Purpose: DOI, orange peel and panel-normal defects.

- long narrow emitters or emissive cards;
- hard-edged black/white transitions;
- camera at grazing and mid angles;
- slow turntable animation.

## 9.3 Compact-source sparkle rig

Purpose: discrete glints.

- compact bright area source rather than an infinitesimal point where possible;
- dark surroundings;
- macro and medium camera distances;
- animation to expose temporal instability.

## 9.4 Diffuse graininess rig

Purpose: graininess without direct sparkle.

- broad dome or very large area illumination;
- suppress direct compact highlights;
- compare effect paint with solid control sample.

## 9.5 Outdoor validation

Purpose: real-world balance.

Use at least:

- overcast diffuse sky;
- direct sun plus sky;
- urban/high-frequency reflected environment;
- low sun/golden-hour grazing light.

Do not tune exclusively outdoors because uncontrolled reflections can disguise layer errors.

# 10. Colour management and exposure

Car paint contains very bright reflections next to dark body colour. Incorrect exposure or display transforms can make flakes appear too large, coat reflections too broad, or dark colours unnaturally crushed.

Rules:

- lock exposure during comparisons;
- retain unclipped highlight information during material development;
- compare paint variants under the same view transform;
- evaluate both HDR energy and final display appearance;
- avoid compensating for a display transform by changing physical material parameters prematurely.

A glint that clips to a large white blob may be a tone-mapping/exposure problem rather than a flake-size problem.

# 11. Parameter discipline and naming

Use names that describe physical roles:

Good:

- `flake_density`
- `flake_orientation_spread`
- `sparkle_intensity`
- `graininess_amount`
- `face_flop_bias`
- `coat_orange_peel_strength`
- `coat_doi_loss`

Bad:

- `magic_detail`
- `metal_noise`
- `shine_boost`
- `carpaint_factor_2`

Each control should have:

- a unit or conceptual scale;
- a documented default;
- a stated layer;
- a diagnostic view;
- a known visual failure when pushed too far.

# 12. Production acceptance checklist

A car paint is not approved until it passes all applicable tests.

## Geometry and normals

- Panel reflections remain continuous across intended surfaces.
- Weighted normals are not hiding inverted normals, duplicate geometry or destructive topology.
- Material detail remains stable when object transforms are applied.

## Coat

- Clearcoat reflection is continuous over flakes.
- Coat colour is neutral unless tinted varnish is intentional.
- Orange peel bends reflections subtly rather than making the surface look rocky.
- Reflected stripe edges have believable DOI.

## Effect pigment

- Sparkle appears under direct/compact light and reduces under broad diffuse light.
- Sparkle converges with distance rather than remaining fixed-size noise.
- Graininess can be tuned separately from sparkle.
- Flakes do not alter the outer coat normal everywhere.
- Animation does not crawl or pop unacceptably.

## Angular colour

- Solid, metallic and pearl variants remain visibly distinct for the correct reasons.
- Face/flop response survives on both flat coupon and curved body.
- Pearl hue travel does not behave as a generic edge rainbow.

## Renderer and shot

- EEVEE and Cycles versions preserve the same material identity.
- The material works under neutral studio, strip, compact-source and outdoor tests.
- Exposure and colour management are fixed during comparisons.

# 13. Additional retained sources

## 12. OpenPBR Surface specification

**Publisher:** Academy Software Foundation  
**Source:** https://academysoftwarefoundation.github.io/OpenPBR/

OpenPBR defines a cross-DCC layered surface model intended for practical VFX and animation materials. It is relevant because Blender's current Principled BSDF follows this model. The specification is the best source for parameter semantics and the relationship between base, coat and other layers.

## 13. Blender Manual - Specular BSDF

**Publisher:** Blender Foundation  
**Source:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/latest/render/shader_nodes/shader/specular_bsdf.html

The current node includes a clear-coat component intended for materials such as car paint. It is useful as an implementation reference and for understanding Blender's alternate layered surface node, although the Principled path remains the preferred common workflow unless a specific need justifies otherwise.

## 14. BYK QC Solutions for Coatings

**Publisher:** BYK-Gardner / BYK Instruments  
**Source:** https://media.byk-instruments.com/shop/Misc/Brochures/Solutions/EN/QC-Solutions-for-Coatings-2018-EN.pdf

This industry document is retained for its concise explanation of orange peel, DOI, wet look, sparkle area, sparkle intensity and graininess. It also demonstrates that flake size can affect both direct-light sparkle and diffuse-light graininess. This is not a BRDF paper; its value is translating real paint-quality vocabulary into measurable appearance attributes.

## 15. Assessment of Paint Appearance Quality in the Automotive Industry

**Type:** Doctoral thesis, Brunel University  
**Source:** https://bura.brunel.ac.uk/bitstream/2438/7900/1/FulltextThesis.pdf

This thesis is retained as a deeper coatings/appearance reference, particularly for orange peel and the difficulty of defining paint appearance with one metric.

## 16. CIE Expert Symposium on Colour and Visual Appearance - effect coatings

**Publisher:** International Commission on Illumination  
**Source:** https://files.cie.co.at/912_CIE%20Symposium%20Prague%202016%20-%20Abstract%20Booklet.pdf

The proceedings include work on multi-angle colour characterisation and gonioapparent materials. The key retained principle is that effect coatings can require many illumination/observation geometries; some diffraction pigments are not adequately characterised by ordinary portable multi-angle instruments.

## 17. Predicting and Measuring the Perceived Texture of Car Paints

**Authors:** Eric Kirchner and Jyotsna Ravi  
**Type:** Automotive appearance research

Retained because it explicitly treats the perceived texture of car paint, including orange peel and sparkle, rather than only average colour. It supports keeping topography and effect texture as separate perceptual targets.

# 14. Source authority and usage rules

Not all sources answer the same question.

- **Peer-reviewed CG papers:** strongest for BRDFs, stochastic filtering and rendering algorithms.
- **OpenPBR/MaterialX/Blender documentation:** strongest for node semantics and production-layer architecture.
- **CIE and metrology literature:** strongest for angular measurement and colour-appearance terminology.
- **Coatings-instrument documents:** strongest for practical appearance metrics and industrial vocabulary, but may also promote specific instruments.
- **Artist tutorials:** useful only after their claims are checked against the categories above.

When sources disagree, prefer the source closest to the question. Do not use a Blender manual page to make claims about real flake physics, and do not use a coatings brochure to infer the exact implementation of Blender's BSDF.

# 15. Research gaps for version 3

The next useful expansion should target:

1. measured BRDF/BTF datasets that can legally be stored and sampled;
2. exact Blender 5.0.1 socket behaviour and shader-code differences from later 5.x manuals;
3. temporal filtering strategies practical in EEVEE node graphs or shader code;
4. spectral approximations for selected pearl/interference paints;
5. measured or manufacturer-specified flake-size distributions;
6. orange-peel spatial-frequency models and real scan data;
7. a Blender scene containing the four standard validation rigs;
8. a first implementation of Solid, Metallic and Pearl node groups with diagnostic outputs.

# Changelog

- **v2.0 - 15 July 2026:** Added coating anatomy, finish taxonomy, industrial appearance metrics, multi-angle measurement, source-to-node matrix, Blender/OpenPBR implementation, node-group architecture, reference acquisition, lighting rigs, colour-management guidance, acceptance checklist and five new source classes.
- **v1.0 - 15 July 2026:** Established core research hierarchy, layer architecture, source cards, failure modes and validation protocol.

# Exhaustive Literature and Production Expansion — v3

**Research cut-off:** 15 July 2026  
**Purpose of this expansion:** Close the largest readiness gaps left in v2: primary-source coverage, quantitative ranges, measured data, automotive-industry terminology, current OpenPBR structure, filtering/temporal research, and a more actionable implementation plan.

## Evidence hierarchy used in this document

Sources are ranked in this order:

1. Peer-reviewed primary computer-graphics or coatings research.
2. Official standards, metrology projects and production shading specifications.
3. Official Blender documentation.
4. Automotive OEM, instrument-manufacturer and effect-pigment technical literature.
5. Public measured-material databases.
6. Secondary tutorials or artist demonstrations, used only for workflow comparison and never as the physical authority.

Commercial instrument literature is useful for terminology, measurement geometry and perceptual metrics, but should not be treated as neutral proof of one vendor's algorithm being uniquely correct.

# Automotive coating stack: quantitative working model

A practical OEM paint stack commonly contains:

- electrocoat / corrosion-protection layer;
- primer or filler/surfacer;
- colour basecoat;
- optional effect-pigment content within the basecoat;
- clearcoat.

Exact film builds vary by process, chemistry, factory and vehicle class. The shader does not need to model every physical layer volumetrically, but its optical structure should preserve the important hierarchy: dielectric clearcoat above a coloured/effect-bearing substrate.

## Safe working ranges for Blender R&D

These are starting ranges, not universal manufacturer specifications:

| Property | Research-grounded working range | Blender use |
|---|---:|---|
| Clearcoat dry film build | approximately 20–60 µm in cited coating studies | Use as conceptual depth; do not model as literal geometry except macro/section work |
| Metallic effect-flake diameter | commonly around 5–45 µm for many commercial aluminium effect pigments | Establish true physical scale before deciding whether flakes should resolve |
| Pearl/mica effect-pigment size | commonly tens of micrometres; many practical distributions lie around 10–60 µm, with specialty products extending outside this | Controls luster, sparkle scale and angular colour structure |
| Orange-peel spatial structure | roughly 0.1–30 mm across industrial wave bands | Drive Coat Normal at several scales, not one generic noise frequency |
| Thin-film interference scale | film thickness comparable to visible wavelengths; OpenPBR parameterizes thickness in micrometres | Only appropriate for interference/colour-shift systems, not ordinary metallic paint |
| Clearcoat IOR | use physically plausible dielectric values, generally around the common polymer range rather than arbitrary extremes | Start near 1.45–1.55 unless sample evidence suggests otherwise |

The clearcoat-IOR range above is a conservative production starting point rather than a universal measured automotive value. Treat it as a calibration prior and adjust only against reference.

# Flake morphology and orientation

Flake appearance depends on more than size:

- diameter and thickness/aspect ratio;
- cornflake, silver-dollar, lenticular or vacuum-metallized morphology;
- surface roughness;
- concentration;
- orientation distribution relative to the panel;
- binder refractive index and absorption;
- clearcoat/basecoat interaction;
- illumination solid angle.

Published optical work on aluminium coatings shows that flake orientation strongly changes the directional reflectance distribution. Better alignment parallel to the panel tends to strengthen directional metallic flop and mirror-like response; broader orientation distributions spread highlights and can increase off-angle sparkle.

## Blender consequence

Do not encode flakes with a single scalar noise map.

The minimum useful procedural representation needs:

1. **Presence field** — where flakes statistically occur.
2. **Orientation field** — local flake normals drawn from a controlled distribution.
3. **Morphology proxy** — narrow versus broad reflector response.
4. **Scale/filter term** — whether flakes are resolvable by the current pixel footprint.
5. **Pigment class** — aluminium, mica/interference, glass, or other effect pigment.
6. **Embedding response** — coloured medium through which incoming and outgoing light travel.

# Industrial appearance vocabulary mapped to Blender

## Gloss

Gloss concerns the amount of reflected light near the specular direction. It is not the same as image clarity.

**Blender proxy:** coat/base roughness and lobe energy.

## Haze

Haze is near-specular scattering that creates a milky halo around reflections.

**Blender proxy:** a broader low-amplitude near-specular component or carefully controlled secondary rough lobe. Do not confuse with volumetric fog.

## Distinctness of image (DOI)

DOI describes how sharply reflected structures are imaged. A surface can have high gloss but poor DOI.

**Blender proxy:** clearcoat normal waviness and near-specular broadening. Validate with reflected stripe cards, not only white highlights.

## Orange peel

Orange peel is mesoscopic waviness that distorts reflections across multiple spatial bands. Industrial wave-scan systems analyze structure from sub-millimetre dullness through millimetre-to-centimetre waviness.

**Blender proxy:** multiband Coat Normal, primarily in object or stable UV space, with physical-scale controls.

## Sparkle

Sparkle is the direct-illumination appearance of discrete bright effect-pigment events.

**Blender proxy:** sparse directional glints whose visibility depends on light direction, view direction, flake orientation and pixel footprint.

## Graininess / diffuse coarseness

Graininess is the coarser non-uniform texture seen under diffuse illumination when sharp sparkle is suppressed.

**Blender proxy:** low-frequency spatial modulation of the buried effect-pigment response, distinct from sharp glints and distinct from dirt.

## Face, flop and travel

- **Face:** appearance near the specular/face viewing condition.
- **Flop:** change in lightness and sometimes chroma/hue away from face.
- **Travel:** broader angular colour path through several aspecular geometries.

**Blender proxy:** calibrated angular substrate/effect response, not just Layer Weight multiplied into Base Color.

# Public measured-material resources

## RGL-EPFL material database

The RGL material database includes spectral and RGB measured BRDFs covering metals, papers, car paints and other materials. Spectral coverage extends from ultraviolet into near-infrared with dense sampling.

**Use in this project:**

- select car-paint examples as directional-response references;
- compare analytic Blender approximations against measured lobe shape;
- do not assume a homogeneous BRDF contains spatial sparkle.

## UTIA BRDF/BTF database

The UTIA database provides measured BRDFs and BTFs, including anisotropic and spatially varying materials. Its public BRDF collection contains many measured samples with dense light/view direction grids.

**Use in this project:**

- inspect effect-coating and anisotropic material behaviour;
- study BTF-style spatial/angular variation;
- use as a conceptual reference for sparkle/graininess separation.

## MERL BRDF database

MERL provides 100 densely measured isotropic BRDFs for research use.

**Use in this project:**

- useful for general BRDF fitting, perceptual editing and lobe-comparison tools;
- not a complete car-paint solution because ordinary BRDFs lack spatial texture and discrete flakes.

## Legacy car-paint datasets and AxF

Earlier car-paint work captured several measured paints and hybrid BRDF/BTF data. Modern automotive appearance exchange often uses AxF-style representations that combine homogeneous reflectance with spatial effect information.

**Use in this project:**

- treat AxF as an industry reference architecture;
- do not assume Blender can ingest full AxF fidelity directly;
- copy the conceptual decomposition: homogeneous directional response + spatial effect texture + scale-aware rendering.

# Current OpenPBR structure relevant to car paint

The official OpenPBR Surface specification v1.1.1 describes a physically specified layered model with:

- a base substrate that statistically mixes metal and dielectric components;
- an optional dielectric coat slab above the base;
- optional thin-film interference associated with the base interface;
- energy-conserving layering rather than arbitrary shader addition;
- distinct coat IOR and roughness;
- optional absorbing coat medium.

## Implications for Blender

1. A clearcoat is a vertical layer, not a simple artistic mix.
2. Coat reflection reduces and modifies energy reaching the base.
3. Ordinary automotive clearcoat should normally remain a dielectric, mostly neutral layer.
4. Colour-shift paint may require thin-film/interference behaviour in the effect pigment or base interface, not a tinted coat.
5. Custom node groups should expose artist controls while mapping them to physically meaningful layer parameters.
6. OpenPBR explicitly warns that specialized materials may require bespoke shading beyond a generic uber-shader. Automotive sparkle is one such specialist case.

# Blender 5.x implementation readiness

## What Principled already handles well

- dielectric coat interface;
- coat weight;
- coat roughness;
- coat IOR;
- coat tint/absorption-style control where supported;
- independent Coat Normal;
- base dielectric/metal mixture;
- production-friendly layered energy behavior.

## What Principled does not solve by itself

- discrete flake statistics;
- BTF-style spatial/angular sparkle;
- automatic pixel-footprint sparkle filtering;
- industrial sparkle-area/intensity metrics;
- diffuse graininess;
- measured multi-angle fitting;
- physically calibrated pearlescent pigment distributions.

## Recommended division of labour

Use Principled for:

- broad base substrate;
- base specular;
- clearcoat interface;
- clearcoat roughness and normal;
- general energy handling.

Use custom nodes/textures for:

- face/flop correction;
- buried effect-pigment response;
- graininess;
- scale-aware sparkle mask;
- angular colour travel;
- diagnostic outputs.

Do not replace Principled with a hand-built Add Shader stack unless a measurable limitation has been demonstrated.

# Filtering and temporal stability research

The main unresolved implementation problem is not generating sparkles; it is generating sparkles that remain plausible across resolution, distance and animation.

## Failure mechanisms

- procedural frequencies exceed the pixel sampling rate;
- random patterns are evaluated inconsistently frame to frame;
- denoisers remove or smear isolated bright glints;
- TAA interprets glints as unstable subpixel noise;
- camera-distance fades pop because they ignore projected footprint;
- motion blur stretches glints unnaturally;
- image textures lack appropriate mip filtering;
- one flake occupies much less than a pixel but still contributes full intensity.

## Required tests

1. Render at 25%, 50%, 100% and 200% resolution.
2. Move the camera slowly and rapidly across the same painted panel.
3. Rotate the light independently of the camera.
4. Compare stills with and without denoising.
5. Compare EEVEE temporal accumulation against Cycles sampling.
6. Test broad area light, compact area light and near-point source.
7. Test flake texture in Object, UV and Generated coordinates.
8. Compare camera-distance fade against a screen-space or derivative-informed approximation.
9. Track mean reflected energy while changing flake density.
10. Verify that distant paint converges into a stable homogeneous response.

## Practical fallback hierarchy

If analytic glints are not feasible in stock Blender nodes:

1. use a high-resolution, tileable, mipmapped flake-orientation texture;
2. derive separate orientation and density channels;
3. blend to a homogeneous fitted response as footprint grows;
4. maintain stable object/UV coordinates;
5. avoid frame-randomized procedural noise;
6. clamp/firefly-handle only after confirming the shader is not over-energized;
7. make Cycles and EEVEE variants only where necessary.

# Quantitative reference-capture protocol

A useful real-paint reference set should contain:

## Geometry

- flat coupon;
- mild convex panel;
- compound-curvature automotive panel;
- sharp crease and broad shoulder.

## Lighting

- broad rectangular softbox;
- long narrow strip;
- compact bright source;
- diffuse hemispherical light;
- black and white reflection cards.

## Camera

- locked exposure and white balance;
- RAW capture where available;
- several aspecular angles;
- macro close-up;
- full-panel distance;
- polarized and unpolarized pairs if possible.

## Required observations

- face colour;
- intermediate travel;
- grazing flop;
- highlight width;
- coat reflection neutrality;
- orange-peel structure;
- direct-light sparkle;
- diffuse graininess;
- transition with distance;
- defects above the coat.

# Source-to-node-to-test matrix

| Physical phenomenon | Strong source class | Blender implementation target | Validation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clearcoat interface | OpenPBR, MaterialX, Blender manual | Principled Coat | Fresnel/grazing strip-light test |
| Coat waviness | automotive appearance metrology | Coat Normal, multiband physical scales | reflected stripe-card DOI test |
| Base colour/scattering | coatings optics, measured BRDF | Principled base + calibrated diffuse/specular | flat coupon under broad source |
| Metallic flop | measured car-paint BRDF | angular response modifier | fixed-light turntable |
| Pearl colour travel | interference-pigment research, OpenPBR thin film | calibrated angular colour function | multi-angle colour comparison |
| Sharp sparkle | stochastic microfacet/glint research | sparse orientation-aware glint layer | compact-light animation |
| Graininess | industrial effect-coating studies | diffuse spatial effect layer | diffuse-dome test |
| Scale convergence | car-paint IBL and glint filtering research | footprint/distance transition | resolution and distance ladder |
| Haze | appearance metrology | broad near-specular lobe | black-card reflection test |
| Surface contamination | top-layer reference photography | optional masks above coat | raking-light close-up |

# Revised production architecture

## Node Group A — CP_Base

Inputs:

- face colour;
- flop colour;
- base roughness;
- base IOR/specular control;
- dielectric/metal balance;
- scattering strength;
- angular travel curve.

Outputs:

- base substrate BSDF or parameter bundle;
- diagnostic base colour;
- diagnostic angular factor.

## Node Group B — CP_EffectPigment

Inputs:

- pigment class;
- physical flake size;
- density;
- orientation spread;
- morphology;
- brightness;
- colour/interference travel;
- random seed;
- graininess scale;
- sparkle visibility scale.

Outputs:

- effect colour/energy;
- flake orientation perturbation;
- sparkle mask;
- graininess mask;
- diagnostics.

## Node Group C — CP_Coat

Inputs:

- coat weight;
- IOR;
- roughness;
- absorption/tint;
- orange-peel short, medium and long bands;
- polish waviness;
- micro-scratch contribution.

Outputs:

- Principled-compatible coat parameters;
- Coat Normal;
- DOI diagnostic.

## Node Group D — CP_SurfaceCondition

Inputs:

- dust;
- fingerprints;
- water;
- scratches;
- polishing haze;
- contamination masks.

These effects sit above the coat and must be disabled for clean-paint calibration.

## Master Group — CP_AutomotivePaint

Modes:

- solid;
- metallic;
- pearl;
- interference/colour-shift;
- satin/matte clearcoat;
- optional aged/repaired finish.

The master group should expose a reduced artist panel while preserving advanced controls inside subgroups.

# Readiness reassessment after v3 research

| Area | v2 | v3 assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Conceptual understanding | Strong | **Very strong** |
| Avoiding common artist mistakes | Strong | **Very strong** |
| Primary-source coverage | Good but secondary-heavy | **Strong; key primary CG, coatings and official sources identified** |
| Citation reliability | Needed audit | **Strong enough for project R&D; final bibliographic audit still required for publication** |
| Quantitative realism | Incomplete | **Moderate-to-strong; useful scale ranges and metrology bands now available** |
| Measured ground truth | Weak | **Moderate; several public BRDF/BTF databases identified, but no project-specific paint sample captured yet** |
| Blender-specific implementation | Partially specified | **Strong architecture; unresolved sparkle filtering remains** |
| Production efficiency | Not proven | **Good planning readiness; requires one reusable .blend laboratory and node implementation** |
| Reusable shader system | Not built | **Fully specified, not yet implemented** |
| Objective validation | Designed, not executed | **Detailed protocol ready, execution pending** |
| EEVEE temporal stability | Unknown | **Research-backed risk identified; must be experimentally characterized** |
| Colour management | Partial | **Known dependency; still needs a dedicated Blender test appendix** |

# What cannot be solved by more literature alone

The following are now experimental rather than research bottlenecks:

1. Exact EEVEE behaviour for subpixel sparkles in the chosen Blender 5.x build.
2. Interaction with the project's chosen resolution, TAA settings and motion.
3. Cycles denoiser treatment of sparse firefly-like glints.
4. Whether a node-only footprint approximation is sufficient.
5. Final artist-facing control ranges.
6. Performance cost on the user's RTX 4070 laptop.
7. Visual match to a chosen real vehicle paint.

These require a controlled `.blend` test scene and repeatable renders.

# Additional retained sources

## Primary computer-graphics research

- Günther, Chen, Goesele, Wald and Seidel — *Efficient Acquisition and Realistic Rendering of Car Paint*.
- Rump, Müller, Sarlette, Koch and Klein — *Photo-realistic Rendering of Metallic Car Paint from Image-Based Measurements*.
- Jakob et al. — *Discrete Stochastic Microfacet Models*.
- Chermain et al. — *Procedural Physically Based BRDF for Real-Time Rendering of Glints*.
- Kneiphof and Klein — *Real-Time Image-Based Lighting of Metallic and Pearlescent Car Paints*.
- Kneiphof and Klein — *A Method for Fitting Measured Car Paints to a Game Engine*.
- Guillen et al. — *A General Framework for Pearlescent Materials*.
- Filip et al. — effect-coating appearance acquisition and sparkle/graininess research.
- Shimizu and Meyer — multi-angle metallic-paint design system.
- Ershov et al. — simulation of scattering in automotive paints.

## Coatings and optical measurement

- NIST / coating literature on aluminium flake orientation and optical reflectance.
- Automotive OEM paint-material and process presentations.
- Research on clearcoat/basecoat interdiffusion and flake orientation.
- Research on orange-peel texture, DOI and clearcoat-thickness effects.
- Effect-coating visual-assessment work on sparkle and graininess.
- EURAMET material-appearance metrology work covering BRDF, sparkle and gloss.
- BYK technical literature on wave-scan, DOI, sparkle and graininess.
- Konica Minolta technical literature on image clarity and surface roughness.

## Production shading and Blender

- OpenPBR Surface specification v1.1.1.
- OpenPBR implementation companion paper.
- MaterialX PBR specification.
- Blender 5.x Principled BSDF documentation.
- Blender 5.x Bump and coordinate documentation.
- Blender 5.x colour-management and rendering documentation.

## Public measured data

- RGL-EPFL material database.
- UTIA BRDF/BTF database.
- MERL BRDF database.
- Public/legacy car-paint BRDF/BTF datasets linked from original research where still available.
