- labels;
- decals;
- panel graphics;
- localized damage/repaired areas.

Retain the core paint on the car body.

### Texel Density Checker

Use whenever image-based masks or flakes must remain physically consistent across multiple panels.

## 10.2 Car-paint products worth auditing

### CC0 Automotive Paint Shader — BlendSwap

Value:

- free;
- separate base/coat normals;
- transparent architecture to dissect.

Limitation:

- procedural normal-distortion sparkle;
- no demonstrated measured fitting;
- no demonstrated footprint filtering.

Decision:

- mandatory benchmark/dissection candidate.

### CarPaint Collection

Claims:

- large preset collection;
- adjustable flakes;
- Cycles and EEVEE;
- Asset Browser workflow.

Decision:

- potentially productive;
- test five representative paints using the lab before purchase/adoption.

Audit set:

1. solid black;
2. fine silver metallic;
3. coarse dark metallic;
4. pearl white;
5. colour-shift paint.

### Car Paint Generator for Blender 5.0

Value:

- current-version targeting;
- may reveal efficient parameterization.

Decision:

- audit graph complexity, dependencies, temporal stability and layer separation before recommendation.

### Advanced Car Paint Shader

Value:

- procedural controls;
- possible quick baseline.

Decision:

- treat as a candidate benchmark, not physical authority.

### Udin Blender Shaders car-paint library

Claims:

- extensive car-paint material catalogue;
- mathematically generated shader options.

Decision:

- inspect free/full-math sample first;
- verify current Blender compatibility;
- compare graph against the validation matrix.

### Automotive Material Pack / MOUVAL Automotive Material Library

Value:

- broad automotive-supporting materials;
- useful for interiors, plastics, rubber and trim.

Decision:

- consider for whole-car productivity;
- do not assume core paint accuracy from library size.

### PBR Advanced

Claims car-paint features such as:

- edge tint;
- flakes;
- coat;
- orange peel;
- thin-film controls.

Decision:

- audit whether these are correctly assigned to separate layers;
- test whether edge tint is a view-only cheat;
- verify colour travel under moving light.

### Car Shader Kit

Published compatibility information found during this audit points to older Blender ranges in some documentation. Do not adopt without direct 5.0.1 testing.

## 10.3 General libraries

### Extreme PBR Nexus

Better suited to:

- asphalt;
- concrete;
- studio surroundings;
- plastics;
- general environment material acquisition.

It is not the first purchase for solving car-paint optics.

### Substance 3D Painter/Designer

Strong for:

- masks;
- scratches;
- dust;
- chips;
- reusable procedural texture sources;
- AxF inspection in supported tools.

Weak for:

- direct reproduction of a complete dynamic automotive flake BTF in standard metal/rough output.

### Material Maker

Strong for:

- open-source procedural source maps;
- orange peel;
- grain;
- scratches;
- stable flake masks.

## 10.4 Utility tools

### Node Wrangler

Enable for authoring speed.

### Material Batch Tools

Useful where many material instances/panels require coordinated updates. Confirm exact operations and back up before batch changes.

### Asset Browser

No addon is required to create a disciplined reusable car-paint library. The master node groups should remain standard Blender assets wherever possible.

---

# 11. Addon scoring framework

Score every candidate from 0–5:

| Criterion | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Compatibility | Verified in Blender 5.0.1 |
| Portability | Scene survives without addon or has clean fallback |
| Transparency | Node/code structure inspectable |
| Physical ownership | Effects assigned to correct layer |
| Temporal stability | Passes animation test |
| Scale discipline | Real units / consistent texture scale |
| Performance | Measured cost acceptable |
| Baking support | Spatial-only bake path |
| Asset Browser integration | Reusable without fragile setup |
| Maintenance | Recent updates and clear support |

Reject or quarantine any product that:

- hides core shader logic;
- requires permanent online access;
- breaks file portability;
- adds unverified handlers/drivers;
- produces unstable animation;
- cannot distinguish coat from base;
- bakes view-dependent effects into static textures.

---

# 12. Source-strength classification

## Tier A — primary authority

- peer-reviewed CG papers;
- CIE/ASTM/ISO standards;
- OpenPBR/MaterialX specifications;
- official Blender documentation;
- NIST/EURAMET work;
- measured database documentation.

Use for physical and measurement claims.

## Tier B — technical industry evidence

- pigment manufacturer catalogues;
- instrument-manufacturer technical notes;
- OEM coating/process presentations;
- AxF documentation;
- renderer vendor documentation.

Use for product ranges, industrial vocabulary and implementation patterns. Note commercial context.

## Tier C — production/practitioner evidence

- addon documentation;
- marketplace product pages;
- artist shader breakdowns;
- open-source shader code.

Use for workflow candidates and practical experiments, not for declaring physical truth.

## Tier D — low-authority content

- generic tutorials;
- unattributed node screenshots;
- social-media recipes;
- AI-generated material pages.

Do not include unless a specific reproducible technique merits testing.

---

# 13. Gaps that genuinely remain after V5

## 13.1 Proprietary data

Much of the highest-fidelity automotive appearance data is proprietary:

- OEM measured paint libraries;
- instrument-vendor databases;
- AxF assets;
- commercial renderer implementations;
- pigment formulations;
- process-specific flake orientation data.

The public literature cannot reproduce those databases.

## 13.2 Exact Blender flake BSDF

Blender does not currently expose a dedicated official stochastic automotive-flake BSDF comparable to specialist nodes in some other renderers.

The remaining options are:

- approximate node graph;
- OSL/custom work;
- explicit geometry;
- image/BTF-inspired texture approach;
- external renderer/reference pipeline.

## 13.3 Standardized sparkle matching

CIE work remains active. Internal metrics can be rigorous, but should not be misrepresented as final international standard measurements.

## 13.4 Project-specific calibration

No research can determine the correct final controls for:

- a specific Pagani paint;
- a chosen reference car;
- the project’s lighting style;
- delivery resolution;
- EEVEE sampling;
- the user’s desired art direction.

Those require the lab and references.

---

# 14. Final V5 readiness verdict

| Area | Readiness |
|---|---|
| General car-paint physics | Very strong |
| Measured BRDF/BTF literature | Strong |
| Spectral/angle-dependent literature | Strong |
| Flake morphology/orientation | Strong |
| Automotive standards | Current and strong |
| Industrial pigment ranges | Strong enough for physical presets |
| AxF/digital material architecture | Strong |
| Blender layer mapping | Very strong |
| Blender stock-node production path | Fully specified |
| Cycles OSL research path | Specified, unproven |
| Explicit geometry macro path | Specified, unproven |
| Addon/product landscape | Broad and critically classified |
| Objective validation | Strong internal protocol |
| Exact stochastic flake implementation | Experimental gap |
| Project-specific paint match | Requires reference and rendering |
| Production speed | High planning readiness; must be measured in practice |

## Stop condition for broad research

Broad car-paint literature searching should now stop only because it has reached diminishing returns, not because the subject is exhausted.

Continue research when:

- Blender changes Principled/OpenPBR behavior;
- a new official flake node appears;
- CIE publishes a finalized sparkle/graininess scale;
- a usable AxF/MaterialX importer appears;
- a shader test reveals a specific unresolved phenomenon;
- a new paint category is added;
- a selected commercial addon needs auditing.

The next highest-value deliverable is the Blender 5.0.1 laboratory and the first implementation benchmark.

---

# 15. V5 annotated priority reading list

## Essential physical/rendering core

1. **Rump et al. — Photo-realistic Rendering of Metallic Car Paint from Image-Based Measurements**  
   Why: hybrid analytical BRDF plus spatial/BTF response.  
   Blender use: justifies separating body response from effect texture.  
   https://diglib.eg.org/items/3aa1c40e-54d8-4038-8516-eee4b6e2c968

2. **Günther et al. — Efficient Acquisition and Realistic Rendering of Car Paint**  
   Why: measured fitting and efficient rendering.  
   Blender use: design the controlled fitting scene and compact production model.  
   https://www.sci.utah.edu/~wald/Publications/2005/CarPaint/carpaint.pdf

3. **Jakob et al. — Discrete Stochastic Microfacet Models**  
   Why: mathematical basis of resolvable glints and footprint dependence.  
   Blender use: prevents misuse of ordinary roughness noise as flakes.  
   https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~ravir/stochastic.pdf

4. **Chermain et al. — Procedural Physically Based BRDF for Real-Time Rendering of Glints**  
   Why: procedural, multiscale glint rendering.  
   Blender use: blueprint for an OSL/custom research branch.  
   https://igg.unistra.fr/People/chermain/assets/pdf/Chermain2020Procedural.pdf

5. **Kneiphof and Klein — Real-Time Image-Based Lighting of Metallic and Pearlescent Car Paints**  
   Why: environment integration and distance/light-size response.  
   Blender use: LOD and homogeneous-convergence design.  
   https://ssrn.com/abstract=3988576

6. **Guillen et al. — A General Framework for Pearlescent Materials**  
   Why: spectral/interference treatment.  
   Blender use: separates pearl colour travel from ordinary metallic sparkle.  
   https://giga.cps.unizar.es/~iguillen/publications/Guillen_TOG2020_Pearlescence.pdf

## Essential physical coating/metrology core

7. **NIST — Aluminium flake orientation and optical reflectance**  
   Why: measured flake geometry and orientation distributions.  
   Blender use: tangent-biased orientation field and area weighting.  
   https://tsapps.nist.gov/publication/get_pdf.cfm?pub_id=860367

8. **CIE spectral gonio-imaging work**  
   Why: spectral colour plus image-based sparkle metrics.  
   Blender use: reference-capture protocol and event metrics.  
   https://files.cie.co.at/x046_2019/x046-OP32.pdf

9. **ASTM E2194-14(2025)**  
   Why: current metal-flake multi-angle measurement.  
   Blender use: three-condition metallic validation.  
   https://store.astm.org/e2194-14r21.html

10. **ASTM E2539-25**  
    Why: current interference-flake measurement.  
    Blender use: expanded pearl/colour-shift angular validation.  
    https://www.astm.org/Standards/E2539.htm

11. **CIE JTC 12**  
    Why: current status of sparkle/graininess standardisation.  
    Blender use: prevents false claims of standards compliance.  
    https://cie.co.at/technicalcommittees/jtc-12-measurement-scales-sparkle-and-graininess

12. **Clearcoat thickness and surface roughness study**  
    Why: separates coat film build, DOI/orange peel and flake orientation effects.  
    Blender use: independent coat/topography controls.  
    https://www.paint.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/Effect-of-Clearcoat-Thickness-on-Surface-Roughness.pdf

## Essential production architecture

13. **OpenPBR Surface**  
    Why: current layered production material model.  
    Blender use: coat/substrate energy ownership.  
    https://academysoftwarefoundation.github.io/OpenPBR/

14. **MaterialX PBR Specification**  
    Why: vertical layering and separate normals.  
    Blender use: clear structure for reusable node groups.  
    https://materialx.org/assets/MaterialX.v1.38.PBRSpec.pdf

15. **AxF documentation and samples**  
    Why: industry architecture for complex measured materials.  
    Blender use: data-preservation checklist and future importer requirements.  
    https://www.xrite.com/categories/appearance/axf  
    https://www.xrite.com/service-support/axf-sample-files

16. **Blender Principled BSDF manual**  
    Why: actual project implementation surface.  
    Blender use: coat, IOR, normals and current socket semantics.  
    https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/latest/render/shader_nodes/shader/principled.html

17. **Blender OSL manual**  
    Why: defines the experimental custom-shader route and limitations.  
    Blender use: Cycles stochastic-flake experiment.  
    https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/latest/render/shader_nodes/osl.html

## Essential practical implementation comparisons

18. **V-Ray Stochastic Flakes**  
    Why: mature aggregate flake model.  
    Blender use: expected controls and filtering behavior.  
    https://docs.chaos.com/display/VMAX/Stochastic+Flakes

19. **Unreal Automotive Materials Pack**  
    Why: production quality tiers, flakes, orange peel and chameleon finishes.  
    Blender use: material-instance and performance-branch design.  
    https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/en-us/unreal-engine/automotive-materials-pack-in-unreal-engine

20. **CC0 Automotive Paint Shader for Blender**  
    Why: transparent Blender graph to dissect.  
    Blender use: free benchmark against the research architecture.  
    https://blendswap.com/blend/31555

---

# V5 changelog

- Updated the standards section to current 2025–2026 revisions.
- Added CIE JTC 12 status and explicit non-compliance warning for internal metrics.
- Added spectral gonio-imaging and psychophysical sparkle implications.
- Added AxF SVBRDF and CPA2 architecture, version changes and Blender-import risks.
- Added commercial pigment D10/D50/D90 examples and physically named flake presets.
- Added cross-renderer specialist flake implementations.
- Added MaterialX flake-proposal mapping to Blender subgroups.
- Added EEVEE, Cycles, OSL and explicit-geometry implementation branches.
- Added node-level coordinate, normal, roughness, LOD and AOV guidance.
- Added internal quantitative acceptance metrics.
- Expanded addon and commercial material-pack audit.
- Replaced the earlier “research complete” language with a precise stop condition and remaining proprietary/experimental gaps.

# V6 final public-source gap audit

## Purpose

This is the final broad literature and workflow audit before adopting the document as a persistent Project Source.

V6 adds categories that were underrepresented in V5:

- current 2025–2026 optical-appearance findings;
- interaction between clearcoat gloss and the visibility of buried effect pigments;
- flake bulk orientation, planar distribution and local angular reflectance;
- tri-coat, tinted-clear and candy-style layer stacks;
- body-panel versus flexible-trim paint differences;
- metamerism and illuminant dependence;
- polarization-assisted reference capture;
- aging, weathering, scratches, mar and clearcoat failure;
- refinish and manufacturing defects;
- surface-quality and lookdev addons beyond material presets;
- stronger Blender implementation consequences.

The review prioritizes publicly accessible primary papers, official standards, official Blender/OpenPBR documentation, OEM/paint-company technical documents and transparent addon documentation.

It does **not** claim literal coverage of every page on the internet. Search completeness is bounded by paywalls, proprietary OEM databases, closed renderer code and non-indexed resources.

---

# A. Verified current standards baseline

The following versions were verified during the V6 audit:

- **ASTM E2194-14(2025)** — multi-angle colour measurement of metal-flake pigmented materials;
- **ASTM E2539-25** — multi-angle colour measurement of interference-flake pigmented materials;
- **ASTM E2175-01(2026)** — geometry specification for multi-angle spectrophotometers;
- **ASTM D523-25** — specular gloss;
- **ASTM E430-23** — high-gloss measurement;
- **ASTM D4039-09(2023)** — reflection haze;
- **ASTM D5767-18(2023)** — distinctness-of-image gloss;
- **ASTM D4086-18(2023)** — visual evaluation of metamerism;
- **ASTM D7187-25** — mechanistic scratch/mar behaviour by nanoscratching;
- **ASTM D5178-21(2026)** — mar resistance;
- **ASTM D6037-26** — dry abrasion mar resistance of high-gloss coatings.

## Blender consequence

The validation scene must treat the following as different measurements:

- multi-angle colour;
- gloss;
- reflection haze;
- DOI;
- sparkle;
- graininess;
- metamerism;
- scratch/mar appearance.

One render and one roughness value cannot validate all of them.

---

# B. New finding: colour, gloss, sparkle and graininess are coupled

## 2025 research result

A 2025 Color and Imaging Conference study used **462 solid and metallic samples** with systematic colour and gloss variation.

The study reports that:

- colour influences measured gloss, particularly for metallic coatings;
- clearcoat glossiness substantially influences measured sparkle and graininess;
- replacing a high-gloss clearcoat with a matte clearcoat reduced measured graininess by approximately **0.6 ± 0.3 units** and sparkle grade at 15° by approximately **3.3 ± 1.7 units** in the tested sample set.

This confirms that the top coat does not merely add an independent reflection over an unchanged flake field. It changes the visibility and contrast of the buried texture.

DOI: 10.2352/CIC.2025.33.1.43

## Physical interpretation

A rough or matte clearcoat spreads light entering and leaving the coating. This lowers the contrast and directional sharpness of the effect-pigment response observed through it.

## Blender correction

Earlier workflow descriptions can imply that:

- coat roughness controls only the top reflection;
- flake sparkle can be added independently beneath it.

That is not sufficient for matte or semi-gloss finishes.

## Practical Blender implementation

For a custom effect layer beneath Principled coat:

1. compute the buried flake/sparkle response;
2. calculate a **coat-transmission visibility factor**;
3. reduce and broaden discrete sparkle as coat roughness rises;
4. reduce the contrast of diffuse graininess;
5. preserve the independent top-coat reflection;
6. validate the attenuation against several gloss grades.

The factor should not be a random artistic multiplier. It should be a monotonic function of:

- coat roughness;
- viewing angle;
- paint mode;
- optional coat thickness/absorption approximation.

## Acceptance test

Create seven clearcoat-grade presets over one fixed metallic base.

As coat gloss decreases:

- the top highlight broadens;
- DOI decreases;
- buried sparkle intensity and event contrast decrease;
- graininess decreases or becomes softer;
- the base colour does not simply fade to grey;
- grazing reflection remains physically plausible.

## Production impact

`CP_EffectPigment` and `CP_Coat` cannot be treated as completely independent modules. They need a controlled coupling interface:

- `COAT_EFFECT_TRANSMISSION`;
- `COAT_SPARKLE_ATTENUATION`;
- `COAT_GRAIN_BLUR`.

---

# C. New finding: flake behaviour has at least three distinct spatial levels

Recent reflectance-transformation-imaging work separates:

1. **local angular reflection** of individual flakes;
2. **bulk angular orientation distribution**;
3. **bulk planar distribution** across the coating.

These produce different appearance attributes.

## C.1 Local angular reflectance

A 2025 Physica Scripta study captured multi-light image collections from seven automotive metallic coatings and extracted local angular-reflection behaviour for flakes.

It proposed an M-LARe index and found a meaningful relationship between average local reflectivity and sparkle grade at 15°.

### Blender implication

Not all flakes with identical orientation should have identical brightness.

Add a restrained distribution for:

- local reflector efficiency;
- morphology/roughness;
- area weight;
- pigment class.

Do not use one uniform flake intensity.

## C.2 Bulk angular distribution

A 2025 Optics Express study used dome-based RTI to estimate flake-orientation distributions and an orientation index.

Reported trends include:

- lightness flop and graininess increase as the orientation index decreases;
- sparkle close to 15° increases with better alignment;
- sparkle at more off-specular geometries can decrease with better alignment.

### Blender implication

Orientation spread redistributes sparkle angularly; it should not simply increase or decrease “sparkle amount.”

A tight alignment preset should:

- concentrate strong events near the gloss direction;
- reduce off-angle event activity;
- strengthen directional flop.

A broad distribution should:

- spread events over more directions;
- increase off-specular texture;
- soften the coherent near-gloss response.

## C.3 Bulk planar distribution and mottling

The planar distribution of flakes influences:

- mesoscopic mottling/clouding;
- local concentration patches;
- directional streaks from application;
- coarse visual non-uniformity.

This is different from microscopic graininess.

### Blender implication

Add a separate **mottle field**:

- scale: centimetres to decimetres, depending on reference;
- low contrast;
- stable panel/object coordinates;
- possibly flow-oriented;
- modifies effect-pigment density/orientation slightly;
- must not look like dirt.

### Required outputs

- `FLAKE_LOCAL_RESPONSE`;
- `FLAKE_ORIENTATION_INDEX`;
- `FLAKE_PLANAR_DENSITY`;
- `MOTTLE_FIELD`;
- `SPARKLE_EVENT`;
- `GRAIN_FIELD`.

---

# D. Reference acquisition upgraded with RTI principles

## Research method

Reflectance Transformation Imaging captures many images from one camera while moving the light across known directions. It reveals local angular response and microgeometry without requiring a full laboratory gonioreflectometer.

## Low-cost project adaptation

A practical capture rig can use:

- fixed camera and sample;
- dark room;
- one compact light moved over a calibrated hemisphere;
- marked light positions;
- locked RAW exposure and white balance;
- neutral reflectance reference;
- macro lens where available;
- scripted naming by light direction.

## Minimal capture set

- 24–48 compact-light directions;
- one broad softbox capture;
- one diffuse-dome approximation;
- one crossed-polarizer pair;
- one parallel-polarizer pair;
- several camera angles for face/flop.

## What can be extracted

- approximate flake activation maps;
- event intensity distribution;
- planar-density/mottling;
- qualitative orientation spread;
- light-direction dependence;
- rough separation of top reflection and body colour.

## Blender use

The RTI set is not directly loaded as a texture. It is used to fit:

- flake-field statistics;
- orientation spread;
- mottle scale;
- sparkle angular concentration;
- colour-travel anchors.

---

# E. Tri-coat, tinted-clear and candy systems

## OEM evidence

An OEM paint-process reference distinguishes:

- ordinary basecoat/clearcoat;
- tinted clearcoats, especially on some reds and selected blues/yellows;
- tri-coats with a pigmented groundcoat plus an effect-flake/mica layer;
- pearl effects on whites and neutrals;
- monocoat solid systems in some commercial applications.

Typical example film builds shown in that reference were:

- clearcoat: 45 µm;
- basecoat: 10–20 µm;
- primer: 25 µm;
- electrocoat: 20 µm;
- phosphate: 5 µm.

These are example OEM values, not universal specifications.

## E.1 Tinted clearcoat

### Physical behaviour

A tinted clear is an absorbing dielectric layer.

Light can:

1. reflect at the outer coat;
2. enter the tinted coat;
3. travel to the base;
4. reflect/scatter;
5. travel through the tinted coat again.

The base contribution therefore experiences angle/path-length-dependent absorption.

### Blender implementation

For physically meaningful tinted clear:

- coat reflection remains largely dielectric and less tinted than the substrate seen through it;
- use a controlled absorption approximation;
- avoid multiplying the entire shader by coat colour;
- grazing/longer paths may become darker or more saturated;
- validate neutral reflected strips separately from body colour.

### Node controls

- `Tinted Coat Enable`;
- `Coat Absorption Colour`;
- `Coat Absorption Density`;
- `Path Approximation Strength`;
- `Double-Pass Compensation`.

## E.2 Pearl tri-coat

### Layer order

1. opaque or semi-opaque groundcoat;
2. translucent effect midcoat containing mica/interference pigment;
3. clearcoat.

### Blender implementation

Use three logical layers:

- `CP_Groundcoat`;
- `CP_PearlMidcoat`;
- `CP_Coat`.

The midcoat should modulate colour and angular response without replacing the groundcoat.

### Critical control

Midcoat application count/thickness changes appearance. Therefore expose:

- `Midcoat Coverage`;
- `Midcoat Density`;
- `Pearl Travel`;
- `Groundcoat Visibility`.

## E.3 Candy-style finish

Candy finishes usually use a coloured transparent layer over a reflective or effect-bearing base.

### Blender implication

Candy depth cannot be reproduced reliably with one saturated Base Color.

Use:

- reflective/effect base;
- transparent absorbing colour layer;
- outer clearcoat if separate;
- path-length-dependent saturation;
- carefully limited absorption to avoid black grazing angles.

## E.4 Layer-order validation

Swap the ground/effect/tinted layers deliberately in a test copy.

If the appearance barely changes, the implementation is not representing vertical layering meaningfully.

---

# F. Body panels and plastic trim may not match perfectly

## OEM evidence

Body and trim coatings can differ in:

- substrate;
- primer system;
- cure temperature;
- clearcoat chemistry;
- hardness;
- flexibility;
- glass-transition temperature;
- supplier and application process.

The cited OEM reference gives an example of harder body paint cured around 140°C and more flexible trim paint cured around 80°C.

## CG significance

Real bumpers and flexible exterior trim may show subtle mismatch in:

- colour;
- flop;
- DOI;
- orange peel;
- gloss;
- aging;
- panel-to-panel reflection continuity.

## Blender workflow

Do not always instance one identical material across every painted object.

Create a shared master with per-object overrides:

- `Panel Colour Offset`;
- `Panel Flop Offset`;
- `Panel Coat Roughness Offset`;
- `Panel Orange Peel Offset`;
- `Panel Mottle Seed`;
- `Panel Age Offset`.

## Restraint

The mismatch should be subtle unless the reference clearly shows repair, ageing or poor production matching.

---

# G. Metamerism and illuminant dependence

## Definition

Metameric samples can match under one illuminant and differ under another because their spectral reflectance differs.

Automotive paints are especially vulnerable because they combine:

- several pigments;
- metallic/interference effects;
- angular variation;
- different body/trim application systems;
- weathering and repaint history.

## Blender consequence

An RGB shader matched under one HDRI can fail under another even if the first render is excellent.

## Validation illuminants

Test under at least:

- D65-like daylight;
- overcast diffuse daylight;
- warm tungsten/halogen;
- cool LED;
- mixed street lighting;
- compact white “sun” source.

## Colour-management discipline

Keep material fitting separate from display grading.

Record:

- illuminant colour;
- source spectrum approximation;
- white balance;
- exposure;
- view transform;
- output gamut.

## Honest limitation

Blender’s standard RGB shading cannot reproduce full spectral metamerism. The goal is robust multi-illuminant appearance, not exact spectral matching.

---

# H. Polarization-assisted reference capture

## Important correction

Cross-polarization is useful, but it is not a perfect “diffuse-only” button.

Modern polarization-separation research shows that diffuse/body reflection can also be partially polarized.

## Practical capture protocol

Capture at least:

- no polarizers;
- parallel polarization;
- crossed polarization;
- several polarizer rotations where practical.

## Use of captures

- crossed state: reduce dominant outer specular reflection and inspect body/effect colour;
- parallel state: emphasize interface/specular components;
- unpolarized state: preserve the real appearance;
- differences: guide separation, not declare exact layers.

## Blender implication

Do not fit Base Color from a crossed-polarized image and Coat from a parallel image without checking that the separated components recombine plausibly.

## Field workflow

A simple linear polarizer on the lens and polarizing gel on the light can provide useful comparisons, but exposure must be re-measured for each state.

---

# I. Weathering and aged automotive paint

## Research findings

Automotive clearcoat/basecoat weathering involves more than colour fading.

Important mechanisms include:

- photooxidation;
- hydrolysis;
- loss or redistribution of UV absorbers;
- consumption/transformation of hindered amine light stabilizers;
- surface erosion;
- gloss loss;
- exposed hard additive particles that scatter light;
- embrittlement;
- microcracking;
- interlayer adhesion loss;
- peeling/delamination.

Research notes that gloss loss and colour fade alone may provide poor warning of impending catastrophic coating failure.

## Blender layer ownership

### Early aging

- slight coat roughness increase;
- mild haze;
- subtle loss of DOI;
- water spotting/environmental etch;
- micro-scratch accumulation;
- small colour shift.

### Intermediate aging

- stronger haze;
- uneven gloss;
- top-surface erosion;
- chalking;
- local clearcoat yellowing;
- mottle changes;
- more obvious scratches.

### Severe failure

- clearcoat cracks;
- delamination;
- peeling;
- exposed basecoat/primer;
- corrosion where the complete stack is breached.

## Node architecture

Create an `CP_AgeState` controller that drives physically located effects.

Do not use one global “Age” value to multiply random dirt everywhere.

### Suggested outputs

- `UV_FADE`;
- `COAT_OXIDATION`;
- `COAT_HAZE`;
- `COAT_EMBRITTLEMENT`;
- `MICROCRACK_MASK`;
- `DELAMINATION_MASK`;
- `BASE_EXPOSURE`;
- `PRIMER_EXPOSURE`.

## Spatial logic

Aging may depend on:

- upward-facing surfaces;
- sun exposure;
- water retention;
- panel edges;
- heat;
- mechanical contact;
- repaint boundaries.

Use object position/normal and hand-authored masks rather than uniform noise.

---

# J. Scratches, mar and swirl marks

## Physical location

Most field scratch appearance is concentrated in the outer clearcoat region. One automotive study reported a locus of field failure within the top approximately 50 µm.

## Categories

### Elastic/plastic mar

Shallow deformation with broad light scattering.

### Micro-scratches

Fine grooves that become visible under compact lights.

### Swirl/webbing

Many curved/random micro-scratches caused by washing or polishing. They appear circular around a compact reflection because the light reveals scratches of favourable orientation.

### Deep scratch

Penetrates clearcoat and may reach basecoat, primer or substrate.

## Common CG failure

A radial swirl texture is painted directly into Base Color.

## Blender implementation

### Micro-scratch field

- lives in Coat Normal and coat roughness;
- contains directional grooves;
- high anisotropy in individual scratches;
- varied length and curvature;
- sparse enough to remain invisible under broad light.

### Swirl appearance

Do not encode a permanent circular white pattern.

The geometry field may be random/curved; the circular visual pattern emerges around a point-like reflection because only suitably oriented grooves light up.

### Deep damage

Use depth/penetration masks:

- clearcoat-only;
- clear + base;
- primer exposure;
- metal exposure.

## Validation

Use:

- compact point/area light;
- grazing strip;
- rotating light;
- broad softbox.

Micro-scratches should almost vanish under some lighting and flare under others.

---

# K. Refinish and manufacturing defect taxonomy

Paint-company troubleshooting manuals document repeatable defect classes useful for CG reference.

## K.1 Mottling/clouding

### Appearance

Dark/light patches, often in metallic finishes.

### Physical cause class

Uneven flake distribution/orientation, application overlap, reducer/flash/spray issues.

### Blender location

Effect-pigment density/orientation at centimetre-to-panel scale.

Not dirt and not clearcoat roughness.

## K.2 Solvent pop and pinholes

### Appearance

Tiny craters or holes in the finish caused by trapped solvent/gas.

### Blender location

Topography piercing or denting the coat, with varied depth and rim shape.

## K.3 Fisheyes/cratering

### Appearance

Round depressions where coating pulls away from contamination.

### Blender location

Clearcoat/basecoat coverage defect with a clean crater centre and raised/soft rim.

## K.4 Dust nibs and inclusions

### Appearance

Particles trapped in basecoat or clearcoat.

### Blender location

Declare whether the particle is:

- under clearcoat;
- inside clearcoat;
- sitting above clearcoat.

The reflection differs for each.

## K.5 Dieback

### Appearance

Loss of gloss or DOI after apparent cure.

### Blender location

Uneven coat roughness/haze, often broad and subtle.

## K.6 Sanding/polishing marks

### Appearance

Directional scratches, holograms or compound residue.

### Blender location

Top coat normal/roughness.

## K.7 Edge mapping/ringing

### Appearance

Repair edges telegraph through later coats.

### Blender location

Broad shallow height/roughness boundary following a repair mask.

## K.8 Delamination/peeling

### Appearance

Layer separation and lifted edges.

### Blender location

Requires geometry/displacement or decals for severe cases; shader masks alone may look flat.

## Production use

Build named defect assets rather than one generic grunge group:

- `CP_DEFECT_MOTTLE`;
- `CP_DEFECT_SOLVENT_POP`;
- `CP_DEFECT_FISHEYE`;
- `CP_DEFECT_DUST_NIB`;
- `CP_DEFECT_DIEBACK`;
- `CP_DEFECT_SWIRLS`;
- `CP_DEFECT_EDGE_MAP`;
- `CP_DEFECT_DELAMINATION`.

---

# L. Matte and satin finishes revisited

## Key physical insight

Matte appearance is caused by outer-surface and/or internal scattering structures. It does not simply scale specular intensity down.

## Effect-pigment interaction

The 2025 gloss/sparkle study shows that matte clearcoat can strongly suppress apparent sparkle and graininess.

## Blender implementation

For matte metallic paint:

- broaden coat reflection;
- reduce DOI;
- maintain plausible grazing return;
- attenuate and broaden buried sparkle;
- reduce grain contrast;
- preserve metallic lightness travel where reference supports it;
- avoid simply increasing base roughness while leaving sharp flakes untouched.

## Test

Compare gloss and matte clear over the exact same effect base. The matte sample should read as the same colour family but a materially different optical stack.

---

# M. Paint appearance under repair and ageing variation

## Realistic panel history states

A vehicle can contain:

- original factory body paint;
- factory flexible-trim paint;
- factory reworked/repainted panel;
- collision-refinished panel;
- spot repair/blend;
- replacement bumper;
- polished panel;
- ceramic-coated panel;
- aged original panel.

## Blender workflow

Assign a shared colour identity but vary:

- mottle seed;
- flake orientation;
- clearcoat gloss;
- orange peel;
- tint;
- age;
- swirl density;
- panel colour offset.

## Benefit

This produces subtle realism without randomising every panel arbitrarily.

---

# N. Appearance versus sensor transparency

Modern pigment products may be designed for radar transparency in bumper/radome zones.

## Scope decision

This is relevant to actual vehicle coating engineering but normally invisible in RGB renders.

Do not complicate the visual shader unless the project involves:

- radar/sensor visualisation;
- material engineering;
- transparent sensor covers;
- physically simulated radio-frequency behaviour.

Record it as an adjacent research category, not a car-paint beauty-shader requirement.

---

# O. Blender workflow changes from the final audit

## O.1 Revised master architecture

```text
CP_AutomotivePaint
├── CP_PanelVariation
├── CP_Groundcoat
├── CP_EffectMidcoat
│   ├── CP_MottleField
│   ├── CP_GrainField
│   ├── CP_FlakeField
│   ├── CP_FlakeResponse
│   └── CP_AngularTravel
├── CP_TintedCoatAbsorption
├── CP_Clearcoat
│   ├── CP_CoatTopography
│   ├── CP_HazeDOI
│   └── CP_EffectTransmission
├── CP_SurfaceCondition
├── CP_AgeState
├── CP_Defects
└── CP_Diagnostics
```

## O.2 New coupling interfaces

- clearcoat roughness → effect visibility;
- clearcoat absorption → substrate saturation/darkening;
- effect orientation → sparkle angular distribution;
- effect planar density → mottling;
- panel type → coat/topography offset;
- aging → coat integrity and layer exposure.

## O.3 Shader modes

- monocoat solid;
- basecoat/clearcoat solid;
- metallic;
- pearl;
- interference;
- tinted clear;
- tri-coat pearl;
- candy;
- matte/satin;
- aged;
- refinished/repaired.

## O.4 Quality tiers

### Tier 0 — Preview

- no discrete sparkle;
- homogeneous effect response;
- simplified orange peel;
- no defects.

### Tier 1 — Animation

- filtered flake texture;
- grain and mottle;
- coat-effect attenuation;
- multiband orange peel;
- selected defects;
- EEVEE-safe.

### Tier 2 — Hero

- higher-frequency flake field;
- improved angular response;
- Cycles samples/denoising tests;
- detailed surface condition.

### Tier 3 — Research

- OSL or explicit geometry;
- RTI/measurement fitting;
- raw AOV analysis;
- no production portability guarantee.

---

# P. Additional Blender addons and tools found in the final audit

## Surface Diagnostics

Official Blender extension aimed at technical-surface quality and curvature diagnostics, including automotive/product surfaces.

### Car-paint value

Paint cannot be evaluated on broken normals. This tool belongs before shader tuning:

- inspect curvature continuity;
- identify reflection breaks;
- separate geometry problems from material problems.

### Decision

High-value modeling/lookdev diagnostic candidate.

## LightPainter

A Blender lighting addon supporting interactive light painting and studio-light workflows; product information found for Blender 3.6–5.1 and Cycles.

### Car-paint value

- place reflection strips quickly;
- shape highlight paths;
- create hero presentation lighting;
- iterate reflection-card positions.

### Limitation

It is an artistic lighting tool, not a neutral metrology rig.

### Decision

Useful after the fixed validation rigs are built.

## MaterialHelper

Official extension focused on material-asset management and updating scene materials from Asset Browser categories.

### Car-paint value

Potentially useful for:

- maintaining one canonical master;
- updating many vehicle files;
- distributing corrected shader versions.

### Risk

Version updates must not silently alter old shots.

### Decision

Audit for library-management workflow after v1 material exists.

## PBR Lookdev

Official extension aimed at look development for PBR texture workflows.

### Car-paint value

Useful for conventional baked spatial channels and Substance workflows.

### Limitation

Car paint exceeds standard metal/rough PBR maps.

### Decision

Supporting tool, not optical core.

## Auto Paint Boundaries

Official extension that constrains texture-paint strokes to connected face regions.

### Car-paint value

Useful for:

- repair-panel masks;
- chips;
- localized ageing;
- panel-specific condition;
- preventing masks bleeding between body regions.

### Decision

Potentially useful for surface-condition authoring.

## Color Helper

Official extension for palette extraction and colour-node creation.

### Car-paint value

Useful for managing reference colour anchors and named palettes.

### Limitation

A sampled RGB colour is not a measured angle-dependent paint.

### Decision

Convenience only.

## Updated addon priority

1. Surface Diagnostics;
2. Node Wrangler;
3. Photographer;
4. Gaffer or native light-management workflow;
5. Ucupaint;
6. Texel Density Checker;
7. one baking tool;
8. DECALmachine;
9. LightPainter;
10. MaterialHelper.

Commercial car-paint preset packs remain benchmark candidates, not research authorities.

---

# Q. Final source categories and completeness assessment

## Covered strongly

- layered coating anatomy;
- solid, metallic, pearl, interference, tinted-clear and tri-coat systems;
- measured BRDF/BTF and AxF architecture;
- effect-pigment scale, morphology, orientation and planar distribution;
- sparkle, graininess and mottling;
- gloss, haze, DOI and orange peel;
- spectral/goniochromatic capture;
- standards and multi-angle geometry;
- clearcoat/basecoat weathering;
- scratches, mar, swirls and defects;
- Blender/OpenPBR implementation;
- EEVEE, Cycles, OSL and geometry alternatives;
- baking boundaries;
- lookdev and material-management tools;
- objective internal validation.

## Covered moderately

- full spectral rendering inside Blender;
- exact thin-film pigment stack inversion;
- proprietary AxF conversion;
- manufacturer-specific paint formulations;
- detailed volumetric multiple scattering;
- neural/differentiable inverse fitting.

## Not publicly solvable from literature alone

- exact OEM paint master data;
- proprietary commercial BRDF/BTF libraries;
- closed stochastic-flake shader source;
- exact paint formula for a chosen vehicle;
- exact EEVEE temporal result before rendering;
- final parameter values for the project’s art direction.

---

# R. Final stop condition

The master is now ready to become the Project Source.

Do not perform another broad search merely to increase page count.

Reopen research when one of these occurs:

- a test fails;
- a selected real paint needs matching;
- Blender/OpenPBR changes;
- CIE finalizes new sparkle/graininess standards;
- a dedicated Blender flake BSDF appears;
- an AxF importer becomes available;
- a commercial addon is being considered for purchase;
- a new category such as chrome, carbon, glass, tyre rubber or asphalt begins.

The next car-paint task should be implementation and measurement, because the major remaining questions are experimental.

---

# S. V6 additions to the priority reading list

1. **How Color Influences Gloss and Gloss Influences Sparkle and Graininess in Metallic Coatings**  
   Eric Kirchner, Color and Imaging Conference 2025.  
   DOI: 10.2352/CIC.2025.33.1.43  
   Importance: proves coat gloss changes measured buried texture.

2. **Characterizing Optical Behavior of Automotive Metal Flake-Pigmented Coating Systems Using Reflectance Transformation Imaging**  
   Fashandi et al., Physica Scripta 100, 2025.  
   DOI: 10.1088/1402-4896/ada4ed  
   Importance: local angular flake reflectivity and RTI capture.

3. **Characterizing the Bulk Angular Distribution of Metal Flakes in Pigmented Coating Systems Leveraging RTI**  
   Fashandi et al., Optics Express 33, 2025.  
   Importance: orientation index, angular redistribution of sparkle and flop.

4. **Visibility of Sparkle in Metallic Paints**  
   Kirchner et al., JOSA A 32, 2015.  
   DOI: 10.1364/JOSAA.32.000921  
   Importance: visibility depends on light size/luminance, distance, flake diameter and paint medium.

5. **Paint Materials and Processes from an Automotive OEM Perspective**  
   Mark Nichols / Ford / NIST presentation.  
   Importance: actual stack, body/trim differences, tinted clears and tri-coats.

6. **Weathering Performance of Clearcoat/Basecoat Automotive Paint Systems**  
   Gerlock, Kucherov and Nichols.  
   Importance: photooxidation, UVA/HALS, cracking and peeling beyond gloss loss.

7. **Scratch Durability of Automotive Clear Coatings**  
   Jardret et al.  
   Importance: scratch location and clearcoat damage mechanisms.

8. **Separating Partially-Polarized Diffuse and Specular Reflection Components Under Unpolarized Light Sources**  
   Kajiyama et al., WACV 2023.  
   Importance: corrects simplistic cross-polarization assumptions.

9. **Axalta / PPG / Sherwin-Williams automotive paint-defect guides**  
   Importance: practical defect taxonomy and layer ownership.

10. **ASTM D4086-18(2023)**  
    Importance: metamerism is a real validation category rather than a colour-management afterthought.

---

# V6 changelog

- Verified the current ASTM version baseline.
- Added 2025 research coupling clearcoat gloss with sparkle and graininess.
- Added RTI-based local-reflectivity, orientation and planar-distribution findings.
- Added a low-cost RTI-inspired reference-capture workflow.
- Added tinted-clear, pearl tri-coat and candy layer models.
- Added body-versus-trim material variation.
- Added metamerism and multi-illuminant validation.
- Added a corrected polarization capture protocol.
- Added weathering, photooxidation, UVA/HALS, cracking and peeling.
- Added scratch, mar and swirl-mark layer mapping.
- Added a structured manufacturing/refinish defect library.
- Added matte-clearcoat attenuation of effect pigments.
- Added panel-history/repaint realism.
- Added Surface Diagnostics, LightPainter, MaterialHelper, PBR Lookdev, Auto Paint Boundaries and Color Helper to the tool audit.
- Revised the master node architecture and quality tiers.
- Declared the final broad-search stop condition.

# V7 adjustment — Blender node knowledge and inventive R&D

V7 changes the compendium from a literature-driven design reference into a node-aware invention source.

## Core addition

A complete `Blender_Node_Behaviour_Atlas_v7.md` has been incorporated below. It records:

- official node functionality;
- expected visual signatures;
- coordinate and scale behaviour;
- common visual failure modes;
- Cycles/EEVEE limitations;
- automotive-paint uses;
- unconventional uses;
- proposed node inventions;
- falsification tests.

## Critical methodological change

Future work must not ask only:

> Which node normally creates this effect?

It must also ask:

> Which measurable signal do we need, and which combination of Blender data, vectors, procedural fields, attributes, drivers, shader closures and bakes can construct it?

## Highest-priority inventions

1. projected-pixel flake LOD;
2. tangent-biased stochastic flake normals;
3. area-preserving flake-size controls;
4. coat-to-effect attenuation;
5. lobe-specific ambient occlusion;
6. Gabor-driven scratches and orange peel;
7. separate mottle, grain and sparkle fields;
8. Geometry-Nodes-assisted shader data;
9. reflection-safe performance branching;
10. OSL and explicit-geometry research variants.

## R&D record

The accompanying `Car_Paint_Node_Experiment_Backlog_v7.csv` is the initial hypothesis register. Results should be appended rather than overwritten.

# Blender Node Behaviour Atlas for Automotive Paint R&D

**Version:** 7.0  
**Project baseline:** Blender 5.0.1  
**Forward reference:** Blender 5.2 LTS  
**Purpose:** Convert Blender node knowledge into observable behaviour, failure diagnosis and original automotive-shader research.

## 1. Evidence labels

Every claim in this atlas uses one of four labels:

- **Documented** — stated by official Blender documentation or release notes.
- **Direct consequence** — follows mathematically from documented node behaviour.
- **Visual inference** — expected or repeatedly visible behaviour that must be confirmed in the project laboratory.
- **Hypothesis** — an original implementation route proposed for testing.

A visual inference is not treated as a measured result until it has passed the project test scene.

## 2. Observation method

Every node or subgroup should be observed through a standardized debug material rather than judged only inside the final paint.

### Scalar preview

Connect the value to Emission Color through a grayscale Combine Color or Color Ramp.

Record:

- minimum;
- maximum;
- mean;
- spatial frequency;
- continuity;
- visible tiling;
- camera dependence;
- object-scale dependence;
- temporal stability.

### Vector preview

Map components from `[-1, 1]` into `[0, 1]`:

```text
Preview RGB = Vector × 0.5 + 0.5
```

Also inspect:

```text
Deviation = 1 - dot(normalize(Vector), normalize(True Normal))
```

This reveals orientation deviation much more clearly than the raw rainbow vector preview.

### Height preview

Observe both:

- the raw grayscale height;
- the resulting Bump normal.

A visually attractive height field may produce unusable derivatives.

### Shader preview

Test with:

- large neutral softbox;
- compact source;
- long strip;
- diffuse environment;
- fixed exposure;
- flat and curved geometry.

### Required observation log

For each tested node graph:

- node graph version;
- coordinates;
- scene scale;
- engine;
- samples;
- resolution;
- camera distance;
- light size;
- visual signature;
- failure signature;
- performance;
- whether the output is spatial, angular, ray-dependent or camera-dependent.

---

# 3. Coordinate and data-input nodes

## 3.1 Texture Coordinate

### Documented role

Outputs coordinate systems for procedural and image textures.

### UV

**Observed visual character**

- follows authored surface coordinates;
- stable through object transforms;
- can preserve deliberate physical scale when texel density is managed;
- seams and mirrored islands become visible in directional or non-periodic effects.

**Automotive uses**

- baked flake orientation maps;
- panel-specific mottle;
- scratches and repair masks;
- orange-peel source images;
- consistent surface condition.

**Failure signature**

- door flakes visibly larger than bonnet flakes;
- scratch direction flips across mirrored islands;
- seams appear as changes in grain or DOI.

**Rule**

Image-based effect fields require a texel-density audit.

### Generated

**Direct consequence**

Generated coordinates are normalized around object bounds.

**Observed visual character**

- instantly fills each object;
- separate parts receive separate normalization;
- object proportions stretch the pattern;
- changing the bounding box can rescale the material.

**Automotive use**

- prototypes;
- object-local diagnostics;
- disposable coupon materials.

**Do not use as final physical coordinates when**

- multiple body panels must share one scale;
- a mirror, spoiler and body shell have different bounds;
- object topology or bounds may change.

### Object

**Observed visual character**

- allows a shared Empty or reference object to control the texture field;
- can make patterns continuous across separate panels;
- transforms of the coordinate object move/rotate/scale the field;
- unapplied or animated transforms can create unexpected sliding.

**Automotive uses**

- common body-space mottle;
- application-flow direction;
- common orange-peel scale;
- debugging world alignment;
- synchronizing patterns across panel objects.

**Inventive use**

A dedicated `CP_COORDINATE_RIG` Empty can define the paint application space independently of every body object. This makes one material portable while preserving a common physical field.

### Normal

**Observed visual character**

- direction-coloured rather than position-textured;
- broad gradients follow surface orientation;
- changes with modified shading normals.

**Automotive uses**

- diagnostics;
- directional masks;
- identifying normal discontinuities;
- rough panel-orientation effects.

**Warning**

It is not a stable surface pattern. Using it as flakes or mottle paints the object according to orientation rather than coating position.

### Reflection

**Observed visual character**

- strongly view-dependent;
- patterns appear to slide across the object as the camera moves;
- resembles environment reflection mapping.

**Automotive uses**

- diagnostics;
- deliberately view-locked cheats;
- LUT experiments.

**Warning**

Never use as a supposed physical flake-location field.

### Camera / Window

**Observed visual character**

- tied to camera or screen;
- useful for screen-space masks and debugging;
- the texture does not remain attached to the coating.

**Automotive uses**

- experimental screen-space LOD;
- shot-specific sparkle suppression;
- debug overlays.

**Risk**

Camera-space effects can pass a still-image review and fail instantly in motion.

---

## 3.2 UV Map

### Function

Selects a named UV map explicitly.

### Practical value

Use it instead of relying on whichever UV map is active.

Recommended named UV sets:

- `UV_PAINT_EFFECT`;
- `UV_SURFACE_CONDITION`;
- `UV_DECALS`;
- `UV_BAKE_DIAGNOSTIC`.

This prevents decal or lightmap UV changes from silently damaging the paint.

---

## 3.3 Object Info

### Documented role

Provides per-object or per-instance information and a stable Random output.

### Observed visual character

- Random remains uniform across one object/instance;
- it is not per-pixel noise;
- duplication/instancing can create different values;
- using it directly on colour produces panel-wide variation.

### Automotive uses

- panel seed;
- slight body/trim mismatch;
- variation among duplicated test coupons;
- mottle offset;
- repair-state variation;
- age offset.

### Failure signature

Excessive Random mapping creates a “different paint per body panel” look.

### Rule

Randomize secondary attributes by a narrow range. Do not randomize the main colour identity without reference.

---

## 3.4 Attribute and Color Attribute

### Function

Read named data stored on geometry.

### Why these nodes are critical

Shader nodes do not expose every useful geometric measurement. Geometry Nodes, modifiers or scripts can calculate data and store it for the shader.

### Candidate stored attributes

- panel ID;
- panel physical scale;
- curvature;
- principal-flow direction;
- tangent/bitangent proxy;
- upward exposure;
- cavity factor;
- self-occlusion bake;
- repair mask;
- age mask;
- flake LOD override;
- selected hero-macro region;
- explicit microflake density;
- panel material-history state.

### Inventive principle

When a shader node cannot know something, do not immediately conclude Blender cannot do it. Ask whether Geometry Nodes or Python can calculate it once and store it as an attribute.

---

## 3.5 Geometry

### Important outputs

- Position;
- Normal;
- True Normal;
- Incoming;
- Backfacing;
- Parametric;
- Pointiness where supported;
- Random Per Island where supported.

### Normal versus True Normal

**Observed use**

Comparing these exposes how smoothing, bump and normal processing alter the geometric reference.

**Diagnostic**

```text
Normal Error = 1 - dot(Normal, True Normal)
```

Large smooth bands may be intentional shading. Sharp discontinuities often reveal broken normals or inappropriate smoothing.

### Incoming

**Direct consequence**

Incoming is the direction toward the shading point from the current ray/view.

**Automotive uses**

- explicit view-angle calculations;
- reflect-vector experiments;
- custom facing terms;
- diagnostic incidence maps.

**Warning**

A view-direction calculation is not a light-direction calculation. It cannot produce physically correct flop by itself.

### Backfacing

Useful for:

- preventing internal/back-face paint artefacts;
- double-sided decals;
- identifying inverted normals.

### Pointiness

**Documented limitation**

Cycles-oriented curvature-like output.

**Observed visual character**

- responds to mesh curvature and topology;
- can highlight convex edges and concave regions;
- changes with tessellation/subdivision;
- is not a metric curvature measurement;
- can create noisy or topology-shaped masks.

**Automotive uses**

- rough defect placement;
- edge wear prototypes;
- debugging;
- optional faster substitute for ray-traced AO in some Cycles masks.

**Do not use for**

- physically measured orange peel;
- panel curvature fitting;
- core paint optics.

---

## 3.6 Camera Data

### Important outputs

- View Vector;
- View Z Depth;
- View Distance.

### View Distance versus Z Depth

**Visual inference**

- View Distance is radial distance from camera to shading point;
- Z Depth behaves along camera depth;
- on wide frames and angled surfaces the two can differ visibly.

### Automotive uses

- flake LOD;
- macro/medium/far shader branching;
- depth diagnostics;
- distance-based defect suppression;
- shot-specific optimisation.

### Limitation

Distance alone does not know output resolution, focal length or flake pixel footprint.

### Inventive upgrade

Feed a driver-computed camera scale into the material:

```text
FocalPixels ≈ RenderWidth × LensMM / SensorWidthMM
ProjectedPixels ≈ PhysicalFeatureSize × FocalPixels / ViewZDepth
```

This creates a much better flake-visibility estimate than arbitrary camera distance.

---

## 3.7 Fresnel

### Documented role

Computes dielectric Fresnel reflection weight from IOR, surface normal and view angle.

### Observed visual character

- low-to-moderate weight near normal incidence;
- rises strongly toward grazing angles;
- changing IOR affects the entire curve;
- a perturbed normal alters the mask.

### Correct automotive uses

- custom dielectric-layer experiments;
- diagnostics;
- testing coat-normal influence;
- physically motivated mixing when Principled cannot be used.

### Incorrect use

A Fresnel ramp is not automatically:

- metallic flop;
- pearl travel;
- edge tint;
- paint depth;
- dirt accumulation.

### Visual failure signature

An aggressively coloured Fresnel produces a bright airbrushed rim around the car independent of lighting.

---

## 3.8 Layer Weight

### Fresnel output

Convenient 0–1 control but conceptually still a dielectric view-angle weight.

### Facing output

**Documented role**

Blends from facing to grazing according to viewing direction.

**Observed visual character**

- clean view-space gradient;
- useful for masks;
- follows camera, not light;
- produces smooth edge brightening/darkening.

### Automotive uses

- art-directed travel approximation;
- diagnostic angular mask;
- limited fixed-shot correction.

### Rule

Suffix any artistic use with `_ART_VIEW`, because it is not a full incident/view angular model.

---

## 3.9 Light Path

### Documented role

Reports the incoming ray type. Blender explicitly notes its usefulness for non-physical tricks.

### Cycles uses

- cheaper shader on secondary glossy rays;
- suppress expensive microdetail after a ray depth;
- diagnostics;
- isolate camera-visible versus reflection-visible effects.

### EEVEE differences

EEVEE does not have full path-traced ray semantics. Some outputs are approximated or unsupported; Ray Length acts as camera distance.

### Automotive risks

Camera-ray-only flakes can disappear in:

- mirrors;
- glass reflections;
- neighbouring body panels;
- reflection probes;
- indirect glossy paths.

### Recommended use

Use Light Path only for deliberate performance tiers, with reflection consistency tests.

### Inventive option

`CP_REFLECTION_SIMPLIFY`:

- full paint for camera rays;
- homogeneous effect response for high-depth glossy rays;
- preserve clearcoat and average energy;
- never remove the paint identity entirely.

---

## 3.10 Ambient Occlusion

### Documented role

Cycles node that ray-traces how much of the upper hemisphere is occluded. It supports Distance and an explicit Normal input. Blender warns that it is expensive and suggests Pointiness or baking when performance matters.

### Observed visual character

- dark/low values in cavities and close-contact regions;
- Distance changes the physical neighbourhood considered;
- large distances produce broad “dirty shading”;
- small distances isolate tight seams;
- using a modified normal changes the tested hemisphere.

### Correct automotive use

AO should generate a **visibility or condition mask**, not be multiplied blindly into Base Color.

### Proposed lobe-specific AO architecture

```text
AO_BASE_VISIBILITY
AO_EFFECT_VISIBILITY
AO_COAT_VISIBILITY
```

Possible first experiment:

- reduce base diffuse/scattering in tight cavities;
- reduce buried effect contribution moderately;
- leave direct clearcoat reflection less affected;
- add optional cavity dirt separately;
- preserve textured albedo.

### EEVEE path

Because the shader AO node is Cycles-specific, use:

- baked AO;
- Geometry Nodes self-occlusion proxy;
- vertex/colour attribute;
- authored cavity map.

### Important limitation

AO is not a replacement for actual shadowing or global illumination.

---

## 3.11 Bevel

### Documented role

Changes shading normals to mimic rounded edges without changing the silhouette.

### Observed visual character

- creates highlight roll-off on sharp geometry;
- cannot change the actual profile or cast the true rounded silhouette;
- can bleed or behave unexpectedly on thin/close geometry;
- adds render cost;
- inappropriate radius makes parts look inflated.

### Automotive uses

- badges;
- grille pieces;
- machined trim;
- tiny fasteners;
- proxy geometry during lookdev.

### Do not use as a universal fix for

- body-panel normal errors;
- panel gaps;
- exterior body curvature;
- missing geometric bevels visible in silhouette.

---

## 3.12 Tangent

### Documented role

Generates a tangent direction, originally associated with anisotropic shading.

### Automotive uses

- scratch direction;
- brushed trim;
- spray/application flow;
- directional mottle;
- orientation basis for procedural flakes.

### Building a complete local frame

Let:

```text
N = normalized panel normal
T = normalized tangent
B = normalize(cross(N, T))
```

`T` and `B` form the surface plane used to orient flakes and scratches.

---

# 4. Texture nodes

## 4.1 Noise Texture

### Documented role

Fractal Perlin-style noise. Musgrave functionality was integrated into Noise in Blender 4.1.

### Observed visual character

- continuous;
- soft organic blobs at low detail;
- increasing detail adds nested octaves;
- high roughness keeps more high-frequency contrast;
- distortion bends the field and can create fluid/marbled shapes;
- 4D W changes the sampled field.

### Good automotive uses

- broad mottling;
- subtle coat variation;
- orange-peel band modulation;
- grain envelope;
- uneven aging;
- application clouding;
- breaking perfect repetition.

### Poor automotive uses

- isolated mirror-like flakes;
- exact scratches;
- hard stable IDs;
- direct sparkle events.

### Visual traps

- Detail pushed too high creates “procedural oatmeal”;
- Distortion makes industrial coatings look biological;
- animating W creates boiling;
- using one Noise for every channel makes all defects visibly correlated.

### Rule

Use independent seeds/coordinates for physically independent mechanisms.

---

## 4.2 Gabor Texture

### Documented role

Produces Gabor noise: random interleaved bands whose direction and width are controllable.

### Important controls

- Scale;
- Frequency;
- Anisotropy;
- Orientation;
- 2D or 3D mode.

### Important outputs

- Value: phase multiplied by random intensity;
- Phase: structured bands without random intensity;
- Intensity: envelope/singularity information.

### Documented visual observations

- higher Frequency increases bands perpendicular to orientation;
- excessively high frequency can lose contrast and interleaving;
- reducing Anisotropy randomizes direction;
- Phase avoids random low-intensity areas;
- Intensity can identify phase singularities.

### Automotive opportunities

This is one of the most underused nodes for car materials.

Use it for:

- directional micro-scratches;
- polishing marks;
- spray/application streaks;
- flow-oriented orange peel;
- sanding marks;
- elongated mottle;
- brushed metal trim;
- directional waviness.

### Original proposal: Gabor orange peel

- low-frequency Noise determines local orientation;
- Gabor Phase generates flow structure;
- Gabor Intensity masks singularities;
- two or three scales are mixed into coat height;
- a final low-frequency Noise breaks excessive banding.

### Original proposal: emergent swirl marks

Use several sparse Gabor scratch fields with slowly varying orientations rather than painting circular white rings. Under a compact moving light, orientation-selective highlights should form apparent webs around the reflection naturally.

---

## 4.3 Voronoi Texture

### Documented role

Worley/Voronoi cellular texture with distance, edge and cell-related outputs depending on mode.

### Observed visual character

- stable cells;
- hard identity regions;
- clear boundaries;
- Distance to Edge creates cell-border networks;
- scale exposes obvious cellular structure when too large;
- high-frequency use can alias;
- the 5.0 implementation uses a faster hashing algorithm, so migrated procedural assets should be visually rechecked.

### Good automotive uses

- stable flake-cell IDs;
- sparse defect placement;
- paint-chip seeds;
- crater/fisheye centers;
- water-spot regions;
- cellular clearcoat defect prototypes.

### Poor direct use

Raw Voronoi cells used as flakes look like reptile scales or hammered metal.

### Original proposal: cell-stable flake field

1. use Voronoi to partition the surface;
2. use cell Color/Position as stable seed data;
3. use White Noise on that seed to derive:
   - presence;
   - size;
   - azimuth;
   - tilt;
   - local intensity;
4. create a soft in-cell footprint;
5. suppress cell borders;
6. crossfade into homogeneous response at distance.

This uses Voronoi as an indexing structure, not as the visible flake shape.

---

## 4.4 White Noise Texture

### Documented role

Returns a pseudorandom value from a scalar/vector seed.

### Observed visual character

- discontinuous;
- tiny coordinate changes can produce unrelated values;
- excellent for IDs and random parameters;
- terrible as a smooth height field;
- when evaluated directly per shading point, high-frequency use can produce harsh temporal noise.

### Good automotive uses

- derive several stable random values from one cell ID;
- per-flake orientation;
- per-panel seed;
- local reflector efficiency;
- size-distribution sampling.

### Poor uses

- direct Bump Height;
- smooth roughness;
- unfiltered colour speckle.

### Rule

White Noise should randomize **parameters of a stable structure**, not create naked subpixel glitter.

---

## 4.5 Wave Texture

### Observed visual character

- regular bands or rings;
- easy to art-direct;
- distortion can break regularity;
- obvious periodicity appears quickly.

### Automotive uses

- controlled test gratings;
- polishing/sanding-direction studies;
- stripe-card procedural reflection tests;
- synthetic wavelength bands for orange-peel validation;
- regular machining marks on trim.

### Research use

Wave is excellent for calibration because its frequency is known and predictable. It is less useful as final natural randomness.

---

## 4.6 Gradient Texture

### Automotive uses

- panel-direction masks;
- repair-blend falloff;
- top-to-bottom weathering;
- deliberate groundcoat gradient;
- orientation visualization.

### Warning

Gradients tied to Generated/Object coordinates can change when bounds or transforms change.

---

## 4.7 Image Texture

### Strengths

- filtering and mipmapping;
- stable authored structure;
- UDIM support;
- predictable bakes;
- external measurement data;
- channel packing.

### Critical controls

- colour space;
- interpolation;
- extension;
- projection;
- alpha interpretation.

### Automotive rules

Use Non-Color data for:

- roughness;
- height;
- normals;
- masks;
- flake orientation;
- grain;
- mottle.

Use physically documented scale and UV density.

### Original proposal

A four-channel orientation texture:

- R/G: encoded tangent-plane orientation;
- B: flake coverage/size;
- A: local reflector efficiency.

This can outperform an enormous live procedural graph while remaining stable and mipmapped.

---

# 5. Vector and normal nodes

## 5.1 Mapping

### Documented role

Transforms coordinates by translation, rotation and scale. Different vector types can apply transformations differently.

### Observed visual character

- scaling coordinates increases apparent texture frequency;
- object-scale mistakes can be hidden temporarily;
- rotation is essential for directional scratches and Gabor fields;
- chained Mapping nodes become difficult to audit.

### Rule

Use one named coordinate subgroup per physical field:

- `CP_COORD_EFFECT`;
- `CP_COORD_COAT`;
- `CP_COORD_DAMAGE`.

Expose physical wavelength rather than raw Mapping scale where possible.

---

## 5.2 Vector Math

### Essential operations

#### Normalize

Required before treating vectors as directions.

Failure to normalize can make strength vary with magnitude.

#### Dot Product

Produces angular similarity.

Automotive uses:

- view-facing diagnostic;
- normal-deviation diagnostic;
- directional masks;
- comparing flake normal with panel normal;
- orientation-index proxy.

#### Cross Product

Builds a bitangent from normal and tangent.

#### Scale

Changes vector magnitude without component-wise ambiguity.

#### Add/Subtract

Build orientation offsets and compare vectors.

#### Distance

Useful for coordinate/seed proximity, not a substitute for geodesic surface distance.

#### Project

Can isolate one vector along another.

#### Reflect/Refract

Useful for custom diagnostics and environment-vector experiments.

### Original flake-orientation construction

```text
B = normalize(cross(N, T))
Phi = Random1 × 2π
Tilt = Distribution(Random2, OrientationSpread)
PlaneDirection = cos(Phi) × T + sin(Phi) × B
FlakeNormal = normalize(N + tan(Tilt) × PlaneDirection)
```

The exact probability distribution should be tested. Even a controlled approximation is better than unconstrained XYZ noise.

---

## 5.3 Vector Rotate

### Automotive uses

- rotate tangent-space scratch directions;
- orient Gabor fields;
- vary flake azimuth;
- rotate coordinate frames without modifying object transforms.

### Visual trap

Mixing degrees/radians conceptually or rotating in the wrong space causes mirrored or camera-dependent patterns.

---

## 5.4 Vector Transform

### Importance

Moves vectors between spaces.

### Automotive uses

- make attributes authored in object/world/tangent spaces compatible;
- compare geometric and shading vectors;
- preserve common paint-flow direction across differently transformed panels.

### Rule

Record source and target space in node labels. Unlabelled vector-space conversion is a future failure.

---

## 5.5 Bump

### Documented role

Samples height at the shading point and nearby points to estimate a perturbed normal.

### Direct visual consequences

- continuous height fields produce coherent normals;
- discontinuities create abrupt derivative changes;
- very high frequency can alias or lose contrast;
- Distance/Strength change the apparent slope, not literal geometry;
- silhouette and true self-shadow remain unchanged.

### Automotive uses

- coat orange peel;
- scratches;
- subtle topography;
- tiny dust undercoat;
- fine defects.

### Rules

- combine related height bands before one final Bump where practical;
- work at real scale;
- avoid White Noise directly into Height;
- preview the resulting normal, not only the height;
- connect orange peel to Coat Normal, not global Normal.

---

## 5.6 Normal Map

### Documented role

Converts RGB normal-map data into a perturbed normal.

### Practical rules

- image must use Non-Color data;
- select the correct normal space;
- tangent-space normal maps depend on UV/tangent quality;
- flipping the green channel changes handedness;
- mixing normal maps as ordinary RGB colours is generally wrong.

### Automotive uses

- baked scratches;
- baked orange peel;
- scanned surface detail;
- stable high-frequency topography.

### Diagnostic

Compare imported normal against True Normal and visualize deviation.

---

## 5.7 Vector Curves

### Automotive uses

- reshape orientation distributions;
- bias random vectors toward the panel normal;
- nonlinearly compress extreme tilt;
- art-direct colour/travel vectors.

### Warning

Curves can alter vector magnitude and direction unpredictably if used without normalization.

---

## 5.8 Displacement and Vector Displacement

### Use only when silhouette/parallax matters

Examples:

- severe delamination;
- deep craters;
- raised dust nibs;
- thick paint chips;
- explicit repair edge;
- extreme macro orange peel.

### Risks

- subdivision cost;
- scale sensitivity;
- self-intersection;
- mismatch between EEVEE and Cycles;
- excessive geometry for microscopic features.

---

# 6. Converter and math nodes

## 6.1 Map Range

### Automotive uses

- physical-value remapping;
- LOD transition;
- roughness grade conversion;
- angle-to-travel mapping;
- coat-to-effect attenuation.

### Preferred interpolation

Use smooth interpolation for transitions that must not pop.

### Failure signature

Hard clipping creates visible rings or bands as the camera moves.

---

## 6.2 Float Curve

### Automotive uses

- face/intermediate/flop response;
- orientation-distribution shaping;
- coat attenuation curve;
- nonlinear defect thresholds;
- artistic response fitted to measurements.

### Rule

Every important curve should have:

- named x-axis meaning;
- named y-axis meaning;
- saved reference screenshot/data;
- reset/default preset.

An unlabeled curve is impossible to audit later.

---

## 6.3 Color Ramp

### Strength

Fast visual remapping and thresholding.

### Risks

- interpolation mode changes the material character;
- tight handles create alias-prone hard edges;
- using it for spectral pearl effects is only an RGB approximation;
- repeated ramps make graphs impossible to calibrate numerically.

### Automotive uses

- mask shaping;
- debug false colour;
- defect classification;
- simple angular colour anchors.

---

## 6.4 Math Node operations

### Multiply

Use for modulation. Beware unintended energy loss when multiplying many masks.

### Add

Use for contributions. Clamp or normalize when sums represent bounded weights.

### Minimum/Maximum

Useful for intersection/union-like masks.

### Less Than/Greater Than

Hard thresholds; useful for IDs and debug, risky for animation.

### Compare

Stable category detection, but tolerance needs real scale.

### Power

Shapes distributions. Power greater than one suppresses mid-values; below one expands them.

### Exponential/Logarithm

Potentially useful for absorption and perceptual mappings, but require safe positive ranges.

### Sine/Cosine/Atan2

Critical for constructing azimuthal flake orientation and periodic test patterns.

### Fraction/Modulo

Useful for tiling and quantization; creates discontinuities.

### Smooth Minimum/Maximum

Useful for organic defect merging, usually inappropriate for the clean optical core.

### Ping-Pong

Useful for test patterns and repeated gradients, rarely physical by itself.

---

## 6.5 Mix

### Distinguish data types

Mixing scalars, vectors, colours and shaders are different operations.

### Automotive warning

A linear colour mix is not vertical optical layering.

Use Principled/OpenPBR for coat/substrate where possible.

---

## 6.6 Separate/Combine XYZ and Color

### Automotive uses

- encode/decode orientation maps;
- build tangent-plane vectors;
- channel-pack masks;
- inspect hidden correlations;
- convert cell seeds into several controls.

### Rule

Document channel semantics in the image and node names.

---

## 6.7 Clamp

### Uses

- prevent invalid roughness;
- bound masks;
- protect logarithm/power operations;
- control debug values.

### Risk

Clamping bright glints hides energy problems and changes the distribution.

Do not use Clamp as the first response to fireflies.

---

## 6.8 Shader to RGB

### Engine limitation

EEVEE-oriented conversion of shader response into colour data.

### Research potential

- stylized or art-directed sparkle extraction;
- lighting-aware masks in EEVEE;
- experimental thresholding of highlights.

### Major warning

This breaks portability and physical interpretation. Treat any use as an EEVEE-specific research branch.

---

# 7. Shader nodes

## 7.1 Principled BSDF

### Core ownership

Use for:

- base substrate;
- dielectric/metal balance;
- base roughness;
- coat;
- coat roughness;
- coat IOR;
- coat normal;
- thin-film experiments where supported.

### Visual observation discipline

Inspect each lobe separately before combining.

Recommended diagnostic materials:

- base only;
- coat only;
- effect only;
- base + coat;
- base + effect;
- complete stack.

### Rule

Do not compensate for an incorrect custom effect layer by corrupting Principled parameters.

---

## 7.2 Metallic BSDF

Where present in the Blender 5.0 node set, this offers a specialist conductor path for experiments.

### Potential use

- isolated effect-flake reflector;
- compare against Principled metallic response;
- research lobe separation.

### Warning

A metallic BSDF still does not create discrete flakes or buried-layer transport automatically.

---

## 7.3 Diffuse and Glossy components

Useful for:

- diagnostic isolation;
- simplified fitted lobes;
- research models;
- A/B comparison.

Avoid adding them blindly to Principled.

---

## 7.4 Mix Shader versus Add Shader

### Mix Shader

Interpolates between closures according to a factor.

### Add Shader

Adds closures and can increase total energy.

### Automotive rule

Use additive stacks only when the physical model and energy budget justify them.

---

## 7.5 Transparent and Volume Absorption

### Tinted-clear research

Volume Absorption or a controlled absorption approximation may help study candy/tinted layers.

### Risks

- path length and mesh thickness;
- shadow behaviour;
- EEVEE/Cycles differences;
- excessive darkening;
- double counting with Principled coat tint.

Use a controlled coupon before applying to a full car.

---

# 8. Output and debugging nodes

## 8.1 AOV Output

### Required AOVs

- `CP_TRUE_NORMAL`;
- `CP_SHADE_NORMAL`;
- `CP_NORMAL_DEVIATION`;
- `CP_COORD_SCALE`;
- `CP_PANEL_ID`;
- `CP_FLAKE_ID`;
- `CP_FLAKE_PRESENCE`;
- `CP_FLAKE_SIZE`;
- `CP_FLAKE_ORIENT`;
- `CP_FLAKE_LOCAL_RESPONSE`;
- `CP_GRAIN`;
- `CP_MOTTLE`;
- `CP_LOD_PIXELS`;
- `CP_DISCRETE_WEIGHT`;
- `CP_HOMOGENEOUS_WEIGHT`;
- `CP_COAT_HEIGHT`;
- `CP_COAT_NORMAL`;
- `CP_COAT_EFFECT_ATTEN`;
- `CP_AO_BASE`;
- `CP_AO_EFFECT`;
- `CP_DAMAGE_DEPTH`;
- `CP_AGE_STATE`.

### Principle

A material is not R&D-ready if its important internal signals cannot be inspected.

---

# 9. New automotive-shader inventions to test

## 9.1 Pixel-footprint flake LOD

### Problem

Camera distance does not directly determine whether a flake is visible.

### Hypothesis

Approximate projected diameter in pixels:

```text
ProjectedPixels = PhysicalFlakeDiameter × FocalPixels / ViewZDepth
```

Feed camera/render values through drivers or object custom properties.

### Node chain

```text
Camera Data View Z Depth
→ Divide physical size × focal-pixel constant by depth
→ Map Range / Smoothstep
→ Discrete flake weight
→ 1 - weight for homogeneous effect
```

### Predicted visual result

- consistent transition across lenses and render resolutions;
- fewer distance-based pops;
- stable full-car paint.

### Falsification

Change render width and lens. If visibility does not scale appropriately, the driver formula or node mapping is wrong.

---

## 9.2 Tangent-biased stochastic flake normals

### Problem

Random bump does not reproduce a plausible flake-orientation distribution.

### Hypothesis

Use Voronoi cell identity + White Noise random parameters + local tangent frame.

### Node logic

```text
N = panel normal
T = tangent
B = cross(N,T)
Phi = random azimuth
Tilt = shaped random × orientation spread
Direction = cos(Phi)T + sin(Phi)B
Nflake = normalize(N + tan(Tilt)Direction)
```

### Predicted visual result

- tight alignment concentrates response;
- broad spread redistributes events off-axis;
