# V10 final exhaustive audit

## CP10-FINAL-001 — What V9 still got wrong

V9 was comprehensive in quantity but not complete in model coverage. The final audit found several significant weaknesses.

### Mistake 1 — The basecoat was still treated too much like a surface colour

The earlier architecture correctly separated coat and effect pigments, but it did not adequately model the fact that ordinary pigmented layers have finite thickness, absorption, scattering, hiding power and substrate dependence.

This omission matters for:

- white and pastel paints;
- semitransparent colours;
- groundcoat influence;
- repair blends;
- thin coverage;
- candy and tinted layers;
- edge and film-build variation;
- black-versus-white substrate comparisons.

### Mistake 2 — Process history was described, but not promoted to a first-class shader input

Mottle, orientation and orange peel were largely treated as appearance fields. Their manufacturing causes must also be represented:

- spray-pass overlap;
- gun/bell direction;
- droplet distribution;
- wet-film thickness;
- panel orientation;
- gravity;
- solvent/water evaporation;
- film shrinkage;
- rheological leveling;
- clearcoat rewetting;
- flash time;
- repair blending.

Without process causality, procedural variation tends to become arbitrary noise.

### Mistake 3 — Orange peel lacked a full areal-surface statistics framework

Wave bands were included, but V9 did not sufficiently connect them to:

- power spectral density;
- autocorrelation length;
- anisotropy;
- ISO 25178-style areal parameters;
- wavelet/bandpass decomposition;
- measured height-map synthesis;
- scale-separated validation.

### Mistake 4 — Geometry quality was still subordinate to material research

Automotive reflections are a coupled geometry–material problem. A correct paint shader on a poor polygonal surface can be less credible than a simpler shader on a high-quality surface.

The final source therefore adds:

- Class-A-style reflection continuity;
- G0/G1/G2/G3 interpretation;
- zebra and reflection-line diagnostics;
- highlight-flow validation;
- panel-level curvature and normal analysis.

### Mistake 5 — The camera/display chain was not separated rigorously enough from the material

Observed sparkle can be altered by:

- lens point-spread function;
- diffraction;
- aberration;
- bloom/glare;
- sensor clipping;
- demosaicing;
- shutter integration;
- motion blur;
- tone mapping;
- colour transform;
- denoising;
- temporal accumulation;
- compression;
- display size and distance.

A visual artefact can therefore be a shader failure, a sampling failure, a camera model result or a delivery-pipeline result.

### Mistake 6 — The V9 unified file contained stale package metadata

The former RO-Crate/checksum blocks referred to multiple V9 files even after the user chose a one-file source. They remain preserved as a historical snapshot, but V10 explicitly supersedes them with one-file metadata.

### Mistake 7 — Some advanced Blender features were described before live verification

Closures, bundles, repeat zones, OSL GPU behaviour and exact group-interface APIs remain candidates until executed in Blender 5.0.1. V10 marks them as implementation hypotheses rather than established project capabilities.

### Mistake 8 — Commercial and community sources need stricter scoping

Marketplace pages and community observations are valuable for:

- discovering tools;
- identifying reported visual behaviour;
- locating workflow ideas.

They are not primary authority for universal physics. V10 retains them but makes source role explicit.

---

# CP10-BULK — Finite-thickness pigmented-layer transport

## Why this is a gold category

A car’s colour layer is not always an infinitely opaque surface. Light may enter the binder, encounter absorbing and scattering pigments, interact with effect particles, reach a groundcoat or substrate and emerge after a longer path.

This determines:

- hiding power;
- opacity;
- saturation;
- groundcoat dependence;
- film-build sensitivity;
- colour at panel edges;
- repair-blend behaviour;
- white/pastel softness;
- candy depth;
- tinted-layer depth.

## Kubelka–Munk and radiative-transfer role

Kubelka–Munk theory represents a scattering/absorbing layer using two counter-propagating diffuse fluxes and wavelength-dependent absorption and scattering coefficients.

It is useful for:

- pigment mixing;
- finite film thickness;
- black/white substrate contrast;
- hiding power;
- layered diffuse coatings;
- estimating when a layer is optically thick.

It is not a complete automotive-effect model because it discards detailed angular information. Metallic and interference flakes still require directional treatment.

A 2026 analysis frames multilayer Kubelka–Munk as a rank-limited projection of radiative transport: stacking more layers does not restore angular information already discarded by the two-flux model.

## Blender architecture

### CP_PigmentedLayerTransport

Inputs:

- ground/substrate colour;
- layer thickness;
- absorption colour or coefficients;
- scattering colour or coefficients;
- pigment concentration;
- opacity/hiding control;
- layer count;
- black/white substrate calibration;
- spectral-mode flag;
- artistic correction, clearly labelled.

Outputs:

- effective body colour;
- transmitted fraction;
- substrate visibility;
- layer optical thickness;
- diffuse/body response;
- diagnostic absorption and scattering terms.

### Production RGB approximation

For ordinary opaque automotive paints:

- retain Principled/OpenPBR as the production surface;
- use a fitted body-colour/opacity function;
- expose thickness only when it changes a real appearance consequence;
- avoid literal volume geometry across the full car unless needed.

### Research branch

For translucent, candy, tinted or under-covered samples:

- construct physical layered coupons;
- compare Surface + Volume Absorption/Scattering approaches in Cycles;
- compare against a fitted LUT or offline Kubelka–Munk calculation;
- test thickness ladders;
- avoid assuming a volume shader is automatically a faithful paint model.

## New tests

### EXP-066 — Black/white drawdown test

Render the same finite-thickness layer over:

- black;
- 18% grey;
- white;
- coloured groundcoats.

A genuinely opaque layer should converge toward substrate independence as thickness increases.

### EXP-067 — Thickness ladder

Test film-build proxies across at least eight thicknesses.

Record:

- mean colour;
- contrast ratio;
- saturation;
- substrate visibility;
- render cost.

### EXP-068 — K/S offline fit

Fit approximate absorption/scattering coefficients from controlled colour/thickness samples and compare a LUT-driven Blender material with direct RGB interpolation.

### EXP-069 — White and pastel body-scattering comparison

Determine whether the standard Principled diffuse/base response is sufficient or whether a shallow scattering approximation improves reference matching.

## Important limitation

BSSRDF/Subsurface Scattering is not automatically appropriate merely because paint contains scattering pigment. Automotive basecoats are extremely thin compared with common skin/wax subsurface examples. Any SSS use requires a scale and reference justification.

## Added sources

- Geometric Realism Without Angular Resolution: Structural Classification of Multilayer Kubelka–Munk Theory within Radiative Transport, 2026:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09139
- Diffusion of Light in Turbid Media and Kubelka–Munk Theory, 2023:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.04065
- Practical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting:  
  https://scrtwpns.com/mixbox.pdf
- Calibrated Vehicle Paint Signatures for Simulating Hyperspectral Imagery:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08228

---

# CP10-PROCESS — Manufacturing-process digital twin

## Principle

Appearance fields should be connected to plausible production causes.

## Important process variables

- atomizer type;
- droplet-size distribution;
- electrostatic attraction;
- spray/bell direction;
- shaping air;
- gun distance;
- traverse speed;
- pass overlap;
- wet-film thickness;
- flash time;
- solvent/water evaporation;
- viscosity evolution;
- surface tension;
- leveling;
- sag;
- panel angle;
- gravity;
- film shrinkage;
- basecoat rewetting by clearcoat;
- bake/cure profile;
- body versus trim cure process;
- repair-gun technique.

## Appearance consequences

| Process variable | Likely appearance channel |
|---|---|
| pass overlap | tiger striping, mottle, film build |
| spray direction | directional flake orientation, streaking |
| droplet distribution | local texture and coverage statistics |
| wet-film thickness | hiding, flow, orange peel, sag |
| evaporation/shrinkage | flake alignment and topography |
| viscosity/leveling | orange peel wavelength balance |
| panel orientation/gravity | sag, flow direction, vertical/horizontal differences |
| clearcoat rewetting | altered base orientation or mottle |
| trim cure chemistry | bumper/body colour and gloss mismatch |
| repair blend | feathered thickness, altered mottle and DOI |

## Blender data model

### CP_ProcessHistory

Store or generate:

- application direction vector;
- pass index;
- pass-overlap field;
- nominal wet-film build;
- local shrinkage factor;
- panel gravity projection;
- leveling time proxy;
- rewetting susceptibility;
- body/trim process class;
- repair-state ID.

### Geometry Nodes preprocessing

Geometry Nodes can generate and store:

- dot product of panel normal with gravity;
- distance from spray-pass paths;
- overlapping pass weights;
- edge distance;
- seam distance;
- panel class;
- local flow vector;
- repair blend zone.

### Shader consequences

The process fields may influence:

- mottle;
- flake orientation spread;
- effect coverage;
- body opacity;
- orange-peel amplitude and direction;
- coat sag;
- panel mismatch.

They must not directly become visible as obvious procedural stripes unless the target is a paint defect.

## New experiments

### EXP-070 — Spray-pass overlap simulator

Create several parallel pass fields and compare:

- perfect overlap;
- underlap;
- overlap;
- cross-coat passes.

Observe whether controlled mottle/tiger-striping emerges without painting generic noise.

### EXP-071 — Gravity-oriented leveling

Use panel-normal versus gravity to vary medium/long coat-wave amplitude.

### EXP-072 — Film-shrink orientation coupling

Test whether a shrinkage proxy can tighten flake alignment while reducing wet-film thickness.

### EXP-073 — Clearcoat rewetting defect

Introduce local broadening/reorientation of the base effect where a rewetting mask is strongest.

## Sources

- Evolution of the Automotive Body Coating Process—A Review, Coatings:  
  https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/6/2/24
- Effects of Automotive Paint Spray Technology on Transfer Efficiency—A Review:  
  https://doi.org/10.1177/0954407017753239
- Drying Paint: From Micro-scale Dynamics to Mechanical Instabilities:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.05644
- NIST aluminium-flake orientation study:  
  https://tsapps.nist.gov/publication/get_pdf.cfm?pub_id=860367

---

# CP10-METRO — Areal surface metrology and spectral decomposition

## Why RMS height is insufficient

Two clearcoat surfaces can share the same mean roughness and still differ visibly because their:

- wavelength distribution;
- peak shape;
- autocorrelation;
- anisotropy;
- slope distribution;
- feature density;
- skewness;
- kurtosis;

are different.

## Relevant areal parameters

The ISO 25178 family uses 3D areal texture concepts.

Useful parameters for CG translation include:

- `Sa`: mean absolute height;
- `Sq`: RMS height;
- `Ssk`: skewness;
- `Sku`: kurtosis;
- `Sal`: autocorrelation length;
- `Str`: texture aspect ratio / isotropy;
- `Std`: dominant texture direction;
- `Sdq`: RMS gradient;
- `Sdr`: developed area ratio;
- `Spd`: peak density;
- `Spc`: mean peak curvature.

These parameters do not directly equal Blender node inputs, but they provide a better target vocabulary.

## PSD and wavelet approach

A measured or photographed topography can be decomposed into:

- short wavelengths affecting gloss/haze;
- medium wavelengths affecting DOI;
- long wavelengths affecting orange peel/reflection flow.

Power spectral density can describe energy by spatial frequency. Wavelets or bandpass filters can retain localised defects and orientation.

## Blender mapping

| Surface statistic | Blender/R&D control |
|---|---|
| Sq/Sa | overall height amplitude |
| Sal | characteristic wavelength |
| Str | isotropy/anisotropy |
| Std | Gabor/flow orientation |
| Sdq | slope/normal variance |
| Spd | defect or peak density |
| Ssk | peak-versus-valley bias |
| Sku | rare extreme peaks/valleys |
| multiband PSD | short/medium/long coat fields |

## Proposed implementation

### CP_SurfaceSpectrum

Inputs:

- band wavelengths in millimetres;
- band energies;
- anisotropy per band;
- orientation per band;
- skew/kurtosis controls;
- seed;
- measured PSD or preset.

Outputs:

- combined height;
- slope variance;
- band AOVs;
- predicted DOI/haze control proxies.

### Measured-map workflow

1. acquire profilometry, photometric stereo or calibrated height map;
2. remove nominal panel form;
3. separate wavelength bands;
4. measure statistics;
5. synthesize a tileable or panel-space field;
6. compare reflection-line degradation;
7. preserve real physical scale.

## New experiments

### EXP-074 — Equal-RMS different-spectrum surfaces

Construct several coat surfaces with equal `Sq` but different wavelengths and verify that their reflected-strip appearance differs.

### EXP-075 — PSD-matched procedural coat

Fit multiband Noise/Gabor parameters to a measured height-map spectrum.

### EXP-076 — Mesh-density sensitivity

Compare Geometry Nodes blur/statistics on several tessellation densities.

### EXP-077 — Height statistics versus reflected-image metrics

Relate height/slope parameters to measured edge spread, DOI proxy and haze proxy.

## Sources

- ISO 25178 surface-texture framework and related areal-topography literature.
- Morphological Characterization of ABS and PC-ABS Surfaces for Automotive Industry:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.12533
- Texture Evaluation of Automotive Coatings:  
  https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6412/10/4/320

---

# CP10-GEOM — Class-A reflection and body-surface diagnostics

## Core correction

Paint quality cannot be evaluated independently of body-surface quality.

A broad strip reflected across a car body responds to:

- macro form;
- patch continuity;
- vertex-normal interpolation;
- topology;
- bevels;
- subdivision;
- clearcoat topography;
- camera/light geometry.

## Continuity vocabulary

- `G0`: surfaces meet in position;
- `G1`: tangent direction is continuous;
- `G2`: curvature is continuous;
- `G3`: curvature-change behaviour is continuous.

A polygon mesh does not become a Class-A NURBS surface by using smooth shading. The vocabulary is used here to diagnose reflection behaviour, not to claim manufacturing-grade CAD continuity.

## Reflection-line shader

A Blender diagnostic can use:

1. Geometry Normal or True Normal;
2. Incoming/View Vector;
3. Vector Math Reflect;
4. projection against a chosen stripe direction;
5. `Atan2`/periodic mapping where available;
6. Wave/Color Ramp to generate high-contrast lines;
7. AOV output for line phase and normal deviation.

Simpler practical alternatives:

- black studio with long white strips;
- zebra environment texture;
- high-frequency black/white reflection cards;
- Surface Diagnostics addon;
- curvature/normal AOVs.

## Geometry acceptance tests

- reflection lines do not kink unintentionally across panels;
- narrow strips do not reveal shading seams;
- modifier order does not change the reflection unexpectedly;
- Weighted Normal is compared against corrected topology;
- subdivision does not introduce panel swelling;
- panel edges retain intended hard/soft transitions;
- body and bumper reflections are evaluated separately.

## New experiments

### EXP-078 — Reflection-line procedural diagnostic

Build a reusable shader independent of the paint material.

### EXP-079 — Weighted Normal ablation

Compare:

- raw topology;
- corrected topology;
- Weighted Normal;
- subdivision;
- bevel + normal combinations.

### EXP-080 — Strip-light geometry/material separation

Render the same body using:

- perfect mirror;
- neutral coated dielectric;
- final paint.

This separates mesh failure from paint failure.

## Sources

- Highlight-line algorithm for real-time surface-quality assessment.
- Reflection-line surface-quality methods.
- Feature-line and differential-geometry literature.
- Surface-finish defect detection using structured-light reflection:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.11904

---

# CP10-SPECTRAL — Hyperspectral, polarimetric and fluorescent appearance

## Hyperspectral vehicle paint

Vehicle paint reflectance can vary beyond visible RGB and may be measured in visible-near-infrared bands. This matters for:

- scientific simulation;
- sensor work;
- metamerism;
- illuminant robustness;
- future spectral rendering;
- recovering pigment/body signatures.

## Hyperspectral-polarimetric BRDF

A 2025 dataset introduced real-world hyperspectral-polarimetric BRDF measurements across 414–950 nm and 68 spectral bands, with polarization and angular dependence.

This is not an automotive-specific database, but it expands the future appearance state from RGB BRDF to wavelength- and polarization-dependent scattering.

## Polarization

Polarization can help separate:

- interface reflection;
- body scattering;
- depolarization;
- anisotropic or layered behaviour.

But diffuse/body return can remain partly polarized. Cross-polarized capture is not perfect layer extraction.

## Fluorescent special finishes

Fluorescence shifts absorbed short-wavelength energy toward longer emitted wavelengths and violates the ordinary same-wavelength BRDF assumption.

A 2025 graphics model demonstrates editable fluorescence in a non-spectral renderer using reduced reradiation representations and optional UV basis functions.

## Blender scope

### Default paint

Do not complicate ordinary OEM paint with fluorescence or Mueller matrices.

### Advanced research modes

- `CP_SpectralReference`;
- `CP_PolarizationCapture`;
- `CP_FluorescentFinish`;
- `CP_HyperspectralSignature`.

### Non-spectral fluorescence approximation

A research-only Blender approximation may use:

- a UV/blue illumination proxy;
- an added wavelength-shifted response;
- strict energy control;
- clearly labelled non-physical RGB reduction;
- no arbitrary Emission disconnected from incident light.

## New experiments

### EXP-081 — Polarization capture decomposition

Capture parallel, crossed and unpolarized image sets and test whether body/coat estimates recombine correctly.

### EXP-082 — RGB metamer stress test

Create two RGB-matching body colours with different spectral assumptions in an external tool or dataset and compare under several illuminants.

### EXP-083 — Fluorescent reduced model

Prototype incident-light-dependent fluorescence in a controlled Cycles/EEVEE research scene.

### EXP-084 — Hyperspectral data down-conversion

Convert vehicle-paint spectra to several illuminant/camera RGB conditions and quantify divergence.

## Sources

- Hyperspectral Polarimetric BRDFs of Real-world Materials, 2025:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.13779
- A Fluorescent Material Model for Non-Spectral Editing & Rendering, 2025:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.19672
- Calibrated Vehicle Paint Signatures for Simulating Hyperspectral Imagery:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.08228
- Efficient Modeling of Depolarizing Mueller BRDFs:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.11976

---

# CP10-CAPTURE — Near-field lighting and inverse rendering

## Gold correction

The existing capture workflow often assumes the environment is distant. Real capture setups contain:

- softboxes close to the car;
- walls;
- floor;
- neighbouring objects;
- flags;
- camera rig;
- local emitters.

Their parallax and local reflection cannot always be represented by one infinite HDRI.

## Near-field inverse rendering

PBIR-NIE models non-distant illumination for glossy-object capture and jointly estimates geometry, material and nearby lighting.

This directly warns against fitting a car paint while treating every reflected source as infinitely distant.

## Practical Blender capture replica

Build a digital copy of:

- softbox dimensions;
- strip positions;
- floor/wall distances;
- black flags;
- camera position;
- lens;
- near-field bright objects.

Then fit material parameters in the replicated rig.

## Neural and frequency-aware material models

Recent neural BRDF work explores:

- frequency-aware representation;
- neural importance sampling;
- compact implicit materials;
- multi-view inverse rendering.

These methods are not direct Blender node replacements. Their strongest immediate use is:

- target fitting;
- surrogate evaluation;
- frequency analysis;
- parameter initialization;
- reference generation.

## New experiments

### EXP-085 — Distant HDRI versus explicit near-field rig

Fit one material using both assumptions and compare held-out camera positions.

### EXP-086 — Geometry/material ambiguity test

Perturb body geometry and paint roughness separately to measure whether one compensates for the other.

### EXP-087 — Frequency-aware fitting loss

Weight errors by spatial frequency so fine sparkle and broad flop are not averaged into one metric.

### EXP-088 — Held-out light and view validation

No inverse-fit candidate is accepted using only the images that produced it.

## Sources

- PBIR-NIE: Glossy Object Capture under Non-Distant Lighting:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.06878
- Inverse Rendering of Glossy Objects via Neural Plenoptic Function and Radiance Fields:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.16224
- FreNBRDF: A Frequency-Rectified Neural Material Representation:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.00476
- Neural BRDF Importance Sampling by Reparameterization:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.08998
- MaterialFusion:  
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.15273

---

# CP10-CAMERA — Material, camera, temporal and display separation

## Four different failure domains

### Material-domain failure

The outgoing radiance is wrong.

### Sampling-domain failure

The material may be correct, but insufficient samples create noise or unstable events.

### Camera-domain effect

Lens and exposure integrate or spread the signal.

### Display/delivery-domain effect

Tone mapping, compression and display conditions alter the final appearance.

## Camera variables that affect sparkle

- focal length;
- sensor size;
- aperture;
- focus;
- depth of field;
- shutter duration;
- motion blur;
- lens point-spread function;
- diffraction;
- chromatic aberration;
- glare/bloom;
- clipping and highlight roll-off;
- demosaicing and sharpening in photographic references.

## Temporal variables

- frame rate;
- shutter angle;
- camera velocity;
- object velocity;
- light velocity;
- TAA history;
- denoiser history;
- frame interpolation;
- playback size;
- display refresh.

## Practical rule

A sparkle event should be inspected in:

1. raw unclamped scene-linear EXR;
2. final view transform;
3. delivery compression;
4. animation at normal speed;
5. slowed playback;
6. no-denoise/no-TAA diagnostic.

## New experiments

### EXP-089 — Shutter integration sweep

Compare 0°, 90°, 180° and 360° equivalent shutter exposure where supported.

### EXP-090 — Lens PSF and bloom separation

Test whether sparkle character is coming from the material or post/lens spread.

### EXP-091 — Highlight-clipping audit

Render unclamped HDR values and several exposure/view-transform settings.

### EXP-092 — Delivery-resolution and viewing-distance test

Evaluate the same animation at mobile, desktop and full-resolution display conditions.

### EXP-093 — Temporal pipeline classification

Label every artefact as:

- event crawling;
- sample noise;
- denoiser smear;
- TAA ghosting;
- motion-blur streak;
- codec ringing;
- tone-map clipping.

---

# CP10-CONDITION — Post-factory optical layers

## Paint protection film

PPF adds another physical stack:

- air;
- film topcoat;
- polyurethane film;
- adhesive;
- original clearcoat;
- original paint.

Potential visible effects:

- added surface texture;
- altered DOI/haze;
- subtle tint;
- panel-edge line;
- trapped contamination;
- lift/bubble;
- seam;
- self-healing topcoat;
- satin or matte finish.

## Ceramic coating, sealant and wax

These may alter:

- contact angle and water beading;
- surface contamination;
- very small roughness/haze;
- reflection contrast;
- dust adhesion;
- apparent slickness.

A nanometre-scale treatment should not automatically become a thick visible refractive shell in CG.

## Vinyl wrap

A wrap is not paint. It has:

- film thickness;
- adhesive;
- printable pigment layer;
- different texture;
- edge/seam behaviour;
- possible trapped air;
- lower-quality reflected image;
- deformation/stretch history.

## Architecture

Add optional top-stack modes:

- `CP_FACTORY_CLEAR`;
- `CP_WAX_SEALANT`;
- `CP_CERAMIC_COATING`;
- `CP_PPF_GLOSS`;
- `CP_PPF_SATIN`;
- `CP_VINYL_WRAP`.

Each mode declares whether it is:

- a true geometric shell;
- a shader-layer approximation;
- a surface-condition adjustment;
- a decal/edge asset.

## New experiments

### EXP-094 — PPF reflection-quality comparison

Compare bare clearcoat, gloss PPF and satin PPF over one fixed paint.

### EXP-095 — Water-beading condition

Couple contact-angle-inspired droplet geometry to the selected protective layer.

### EXP-096 — Wrap stretch field

Use UV/Geometry Nodes strain proxy to modify film texture and print distortion.

---

# CP10-SPECIAL — Future and unusual automotive finishes

## Categories

- optically variable interference pigments;
- structural-colour pigments;
- fluorescent paints;
- phosphorescent accents;
- photochromic finishes;
- thermochromic finishes;
- electrochromic/body-panel display concepts;
- retroreflective particles;
- radar-transparent pigment systems;
- active illuminated paint concepts.

## Policy

These are retained because the corpus is exhaustive, but they are not enabled by default.

Every special mode must declare:

- activation stimulus;
- response time;
- wavelength dependence;
- reversibility;
- ageing;
- energy source;
- render-engine approximation;
- whether the effect is a material, light, animation or compositing problem.

---

# CP10-VALIDATE — Physical invariants and new metamorphic tests

## BRDF/BSDF principles

Ordinary passive reflection should respect:

- non-negative response;
- energy conservation;
- Helmholtz reciprocity where the model assumptions require it;
- consistent coordinate spaces;
- normalized direction vectors.

Layered, fluorescent or active materials may require broader scattering descriptions and may not fit an ordinary same-wavelength BRDF.

## New metamorphic relations

### MR-16 — Black/white hiding convergence

Increasing optical thickness must reduce substrate dependence.

### MR-17 — Reciprocity diagnostic

Swap controlled incident and outgoing geometry for a passive reciprocal branch and compare the response.

### MR-18 — Coat-stack order

Swapping vertically distinct layers should change the result.

### MR-19 — Process-cause directionality

Rotating the application-direction field should rotate directional mottle/scratch/flow response.

### MR-20 — Equal-RMS spectrum inequality

Equal RMS height with different PSD must not be assumed visually identical.

### MR-21 — Near-field parallax

Moving the camera around a close softbox should change the reflection differently from an infinite HDRI.

### MR-22 — Camera-pipeline separation

Raw scene-linear sparkle should remain constant while post/lens settings change its displayed footprint.

### MR-23 — Protective-layer inheritance

Adding PPF must preserve the underlying paint identity while altering the declared top-layer properties.

### MR-24 — Special-finish stimulus

A fluorescent/photochromic/thermochromic mode must not activate without its declared stimulus.

---

# CP10-AUTO — Single-file practical automation

The user requested one file. Therefore all operational templates remain embedded here.

These scripts are starting templates. They use stable Blender concepts but must be executed and verified in Blender 5.0.1 before being promoted to artifact grade AQ2 or above.

## Template 1 — Blender version guard

```python
import bpy

REQUIRED = (5, 0, 1)

def cp_assert_blender_version(required=REQUIRED) -> None:
    running = tuple(bpy.app.version[:3])
    if running != required:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Car-paint R&D requires Blender {required}; running {running}. "
            "Use a copied file for forward-version tests."
        )

cp_assert_blender_version()
```

## Template 2 — Node-tree inventory and link dump

```python
import bpy
import json
from pathlib import Path

def socket_record(socket):
    record = {
        "name": socket.name,
        "identifier": getattr(socket, "identifier", ""),
        "bl_idname": getattr(socket, "bl_idname", ""),
        "enabled": bool(getattr(socket, "enabled", True)),
        "linked": bool(socket.is_linked),
    }
    if hasattr(socket, "default_value"):
        value = socket.default_value
        try:
            record["default"] = list(value)
        except TypeError:
            try:
                record["default"] = float(value)
            except (TypeError, ValueError):
                record["default"] = repr(value)
    return record

def dump_node_tree(tree):
    if tree is None:
        raise ValueError("Node tree is missing.")

    nodes = []
    for node in tree.nodes:
        nodes.append({
            "name": node.name,
            "label": node.label,
            "bl_idname": node.bl_idname,
            "location": [float(node.location.x), float(node.location.y)],
            "mute": bool(node.mute),
            "inputs": [socket_record(s) for s in node.inputs],
            "outputs": [socket_record(s) for s in node.outputs],
        })

    links = []
    for link in tree.links:
        links.append({
            "from_node": link.from_node.name,
            "from_socket": link.from_socket.name,
            "to_node": link.to_node.name,
            "to_socket": link.to_socket.name,
            "valid": bool(getattr(link, "is_valid", True)),
        })

    return {
        "tree_name": tree.name,
        "tree_type": tree.bl_idname,
        "nodes": nodes,
        "links": links,
    }

material = bpy.context.object.active_material
if material is None or not material.use_nodes:
    raise RuntimeError("Active object has no node material.")

payload = {
    "blender_version": bpy.app.version_string,
    "material": material.name,
    "node_tree": dump_node_tree(material.node_tree),
}

output = Path(bpy.path.abspath("//CP_node_graph_dump.json"))
output.write_text(json.dumps(payload, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")
print(f"Wrote {output}")
```

## Template 3 — Required-node and socket audit

```python
import bpy

REQUIREMENTS = {
    "ShaderNodeBsdfPrincipled": {
        "inputs_any": {"Base Color", "Roughness", "Normal"},
    },
    "ShaderNodeBump": {
        "inputs_any": {"Height", "Normal"},
    },
    "ShaderNodeOutputAOV": {
        "inputs_any": {"Color", "Value"},
    },
}

def cp_audit_node_types(tree, requirements=REQUIREMENTS):
    report = []
    by_type = {}
    for node in tree.nodes:
        by_type.setdefault(node.bl_idname, []).append(node)

    for bl_idname, rules in requirements.items():
        nodes = by_type.get(bl_idname, [])
        if not nodes:
            report.append({
                "severity": "ERROR",
                "node_type": bl_idname,
                "message": "Required node type not found.",
            })
            continue

        available_inputs = {
            socket.name
            for node in nodes
            for socket in node.inputs
        }
        wanted = set(rules.get("inputs_any", set()))
        missing = wanted - available_inputs
        if missing:
            report.append({
                "severity": "WARNING",
                "node_type": bl_idname,
                "message": f"Expected input names not found: {sorted(missing)}",
                "available": sorted(available_inputs),
            })

    return report

material = bpy.context.object.active_material
if material is None or not material.use_nodes:
    raise RuntimeError("Active object has no node material.")

for item in cp_audit_node_types(material.node_tree):
    print(item)
```

The audit deliberately records available sockets rather than assuming every historical name remains valid.

## Template 4 — Scene/render manifest

```python
import bpy
import json
from pathlib import Path

scene = bpy.context.scene
camera = scene.camera

manifest = {
    "blender_version": bpy.app.version_string,
    "engine": scene.render.engine,
    "resolution": {
        "x": scene.render.resolution_x,
        "y": scene.render.resolution_y,
        "percentage": scene.render.resolution_percentage,
        "fps": scene.render.fps,
    },
    "frame": {
        "start": scene.frame_start,
        "end": scene.frame_end,
        "current": scene.frame_current,
    },
    "camera": None if camera is None else {
        "name": camera.name,
        "lens_mm": camera.data.lens,
        "sensor_width_mm": camera.data.sensor_width,
        "location": list(camera.location),
        "rotation_euler": list(camera.rotation_euler),
    },
    "view_settings": {
        "view_transform": scene.view_settings.view_transform,
        "look": scene.view_settings.look,
        "exposure": scene.view_settings.exposure,
        "gamma": scene.view_settings.gamma,
    },
    "world": scene.world.name if scene.world else None,
    "objects": len(scene.objects),
    "materials": sorted({slot.material.name for obj in scene.objects for slot in obj.material_slots if slot.material}),
}

path = Path(bpy.path.abspath("//CP_scene_manifest.json"))
path.write_text(json.dumps(manifest, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")
print(f"Wrote {path}")
```

## Template 5 — Parameter sweep strategy

A parameter-sweep script should:

1. duplicate or restore a known clean scene state;
2. set one defined parameter vector;
3. render named cameras and light collections;
4. save EXR, viewable image and manifest;
5. collect render time;
6. compute ROI metrics outside Blender where useful;
7. never overwrite prior evidence;
8. use deterministic experiment IDs.

Suggested filename:

```text
EXP-###__GROUP-v###__ENGINE__CAMERA__LIGHT__PARAMHASH__FRAME.ext
```

## Embedded operational tables

### Required evidence per experiment

| Evidence | Still-only effect | Temporal/LOD effect |
|---|---:|---:|
| node-tree dump | required | required |
| scene manifest | required | required |
| raw EXR | required | key frames |
| viewable PNG | required | optional key frames |
| AOVs | required | required where diagnostic |
| animation | optional | required |
| render-time log | required | required |
| written observation | required | required |
| contradiction update | if relevant | if relevant |

### Performance dimensions

Record:

- shader compilation time;
- first-frame render time;
- steady-state frame/render time;
- peak VRAM;
- texture memory;
- `.blend` size;
- node count;
- image texture count;
- samples;
- denoising;
- AOV overhead;
- Geometry Nodes evaluation time;
- OSL CPU/OptiX backend.

---

# CP10-PRACTICAL — Practicality improvements

## Build the laboratory before the hero shader

The first implementation asset should be a laboratory with:

- controlled flat/curved/creased coupons;
- complete light collections;
- reflection-line and true-normal diagnostics;
- black/white substrate cards;
- thickness ladder;
- process-field test panel;
- camera and resolution matrix;
- AOV compositor/output setup;
- automated manifest export.

## Create reference-grade macro ground truth

Use one small patch with:

- explicit geometry flakes;
- dense sampling;
- controlled compact light;
- no denoising;
- high-resolution output.

This is not the production method. It provides a local comparison target for cheaper approximations.

## Separate control surfaces from internal parameters

Artist controls:

- paint family;
- face/flop;
- flake character;
- sparkle grade;
- grain;
- mottle;
- coat grade;
- orange peel grade;
- condition;
- process variation.

Internal parameters:

- distribution moments;
- K/S coefficients;
- filter widths;
- PSD bands;
- angular LUT;
- pixel-footprint thresholds;
- attenuation coefficients.

Do not expose every mathematical parameter to the artist by default.

## Maintain shot-class presets

- turntable validation;
- full-car animation;
- medium hero;
- macro hero;
- EEVEE preview;
- Cycles reference;
- damaged/refinished;
- PPF/wrap;
- special finish.

## Keep one material identity across quality tiers

Quality tiers may change:

- filtering;
- discrete detail;
- sampling;
- texture resolution;
- ray-path complexity.

They should not silently change:

- body colour identity;
- face/flop family;
- coat character;
- angular travel;
- panel variation seed.

---

# CP10-CORRECTIONS — Point-in-time and factual caveats

1. Blender 5.0.1 remains the implementation target even though 5.2 LTS is a later reference build.
2. Exact closures, bundles and repeat-zone workflows must be verified live before production dependence.
3. OSL support and feature completeness differ by Cycles backend; all OSL claims require local CPU/OptiX tests.
4. The Bump node Filter Width is a useful stock filtering control, not a substitute for a full normal-distribution model.
5. Kubelka–Munk is a diffuse two-flux approximation and cannot replace directional flake BRDF/BTF behaviour.
6. A Subsurface Scattering node is not automatically a physically valid basecoat model.
7. Cross-polarization does not perfectly isolate “diffuse only.”
8. A camera-distance fade is not the same as projected pixel footprint.
9. Principled/OpenPBR is the baseline, not proof that every specialist effect is already solved.
10. Marketplace compatibility claims must be rechecked on installation day.
11. Visual similarity under one HDRI does not establish material equivalence.
12. Raw Emission speckles remain invalid as a general physical sparkle solution.
13. A normal modifier can improve shading while hiding unresolved topology problems.
14. Denoiser removal of sparkle is not evidence that the underlying material lacks sparkle.
15. A single unified Markdown file is convenient for project retrieval but less directly machine-operable than separate JSON/CSV; V10 preserves machine blocks and extraction templates inside the source.

---

# V10 final conclusion

The broad exhaustive-search phase is complete enough to stop responsibly.

The remaining high-value knowledge will come from:

- building;
- rendering;
- measuring;
- breaking;
- comparing;
- recording;
- inventing again.

V10 is not a declaration that all automotive-paint science is solved. It is the final unified foundation from which unanswered questions can be attacked without lowering standards, deleting history or pretending Blender’s current limitations are immutable.

# V11 final OEM paint-intelligence expansion

## CP11-OMISSION — The human observation V10 missed

Factory paint catalogues are not a decorative appendix to automotive-paint research.

They are a large, continuously changing set of real production decisions that connect:

- coating chemistry;
- pigment class;
- layer architecture;
- manufacturing process;
- body-surface design;
- brand identity;
- price and rarity;
- configurator rendering;
- repair systems;
- historical colour genealogy;
- customer personalization;
- Blender look-development targets.

The omission was substantial.

A literature-derived shader architecture should be confronted with the actual population of finishes manufacturers sell:

- solid;
- metallic;
- mica;
- pearl;
- tri-coat;
- tinted clear;
- matte/satin;
- colourstream/interference;
- two-tone;
- gradient;
- object-matched bespoke;
- hand-painted graphics;
- historical reissues;
- one-off experimental finishes.

OEM disclosures can tell us which phenomena matter commercially, which combinations recur, which processes manufacturers consider premium, and which effects deserve dedicated Blender modes.

---

# CP11-NO-SINGLE-HEX — Why a factory paint is not a hex code

## The categorical mistake

A hexadecimal RGB value describes one encoded display colour under an assumed colour space and transfer function.

An automotive paint may vary with:

- illumination spectrum;
- illumination direction;
- view direction;
- source size;
- body curvature;
- exposure;
- clearcoat;
- flake orientation;
- flake morphology;
- pigment concentration;
- film thickness;
- groundcoat;
- ageing;
- body-versus-trim process;
- camera and display transform.

Therefore a metallic, pearl, interference, matte or multilayer factory finish has no single physically complete hex.

## Acceptable display-proxy fields

Use explicit names:

- `ui_swatch_hex_srgb`;
- `configurator_chip_hex_srgb`;
- `marketing_image_sample_hex_srgb`;
- `approx_face_hex_srgb`;
- `approx_intermediate_hex_srgb`;
- `approx_flop_hex_srgb`;
- `diffuse_proxy_hex_srgb`.

Never store a field named simply:

- `paint_hex`;
- `true_hex`;
- `factory_hex`.

## What an official UI hex means

An official website may use a CSS colour or small image swatch to make an option recognizable in a menu.

That value may be:

- a deliberately simplified icon;
- gamut-limited;
- altered for contrast against the UI;
- unrelated to the 3D configurator material;
- tone-mapped;
- uncalibrated;
- changed without the physical paint changing.

It is still valuable as **official interface evidence**, but not as a physical material measurement.

## “Same hex, different paint”

The same display colour can correspond to:

- solid red;
- metallic red;
- pearl red;
- tinted-clear red;
- satin red.

They can match in one flat swatch and diverge completely on a curved car.

## “Different hex, same paint”

One physical paint can produce different sampled hex values across:

- face;
- flop;
- direct sun;
- overcast sky;
- warm studio;
- cool LED;
- different camera transforms.

V11 therefore treats display colour, paint identity and measured appearance as separate objects.

---

# CP11-CODE-ONTOLOGY — Paint names and codes are different systems

## Marketing name

Examples:

- a memorable colour name;
- heritage name;
- bespoke client name;
- launch-colour name.

The marketing name is not guaranteed unique.

## Configurator option ID

A dynamic internal ID used by the web configurator or order system.

It may:

- differ by market;
- differ by model;
- change between site versions;
- be unrelated to the repair code.

## Order code

The factory ordering code used in a price guide or build specification.

It can identify:

- a standard colour;
- a premium finish;
- a package;
- a two-tone combination.

## Repair paint code

The code used to identify a refinish formula or vehicle colour label.

It may have:

- multiple supplier variants;
- alternate formulations;
- plant or year variants;
- different names across markets.

## Formula variant

Refinish systems may publish several variants because production paint changes with:

- batch;
- supplier;
- plant;
- application;
- age;
- repair history;
- body/trim substrate.

## Vehicle-specific authority order

For identifying the paint on one real vehicle:

1. physical vehicle paint label and VIN/build data;
2. official build sheet or manufacturer record;
3. official parts/touch-up lookup;
4. official model-year order guide;
5. official configurator/brochure;
6. paint-supplier refinish database;
7. reputable specialist database;
8. owner/forum report.

No lower source silently overrides a higher source without a contradiction record.

## One-off and bespoke complications

A unique commission may have:

- no public repair code;
- an internal project name only;
- a client-specific name;
- a formula withheld by the manufacturer;
- a finish impossible to reorder on another model;
- painted graphics rather than one body colour.

The registry allows `public_code = null` without inventing one.

---

# CP11-SOURCE-HIERARCHY — Official information collection

## Tier O1 — Vehicle-specific official record

- VIN/build sheet;
- paint label;
- manufacturer/dealer record;
- official certificate.

## Tier O2 — Official ordering and service data

- order guide;
- price list;
- parts catalogue;
- touch-up catalogue;
- repair manual;
- paint bulletin.

## Tier O3 — Official configurator and visualizer

Useful for current:

- names;
- option IDs;
- availability;
- prices;
- displayed swatches;
- official rendered interpretation.

Not sufficient for repair identity or physical colour.

## Tier O4 — Official press, factory and brand programme pages

Useful for:

- bespoke programme;
- process;
- pigment;
- layer;
- handwork;
- historical and launch context.

## Tier O5 — Official brochures and archived brand material

Useful for historical model-year palettes.

## Tier S1 — Paint manufacturer/refinish-system data

Potentially strong for:

- formula codes;
- variants;
- spectrophotometer matching;
- repair practices.

It is not necessarily an OEM disclosure.

## Tier S2 — High-quality independent reporting

Useful when it directly quotes or observes a factory process.

## Tier S3 — Specialist databases, auction documents and owner records

Useful for discovery and cross-checking.

## Tier S4 — Forums/social media

Lead generation only until corroborated.

---

# CP11-OFFICIAL-EVIDENCE — Verified examples and their research linkage

## BMW Individual

BMW’s official Individual page states that the programme offers around 150 paint finishes and explicitly distinguishes:

- matt;
- metallic;
- uni/solid;
- two-tone combinations.

The page gives current examples such as:

- Borusan Turkish Blue;
- Velvet Orchid metallic;
- Speed Yellow;
- Fire Orange;
- Frozen Tampa Bay Green metallic.

BMW also states that two-tone finishes use:

- precise coach-line separation;
- hand masking;
- polishing;
- an edge-free finish.

### Research linkage

| BMW disclosure | V10/V11 concept |
|---|---|
| around 150 finishes | OEM palette population |
| matt versus metallic versus uni | distinct shader families |
| Frozen finish | matte coat and coat-effect attenuation |
| metallic refraction/shimmer | face/flop, flake and grain |
| two-tone hand masking | process history, panel masks, coach line |
| polished edge-free separation | DOI and topography around masking boundary |

Official source:  
https://www.bmw-m.com/en/fastlane/bmw-individual.html

## Mercedes-Benz MANUFAKTUR

Mercedes-Benz maintains an official MANUFAKTUR personalisation programme and routes customers toward market/model configuration.

The exact palette is dynamic and market-specific. Therefore V11 records:

- the programme;
- official configurator route;
- model/year/market palette snapshots;
- current swatches and option IDs at capture time.

It does not create one permanent global Mercedes palette.

Official source:  
https://www.mercedes-benz.com/en/vehicles/mercedes-benz-personalisation/manufaktur/

## Bentley and Mulliner

Bentley’s official paint article provides unusually valuable material disclosures.

### Orange Flame

Bentley states that Orange Flame:

- contains mica pigment;
- contains powdered aluminium;
- is painted in a single layer;
- is topped with clearcoat;
- is cured;
- receives a “mirror finish” polish.

### Yellow Flame

Bentley states that Yellow Flame uses a colourstream pigment containing rice-husk ash and produces a colour transition that emphasizes body form.

### Jetstream II

Bentley describes a fine metallic finish that can move from cool silver in warm light to soft blue in cooler light.

### Sequin Blue

Bentley describes:

- a rich metallic blue;
- deep shadow;
- “flop”;
- a custom match originating from a dress sequin.

### Azure Purple

Bentley describes a bespoke match inspired by an African violet.

### Research linkage

| Bentley disclosure | R&D consequence |
|---|---|
| mica + powdered aluminium | mixed effect-pigment class |
| single layer + clear | explicit layer mode |
| cured + mirror polish | coat DOI/surface-condition target |
| colourstream transition | interference/angular travel mode |
| warm/cool illuminant shift | metamerism/illuminant testing |
| deep shadow/flop | multi-angle body response |
| object matching | spectral/metameric bespoke workflow |
| custom coachbuilding | one-off identity without public code |

Official sources:

- https://www.bentleymedia.com/en/newsitem/1100-a-rainbow-of-bentleys-as-community-support-continues
- https://www.bentleymotors.com/en/models/mulliner/coachbuilt.html

## Rolls-Royce Bespoke

Current reporting from the Goodwood factory describes more than 44,000 colour options, while Rolls-Royce has continued investing in bespoke production.

Historically documented examples include:

- two-tone pearl finishes;
- Xirallic effect colours;
- fine glass-particle finishes;
- mother-of-pearl paint;
- hand-painted coachlines;
- client-matched colours.

### Research linkage

Rolls-Royce is a high-value case study for:

- massive palette identity;
- client-specific colour matching;
- pearl and glass-particle modes;
- hand-painted line assets;
- bespoke records without public repair codes;
- colour-name genealogy;
- one-off source preservation.

Current high-quality report:  
https://www.businessinsider.com/inside-rolls-royce-factory-where-six-figure-cars-are-made-2026-7

Official press archive:  
https://www.press.rolls-roycemotorcars.com/

## Range Rover SV Bespoke / JLR

Reuters reported in January 2025 that JLR would invest £65 million in additional paint facilities and more than double SV Bespoke Paint matching capacity.

The service allows Range Rover SV clients to request colours matched to objects such as private jets or yachts.

### Research linkage

This directly motivates:

- object-to-paint capture;
- spectral versus RGB matching;
- metamerism testing;
- substrate/sample metadata;
- custom colour approval references;
- capacity/process classification;
- client-specific paint records.

Source:  
https://www.reuters.com/business/autos-transportation/jlr-invest-80-mln-meet-demand-custom-luxury-car-colours-2025-01-27/

## Porsche Paint to Sample and Sonderwunsch

Porsche’s Paint to Sample and Sonderwunsch systems are important registry targets because they include:

- standard and historical paint identities;
- reissued colours;
- customer-request projects;
- painted liveries;
- factory recommissioning;
- one-off surface effects.

Exact current colour counts, prices and availability must be captured by:

- model;
- market;
- model year;
- configurator state.

No universal static Porsche palette should be inferred.

## Ferrari Tailor Made and One-Off

The relevant data classes include:

- standard palette;
- historical colours;
- special paints;
- two-tone and livery;
- Tailor Made;
- One-Off;
- client/project-specific names.

The 2026 Tailor Made 12Cilindri “Yoonseul” illustrates a colour-shifting finish and demonstrates that official factory colour identity may be artistic, geographic and project-specific rather than reducible to a repair code.

## Lamborghini Ad Personam

Ad Personam is a key registry target for:

- large premium palette;
- matte finishes;
- pearl/interference;
- exposed carbon combinations;
- gradients;
- special particles;
- one-off liveries.

Current palette size claims require point-in-time verification.

## McLaren Special Operations

MSO records should distinguish:

- standard McLaren paint;
- MSO Defined;
- MSO Bespoke;
- limited-series paint;
- hand-painted artwork;
- historic/papaya references.

The 2025 “Project Viva” example was reported as hand-painted, with a custom black containing cyan, magenta and green flecks.

## Aston Martin Q

Q by Aston Martin should be tracked as:

- configured Q options;
- Q Collections/limited commissions;
- one-off paint;
- liveries;
- historic colour references.

## Maserati Fuoriserie / BottegaFuoriserie

Track:

- catalogue Fuoriserie finishes;
- Corse and heritage references;
- colour-shifting bespoke examples;
- one-off/few-off Bottega projects;
- model/year availability.

## Lotus Chapman Bespoke

Track:

- factory standard options;
- Chapman Bespoke catalogue;
- Tailor Made requests where offered;
- Collection limited editions;
- heritage motorsport colours.

## Audi exclusive

Track:

- model configurator option;
- Audi exclusive catalogue;
- matte/pearl/crystal effect categories;
- special request colour;
- historical colour reissues;
- order and repair-code distinction.

## Mazda Takuminuri

Mazda’s Takuminuri finishes are important because they represent high-volume OEM attempts to reproduce a craftsman-like finish using production coating technology.

Registry targets include:

- Soul Red family;
- Machine Gray;
- Rhodium White;
- Artisan Red;
- layer/process descriptions;
- model-year formula changes;
- display swatches;
- body/repair constraints.

## Lexus and Toyota

Relevant classes include:

- multi-layer premium reds and whites;
- Structural Blue;
- self-repairing clearcoat examples;
- hand-finishing on flagship production;
- model-specific special colours;
- matte LFA/GR/Lexus options;
- Toyota colour codes and Lexus marketing names.

## Honda and Acura

Relevant classes include:

- historical NSX multistep aluminium-body paint;
- Andaro aerospace-grade finishes;
- heritage Championship White;
- matte Type S finishes;
- model-year paint codes;
- body/bumper matching.

## Tesla

Tesla should be tracked by:

- factory and plant;
- current configurator;
- Berlin multilayer colours;
- paint option ID;
- UI chip;
- official 3D configurator render;
- paint-label/repair identity where available;
- model-year changes.

Do not infer a Tesla paint’s physical model from the configurator hex.

# CP11-CONFIGURATOR — Official configurator ingestion workflow

## Purpose

Capture the manufacturer’s current public option state without pretending the UI is a physical measurement.

## Evidence to preserve

- full URL;
- model;
- derivative/trim;
- market;
- language;
- date and time;
- page HTML;
- JavaScript/JSON responses where legitimately accessible;
- option IDs;
- option names;
- prices and currency;
- CSS colours;
- swatch image URLs;
- 3D material/texture asset references if public;
- screenshots;
- selected environment;
- camera;
- version/build identifiers;
- terms/licensing notes;
- checksum.

## Ethical and legal rule

Do not:

- bypass authentication;
- defeat access controls;
- evade rate limits;
- scrape against explicit prohibitions;
- redistribute proprietary 3D assets without permission.

Where automated access is unsuitable, use:

- manual export;
- browser developer tools;
- screenshots;
- saved official PDFs;
- official brochures;
- archival references.

## Ingestion stages

### Stage A — Discovery

Identify:

- configurator URL;
- model/market route;
- option group containing exterior paint;
- official programme or visualizer.

### Stage B — Raw preservation

Save the original official evidence before transforming it.

### Stage C — Option extraction

Extract:

- name;
- ID;
- price;
- displayed finish classification;
- swatch;
- availability dependencies.

### Stage D — Display-proxy analysis

Record:

- raw CSS/image value;
- assumed colour space;
- central median/mean if sampled;
- alpha;
- surrounding UI;
- no physical claim.

### Stage E — Official 3D render capture

Capture several fixed views and, where possible:

- studio;
- daylight;
- dark environment;
- face;
- grazing.

### Stage F — Cross-reference

Compare:

- order guide;
- brochure;
- build data;
- repair/touch-up source;
- press descriptions.

### Stage G — Blender target creation

Create:

- reference card;
- layer classification;
- initial parameter prior;
- confidence grade;
- missing-data list.

### Stage H — Update diff

At a later date, append:

- added colours;
- removed colours;
- renamed colours;
- price changes;
- code changes;
- swatch changes;
- renderer changes.

Never overwrite the previous snapshot.

---

# CP11-SWATCH-TOOLS — Display-proxy extraction

## sRGB hex to scene-linear conversion

This converts a UI swatch for controlled display use. It does not turn it into a physical automotive paint.

```python
from __future__ import annotations

def srgb_channel_to_linear(channel_8bit: int) -> float:
    if not 0 <= channel_8bit <= 255:
        raise ValueError("sRGB channel must be in [0, 255].")

    c = channel_8bit / 255.0
    if c <= 0.04045:
        return c / 12.92
    return ((c + 0.055) / 1.055) ** 2.4

def hex_srgb_to_linear_rgb(value: str) -> tuple[float, float, float]:
    cleaned = value.strip().removeprefix("#")
    if len(cleaned) != 6:
        raise ValueError("Expected six-digit sRGB hex.")

    rgb = tuple(int(cleaned[i:i + 2], 16) for i in (0, 2, 4))
    return tuple(srgb_channel_to_linear(c) for c in rgb)
```

## Generic saved-JSON option extractor

This template operates on a locally saved response and deliberately makes no assumptions about one manufacturer’s private API.

```python
from __future__ import annotations

from collections.abc import Iterable
from pathlib import Path
import json

PAINT_KEYS = {
    "paint",
    "exteriorcolour",
    "exteriorcolor",
    "bodycolour",
    "bodycolor",
    "colour",
    "color",
}

def walk(value, path=()):
    if isinstance(value, dict):
        yield path, value
        for key, child in value.items():
            yield from walk(child, path + (str(key),))
    elif isinstance(value, list):
        for index, child in enumerate(value):
            yield from walk(child, path + (str(index),))

def looks_like_paint_option(path, obj) -> bool:
    path_text = "/".join(path).lower().replace("_", "").replace("-", "")
    if not any(key in path_text for key in PAINT_KEYS):
        return False

    keys = {str(key).lower() for key in obj}
    return bool(keys & {"name", "label", "title", "code", "id", "price", "hex", "colour", "color"})

def extract_candidates(source: Path) -> list[dict]:
    payload = json.loads(source.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    candidates = []

    for path, obj in walk(payload):
        if looks_like_paint_option(path, obj):
            candidates.append({
                "json_path": list(path),
                "raw": obj,
            })

    return candidates

source = Path("saved_configurator_response.json")
output = Path("paint_option_candidates.json")

result = extract_candidates(source)
output.write_text(json.dumps(result, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False), encoding="utf-8")
print(f"Wrote {len(result)} candidates to {output}")
```

The output requires human/LLM review. Automatic extraction must not fabricate semantic meaning from ambiguous fields.

---

# CP11-OEM-TO-BLENDER — Direct research linkage

## Solid and uni finishes

Map to:

- `CP_Groundcoat`;
- body colour/opacity;
- clearcoat;
- orange peel;
- process/panel variation.

Do not add flakes merely because the material is automotive.

## Metallic finishes

Map to:

- homogeneous angular response;
- aluminium/effect flake field;
- orientation distribution;
- grain;
- sparkle;
- clearcoat;
- LOD/moment filtering.

## Pearl and mica

Map to:

- groundcoat;
- translucent/effect midcoat;
- chromatic angular travel;
- grain/sparkle;
- coat.

## Interference and colourstream

Map to:

- multi-angle chromatic response;
- constrained thin-film/interference model or LUT;
- distinct effect-particle field;
- illuminant testing.

## Tinted clear and candy

Map to:

- reflective/effect base;
- absorbing colour layer;
- clearcoat interface;
- thickness/path dependence.

## Matte/Frozen/Satin

Map to:

- outer coat roughness/haze;
- reduced DOI;
- coat-to-effect attenuation;
- altered scratch/maintenance state.

## Two-tone

Map to:

- exact panel/region mask;
- coach line;
- masking history;
- local edge topography/DOI;
- separate paint identities.

## Gradient/ombré

Map to:

- multiple paint identities;
- physically/process-informed transition region;
- pass direction;
- blend width;
- technician/process variation;
- no simple Base Color gradient assumption.

## Hand-painted coachline/livery

Map to:

- decal/vector/curve asset;
- paint depth and coat order;
- hand variation;
- layer position;
- protected or exposed finish.

## Object-matched bespoke paint

Map to:

- source-object capture;
- illuminant and spectral data;
- metamerism risk;
- approval reference;
- client/project identity;
- no assumed public code.

## Historic colour reissue

Map to:

- historical name/code;
- original chemistry/process;
- modern re-formulation;
- modern clearcoat;
- new body shape;
- same-name/different-system comparison.

---

# CP11-PAINT-DNA — Brand and era analysis

## Brand paint DNA is not one average colour

A brand can be analysed through:

- prevalence of solid versus effect finishes;
- typical flake scale;
- preference for high-chroma or muted colours;
- matte availability;
- use of heritage reissues;
- two-tone tradition;
- bespoke openness;
- launch-colour strategy;
- body-shape interaction;
- premium pricing;
- colour-name semantics;
- historical national-racing colour links.

## Statistical analyses

Once registry coverage is sufficient:

- finish-family frequency by brand/year;
- median paint-option premium;
- colour-hue distribution;
- neutral versus chromatic share;
- matte adoption over time;
- number of heritage reissues;
- special programme breadth;
- effect-class versus model segment;
- colour lifespan in configurators;
- swatch gamut;
- marketing name reuse;
- code/name collisions;
- process disclosure depth.

## Practical output

Use OEM populations to select representative benchmark paints:

1. mainstream solid white;
2. mainstream metallic silver;
3. dark fine metallic;
4. coarse bright metallic;
5. pearl white tri-coat;
6. premium deep red;
7. matte/Frozen finish;
8. interference colourstream;
9. tinted-clear/candy;
10. two-tone;
11. bespoke object match;
12. gradient/ombré.

This benchmark set is more defensible than choosing arbitrary attractive colours.

---

# CP11-ASSET-METADATA — Blender asset integration

## Asset name

```text
CP_OEM__BRAND__MARKETING-NAME__CODE__MY__MARKET__v###
```

Unknown values remain explicitly `UNKNOWN`, never guessed.

## Custom properties

Recommended material custom properties:

- `cp_oem_record_id`;
- `cp_brand`;
- `cp_marketing_name`;
- `cp_option_code`;
- `cp_repair_code`;
- `cp_model_year`;
- `cp_market`;
- `cp_finish_family`;
- `cp_source_grade`;
- `cp_ui_swatch_hex_srgb`;
- `cp_reference_date`;
- `cp_shader_version`;
- `cp_validation_status`;
- `cp_confidence`;
- `cp_known_limitations`.

## Asset Browser hierarchy

```text
Automotive_Paint/
├── OEM/
│   ├── BMW_Group/
│   ├── Mercedes_Benz/
│   ├── Volkswagen_Group/
│   ├── Ferrari_Stellantis/
│   ├── JLR/
│   ├── Japanese/
│   ├── Korean/
│   ├── North_American/
│   ├── Chinese/
│   └── Low_Volume/
├── Finish_Family/
├── Historical/
├── Bespoke/
├── Measured/
├── Configurator_Proxy/
└── Research_Only/
```

A configurator-derived material must carry `Configurator_Proxy`, not `Measured`.

# CP11-EXPERIMENTS — OEM-linked R&D

## EXP-097 — Same hex, different finish

Create solid, metallic, pearl and matte finishes sharing one face-on display proxy.

Expected result: the curved and moving outputs diverge strongly.

## EXP-098 — Official UI chip versus official configurator render

Compare:

- CSS/image swatch;
- official 3D configurator car;
- official marketing photography.

Record how the official sources disagree.

## EXP-099 — Configurator render inversion

Attempt to fit the official rendered appearance, then test under a different Blender light rig.

Purpose: determine how much of the official target belongs to paint versus its renderer/environment.

## EXP-100 — Same OEM paint on different body shapes

Use one accepted material on:

- flat coupon;
- sedan;
- SUV;
- sports car.

Measure whether perceived paint identity survives curvature.

## EXP-101 — Same marketing name across years

Compare codes, swatches, descriptions and references for recurring names.

## EXP-102 — Same code, formula variants

Document repair variants and test a controlled family of small appearance differences.

## EXP-103 — Body-versus-bumper match

Apply body and flexible-trim variants with subtle process differences.

## EXP-104 — Historical reissue

Model:

- original-era finish;
- modern factory reissue;
- modern clearcoat;
- current body shape.

## EXP-105 — BMW Frozen versus gloss family

Hold body/effect constant and test outer-coat changes and effect attenuation.

## EXP-106 — Bentley Orange Flame reconstruction

Use disclosed:

- mica;
- powdered aluminium;
- single effect layer;
- clearcoat;
- cured/polished finish.

Compare against generic red metallic.

## EXP-107 — Bentley Sequin Blue angular reconstruction

Fit:

- deep shadow;
- flop;
- illuminant response;
- body-form emphasis.

## EXP-108 — Object-match metamerism

Capture a source object and construct two RGB-matching formulations with different spectral assumptions.

## EXP-109 — Two-tone hand-mask boundary

Model:

- precise coach line;
- local layer order;
- edge-free polish;
- optional microscopic process evidence.

## EXP-110 — Gradient/ombré process

Compare:

- Base Color gradient;
- blended paint-layer/process model;
- separate technician passes.

## EXP-111 — Palette option-diff

Capture one configurator twice and produce an append-only change report.

## EXP-112 — Swatch collision detector

Find different OEM options sharing very similar UI hexes but different finish classes.

## EXP-113 — Official swatch gamut analysis

Plot current OEM UI swatches in a perceptual colour space while retaining finish metadata.

## EXP-114 — Price versus complexity

Test whether premium price correlates with:

- matte;
- special pigment;
- additional layer;
- hand process;
- bespoke matching;
- low production throughput.

Do not interpret price as a pure physics measure.

## EXP-115 — Brand benchmark blind test

Render equivalent finish classes with branding removed and test whether claimed “brand paint DNA” is actually visible.

## EXP-116 — Launch-colour bias

Compare the level of official imagery and shader fitting attention given to launch colours versus ordinary catalogue colours.

## EXP-117 — Official 3D material asset inspection

Where publicly exposed and legally usable, record:

- texture channels;
- environment;
- material IDs;
- swatches;
- tone mapping.

Do not redistribute proprietary assets.

## EXP-118 — Repair-code disambiguation

Use one vehicle example to trace:

- marketing name;
- option code;
- physical paint label;
- official parts lookup;
- refinish formula.

## EXP-119 — Dynamic market variation

Compare the same model’s official colours in:

- UK;
- EU;
- US;
- Middle East;
- Japan;
- China.

## EXP-120 — OEM population-driven preset design

Build shader presets from observed finish-family clusters rather than arbitrary artist categories.

---

# CP11-CONTRADICTIONS — New contradiction register

## CTR-008 — Official swatch as base colour

**Claim A:** The manufacturer’s UI swatch is the best starting Base Color.

**Claim B:** The swatch is only a recognition icon and may bias fitting.

**Resolution experiment:** EXP-098 and EXP-099.

## CTR-009 — Same name means same paint

**Claim A:** A historical/reissued name preserves one colour identity.

**Claim B:** chemistry, supplier, layer stack and clearcoat may change materially.

**Resolution experiment:** EXP-101 and EXP-104.

## CTR-010 — Repair code uniquely determines appearance

**Claim A:** one code equals one formula.

**Claim B:** production and refinish variants require several formulas.

**Resolution experiment:** EXP-102 and EXP-118.

## CTR-011 — Bespoke object match is spectrally faithful

**Claim A:** matching a yacht, jet or personal object reproduces its colour universally.

**Claim B:** the match may be approved under a limited set of illuminants and geometries.

**Resolution experiment:** EXP-108.

## CTR-012 — Official configurator render is physical truth

**Claim A:** the official 3D renderer is the authoritative material.

**Claim B:** it is an authored perceptual sales representation.

**Resolution experiment:** EXP-099.

## CTR-013 — Brand paint DNA is measurable

**Claim A:** brands have recognizable finish tendencies.

**Claim B:** perceived identity comes mainly from body design, photography and marketing.

**Resolution experiment:** EXP-115.

---

# CP11-COVERAGE-SCORE — Registry completeness

Score each record independently:

## Identity score

- 0: name only;
- 1: official name;
- 2: official name + model/year/market;
- 3: option/order code;
- 4: repair code/build confirmation;
- 5: vehicle-specific authoritative identity.

## Appearance score

- 0: no visual evidence;
- 1: UI chip;
- 2: official render;
- 3: multi-environment official/photographic evidence;
- 4: calibrated multi-angle data;
- 5: measured spectral/BRDF/BTF/AxF.

## Process score

- 0: unknown;
- 1: finish class inferred;
- 2: official finish class;
- 3: official layer/pigment clue;
- 4: official process description;
- 5: measured or factory-documented layer/process data.

## Blender score

- 0: no material;
- 1: UI proxy;
- 2: initial classified preset;
- 3: controlled test;
- 4: motion/cross-light validated;
- 5: target-specific production accepted.

A high score in one category does not compensate for zero in another.

---

# CP11-MAINTENANCE — Dynamic updates

## Update triggers

- new model year;
- configurator redesign;
- new bespoke programme;
- paint option added/removed;
- price or option-code change;
- formula revision;
- special edition;
- official process disclosure;
- new measured dataset;
- selected project vehicle.

## Snapshot naming

```text
OEM__BRAND__MODEL__MY__MARKET__YYYY-MM-DD__SOURCE-TYPE
```

## Append-only diff

Record:

- previous snapshot;
- new snapshot;
- additions;
- removals;
- renames;
- code changes;
- swatch changes;
- price changes;
- source changes;
- confidence changes.

## Archival caution

Web pages disappear. Preserve:

- citation;
- title;
- date;
- screenshot;
- locally saved text/JSON where legally appropriate;
- checksum;
- archival URL where available.

---
