# Part II — Automotive body material ontology

## ABR-MAT-001 — Appearance is not material identity

A visible silver accent may be:

- polished stainless steel;
- polished aluminium;
- bright nickel with chromium;
- plated ABS;
- PVD-coated plastic;
- vacuum-metallised plastic under clearcoat;
- silver paint;
- aluminium-filled paint;
- anodised aluminium;
- brushed metal;
- satin metal;
- a rendered UI approximation.

These are not interchangeable.

The same rule applies to “carbon,” “black plastic,” “glass,” “rubber,” “ceramic” and “chrome.”

## ABR-MAT-002 — Required material record

Every accepted automotive-body material should record:

```json
{
  "record_id": null,
  "common_name": null,
  "actual_material_or_stack": [],
  "substrate": null,
  "manufacturing_process": [],
  "surface_process": [],
  "layer_order": [],
  "geometry_requirements": [],
  "optical_model": null,
  "directional_properties": [],
  "physical_scale": {},
  "condition_state": null,
  "reference_ids": [],
  "blender_group": null,
  "engine_scope": [],
  "implementation_status": "HYP",
  "known_failure_modes": [],
  "validation_experiments": []
}
```

## ABR-MAT-003 — Five-scale decomposition

For every body material, separate:

1. **Macro form** — panel, grille, lens, wheel or trim geometry.
2. **Meso manufacturing structure** — weave, brush direction, mould texture, machining marks, perforations.
3. **Micro surface structure** — roughness, scratches, pits, coating topography.
4. **Optical layer structure** — interfaces, absorption, scattering, metal layers, resin, clearcoat.
5. **Camera/display response** — sampling, bloom, clipping, temporal integration.

A texture cannot repair a missing macro form. A roughness map cannot replace an optical layer. A normal modifier cannot create correct silhouette.

---

# Part III — Carbon fibre and composite accents

## ABR-COMP-001 — Composite families

### Continuous woven carbon

- plain weave;
- 2×2 twill;
- 4×4 twill;
- satin weave;
- spread tow;
- hybrid carbon/aramid;
- coloured tracer fibres.

### Continuous unidirectional carbon

- visible aligned bands;
- tape lay-up;
- automated fibre placement;
- structural but sometimes cosmetically exposed.

### Chopped and moulded carbon

- carbon sheet moulding compound;
- forged/chopped composite;
- compression-moulded flakes/tow bundles;
- random or flow-aligned orientation.

### Other visible composites

- glass-fibre composite;
- basalt fibre;
- aramid/Kevlar;
- flax/natural fibre;
- carbon-aramid hybrid;
- carbon-titanium hybrid structures;
- fibre-reinforced thermoplastics.

## ABR-COMP-002 — A carbon surface is a layered optical structure

A useful representation separates:

- air/clearcoat interface;
- clearcoat roughness and orange peel;
- resin-rich surface;
- fibre/resin refractive contrast;
- fibre direction;
- tow geometry;
- weave interlacing;
- sub-surface depth and occlusion;
- lower plies or black substrate;
- manufacturing defects;
- wear and UV state.

A black checker texture with a glossy coat does not reproduce these components.

## ABR-COMP-003 — Weave terminology

### Warp and weft

Two principal fibre directions.

### Tow

A bundle of filaments. The tow has width, thickness, edge softness, twist/spread and local directional response.

### Crimp

Out-of-plane waviness produced by interlacing. Crimp changes:

- highlight direction;
- apparent depth;
- shadowing;
- local normal;
- fibre packing.

### Float length

Number of opposing yarns passed over before going under.

### Bias and drape

A woven sheet can shear as it conforms to compound curvature. The pattern does not remain a rigid planar checker.

## ABR-COMP-004 — Manufacturing routes and visual consequences

| Process | Visual consequences |
|---|---|
| prepreg/autoclave | controlled fibre/resin ratio, possible high cosmetic quality, seams and print-through remain possible |
| resin-transfer moulding | mould-face quality, resin flow and dry spots can matter |
| wet lay-up | variable resin richness, bubbles, weave distortion and surface correction |
| compression moulding/SMC | chopped bundles, flow alignment, marbled appearance |
| thermoplastic stamp forming | drape, consolidation and flow signatures |
| cosmetic outer ply | attractive weave may not match structural plies |
| painted composite | fibre may be invisible but print-through can remain |
| clearcoated exposed carbon | coat optics and UV protection dominate the outer response |

## ABR-COMP-005 — Print-through

Print-through is the appearance of underlying fibre/ply structure in the final surface topography.

It can affect:

- reflected strip lines;
- DOI;
- broad waviness;
- local normal;
- visible weave depth.

It must be treated as coat/resin topography, not merely darkening the weave.

The CFRP mirror research retained in the source demonstrates that ply orientation can affect local surface deformation and optical-quality print-through. [REF-ABR-CFRP-PRINT]

## ABR-COMP-006 — Resin-rich and fibre-rich zones

### Resin-rich

- smoother colour field;
- stronger dielectric interface;
- potentially deeper-looking fibre;
- pools near corners or seams;
- greater local clear/amber tint.

### Fibre-rich

- stronger fibre texture;
- more local roughness;
- reduced apparent resin depth;
- possible pinholes or dry fibre.

The shader should vary these with a manufacturing field, not one generic Noise texture.

## ABR-COMP-007 — Carbon weave coordinate problem

A production-quality weave requires:

- stable physical pitch;
- consistent tow width;
- known warp/weft directions;
- panel-specific drape;
- seam placement;
- no Generated-coordinate resets;
- separate visible-cosmetic and structural orientation where needed.

### Recommended data

- `cf_uv_weave`;
- `cf_warp_direction`;
- `cf_weft_direction`;
- `cf_drape_shear`;
- `cf_ply_id`;
- `cf_seam_distance`;
- `cf_resin_richness`;
- `cf_print_through`.

## ABR-COMP-008 — Drape and shear model

A planar weave mapped to a doubly curved panel should allow local shear.

Represent the local weave with two non-collinear direction fields:

```text
WARP = normalized direction field A
WEFT = normalized direction field B
shear_angle = acos(clamp(dot(WARP, WEFT), -1, 1))
```

The angle need not remain exactly 90°.

Potential sources:

- UV deformation;
- Geometry Nodes tangent fields;
- manually authored direction maps;
- cloth/drape simulation bake;
- CAD/manufacturing reference.

## ABR-COMP-009 — Procedural weave cell

A single weave cell requires:

- warp tow;
- weft tow;
- over/under ownership;
- tow profile;
- crimp normal;
- tow-edge transition;
- fibre substructure;
- resin gap.

### Plain weave

Alternating over/under every tow.

### 2×2 twill

Diagonal progression with two-over/two-under pattern.

### Spread tow

Flatter, wider tows and lower crimp.

### Common failure

A perfect sine checker produces fabric wallpaper rather than cured composite.

## ABR-COMP-010 — Node architecture

### `CF_COORDINATE_STATE_B50`

Outputs:

- physical UV/panel space;
- warp/weft directions;
- cell coordinate;
- seam and ply fields.

### `CF_WEAVE_TOPOLOGY_B50`

Outputs:

- warp mask;
- weft mask;
- over/under state;
- tow centre distance;
- gap mask;
- crimp height.

### `CF_FIBRE_RESPONSE_B50`

Outputs:

- directional fibre colour;
- local fibre roughness;
- tangent-aligned normal variation;
- filament banding.

### `CF_RESIN_B50`

Outputs:

- resin colour/absorption;
- resin roughness;
- depth/coverage proxy;
- pinhole mask;
- resin-rich field.

### `CF_CLEARCOAT_B50`

Reuses the paint coat discipline:

- separate coat normal;
- coat topography;
- haze/DOI;
- damage/age.

### `CF_PRINT_THROUGH_B50`

Low-amplitude, physically scaled coat/resin height linked to ply structure.

### `CF_LOD_B50`

Transitions from:

- discrete filament/tow geometry;
- normal/height weave;
- filtered directional texture;
- homogeneous anisotropic response.

## ABR-COMP-011 — Fibre direction and anisotropy

Visible carbon often has strong directional cues, but a generic anisotropic metal shader is not sufficient.

The apparent response combines:

- dielectric resin interface;
- dark absorbing fibres;
- fibre-aligned microstructure;
- tow normal;
- weave crimp;
- clearcoat.

Use fibre direction to drive:

- anisotropic roughness where supported;
- directional Gabor/micro-striation;
- tangent-space normal detail;
- local contrast.

Do not make carbon metallic by default.

## ABR-COMP-012 — Gabor linkage

Gabor fields are useful for:

- filament-aligned microstructure;
- tow-edge fuzz;
- sanding/polishing marks in clearcoat;
- directional print-through.

They are not the whole weave topology.

## ABR-COMP-013 — Forged/chopped carbon

A chopped-composite field requires:

- elongated irregular bundle shapes;
- broad size distribution;
- local orientation;
- overlap/occlusion;
- resin gaps;
- compression-flow direction;
- edge clipping;
- no obvious Voronoi polygon boundaries.

### Candidate approach

1. Generate stable bundle centres with Poisson/Voronoi IDs.
2. Assign bundle length, width, orientation and depth.
3. Use local coordinates to form elongated masks.
4. Layer several depth/size populations.
5. Drive directional fibre response per bundle.
6. Filter to a marbled homogeneous response at distance.

### Better hero approach

- Geometry Nodes curves/instances embedded below a resin surface;
- bake orientation, depth and coverage maps;
- shader reads the baked fields.

## ABR-COMP-014 — Carbon edge and cut surface

A cut composite edge differs from the mould face.

Possible features:

- visible ply stack;
- rough machining;
- exposed fibres;
- resin;
- paint/clearcoat termination;
- chamfer or sealant.

Use real edge geometry for hero parts.

## ABR-COMP-015 — Defects and condition

- pinholes;
- trapped air;
- dry spots;
- resin pools;
- weave waviness;
- seam/overlap;
- ply print-through;
- UV-yellowed resin;
- cloudy clearcoat;
- sanding marks;
- delamination;
- impact whitening;
- chipped clearcoat;
- polished-through edges.

Each defect must own a physical layer.

## ABR-COMP-016 — Implementation tiers

### Tier C0 — distant preview

- image texture;
- stable UV;
- Principled dielectric;
- mild normal/roughness.

### Tier C1 — production animation

- authored weave orientation map;
- filtered tow normal;
- separate clearcoat;
- no subpixel filament glitter.

### Tier C2 — hero

- procedural or baked over/under weave;
- crimp;
- resin depth;
- print-through;
- seams;
- directional microstructure.

### Tier C3 — macro ground truth

- explicit tow/filament geometry patch;
- transparent resin shell or layered approximation;
- dense Cycles sampling;
- no denoising reference.

## ABR-COMP-017 — Validation

Test with:

- broad strip;
- compact source;
- cross-polarized reference;
- camera rotation;
- object rotation;
- macro-to-full-car distance;
- UV/drape debug;
- mirror/reflection comparison.

## ABR-COMP-018 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-001`: plain versus twill topology.
- `EXP-ABR-002`: rigid UV weave versus draped direction field.
- `EXP-ABR-003`: equal pattern, different clearcoat.
- `EXP-ABR-004`: print-through height-band test.
- `EXP-ABR-005`: forged-carbon bundle field.
- `EXP-ABR-006`: image/bake/procedural/geometry LOD comparison.
- `EXP-ABR-007`: fibre-direction response under moving strip.
- `EXP-ABR-008`: resin-richness ablation.
- `EXP-ABR-009`: visible ply cut edge.
- `EXP-ABR-010`: motion stability at delivery resolution.

---

# Part IV — Metals, chrome and deposited finishes

## ABR-METAL-001 — “Chrome” classification

### Electroplated decorative chromium

Usually a multilayer system whose appearance and corrosion performance are strongly influenced by underlying nickel and substrate preparation.

### Plating on plastic

A plastic substrate is etched/activated, metallised and plated. Geometry still has moulded-plastic tolerances and parting lines.

### PVD coating

A thin deposited coating can produce:

- bright chrome-like;
- dark/black chrome;
- coloured metallic;
- satin finishes.

### Vacuum metallisation

A thin aluminium-like film on plastic, often protected by a clear layer.

### Polished metal

The substrate itself supplies the reflection:

- stainless steel;
- aluminium;
- nickel;
- titanium;
- brass.

### Metallic paint

Not chrome. Pigment is dispersed within a binder and has a broad, particulate effect.

## ABR-METAL-002 — Layer architecture

A plated decorative part can contain:

```text
air
→ optional clear/protective topcoat
→ chromium/PVD/deposited appearance layer
→ bright or satin nickel
→ copper/electroless conductive layer where applicable
→ substrate
```

The top metal layer may be nanometres or micrometres thick and optically dominated by the entire stack and surface finish.

## ABR-METAL-003 — Substrate fingerprints

### Plated plastic

Look for:

- injection-moulded seam logic;
- wall thickness;
- clips and bosses;
- soft edge radii;
- local sink/warp;
- peeling or blistering at damage.

### Plated metal

Look for:

- stamping/casting/machining form;
- sharper durable edges;
- corrosion under plating;
- dents;
- different thermal and scratch history.

## ABR-METAL-004 — Polished versus chrome-like

Polished stainless/aluminium and bright plating differ in:

- spectral reflectance;
- native oxide;
- roughness;
- scratch morphology;
- colour tint;
- edge/process history;
- corrosion.

Do not represent all with Metallicity 1 and Roughness 0.

## ABR-METAL-005 — Brushed metal

Required fields:

- brush direction;
- stroke-width distribution;
- scratch depth;
- scratch-length distribution;
- cross-scratches;
- polishing haze;
- panel/manufacturing orientation;
- edge turnaround.

### Node linkage

- tangent frame;
- Gabor or authored directional texture;
- anisotropic BSDF controls;
- separate sparse deep scratches;
- filtered micro-normal.

### Failure

Perfect parallel procedural lines produce vinyl rather than brushed metal.

## ABR-METAL-006 — Machined and diamond-cut surfaces

Features:

- concentric or tool-path grooves;
- deterministic pitch;
- feed variation;
- sharp fresh facets;
- clearcoat or oxidation;
- curb damage;
- local directional sparkle.

The geometry may need real grooves for macro shots. A normal map may suffice at wheel-view distance.

## ABR-METAL-007 — Bead-blasted and satin metal

Use:

- broad microfacet distribution;
- isotropic or process-dependent microtexture;
- restrained normal variation;
- no painted diffuse noise.

Satin chrome may be produced by the underlying texture plus plating, not by roughening a perfect chromium surface arbitrarily.

## ABR-METAL-008 — Anodised aluminium

Anodising forms an oxide layer whose pore structure and colour treatment can alter appearance.

CG consequences:

- metal substrate remains;
- surface may be dyed or interference-coloured;
- roughness inherits machining/blasting;
- edge wear can expose raw aluminium;
- fingerprints/water spotting differ from paint.

Do not treat anodised aluminium as opaque coloured plastic.

## ABR-METAL-009 — Black chrome and dark metallic coatings

Possible real systems include:

- dark PVD;
- black nickel/chromium variants;
- tinted clear over metal;
- dark metallic paint;
- DLC-like coatings.

The material record must identify the system before choosing a shader.

## ABR-METAL-010 — Heat tint

Titanium and stainless exhaust components can develop oxide-film colours.

A useful model separates:

- base metal;
- temperature/exposure field;
- thin-film/oxide colour;
- soot;
- polishing/scratches;
- weld heat-affected zone.

A rainbow ramp painted in object space is only an artistic proxy unless linked to thermal/process history.

## ABR-METAL-011 — Metal node architecture

### `METAL_SUBSTRATE_B50`

- conductor colour/F0 proxy;
- roughness;
- anisotropy;
- native oxide state.

### `METAL_PROCESS_TEXTURE_B50`

- brushed;
- machined;
- blasted;
- polished;
- cast.

### `METAL_DEPOSITED_LAYER_B50`

- plating/PVD/vacuum metal;
- optional thin-film tint;
- protective clear.

### `METAL_DAMAGE_B50`

- scratches;
- pits;
- corrosion;
- peeling;
- curb rash;
- exposed substrate.

### `METAL_LOD_B50`

- groove geometry;
- normal map;
- anisotropic homogeneous response.

## ABR-METAL-012 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-011`: plated ABS versus polished stainless.
- `EXP-ABR-012`: bright nickel/chromium layer ablation.
- `EXP-ABR-013`: brush-direction motion test.
- `EXP-ABR-014`: diamond-cut wheel groove LOD.
- `EXP-ABR-015`: PVD black chrome versus tinted clear.
- `EXP-ABR-016`: anodised edge wear.
- `EXP-ABR-017`: heat-tint field around weld.
- `EXP-ABR-018`: vacuum-metallised lamp reflector ageing.

---

# Part V — Plastics, elastomers and rubber

## ABR-PLASTIC-001 — Exterior plastic families

- polypropylene bumper/grille plastics;
- ABS;
- PC-ABS;
- polycarbonate;
- PMMA/acrylic;
- polyurethane;
- nylon/polyamide;
- glass-filled polymers;
- thermoplastic elastomers;
- EPDM seals;
- painted flexible plastics;
- piano-black coated plastic;
- mould-textured unpainted trim.

## ABR-PLASTIC-002 — Injection-moulding fingerprints

Potentially visible:

- mould texture;
- parting line;
- gate vestige;
- ejector marks;
- sink marks;
- flow lines;
- weld/knit lines;
- fibre orientation;
- gloss variation;
- local warpage;
- draft;
- rounded nominal thickness.

These are not generic dirt.

## ABR-PLASTIC-003 — Surface metrology

The ABS/PC-ABS automotive-surface research retained here uses ISO 25178-style areal parameters to quantify morphology after surface treatment. [REF-ABR-ABS-PCABS]

This links directly to:

- microtexture synthesis;
- paint adhesion state;
- gloss;
- scratch visibility;
- mould-finish presets.

## ABR-PLASTIC-004 — Unpainted textured trim

A plausible shader separates:

- bulk polymer colour;
- moulded areal texture;
- grazing Fresnel;
- pigment/filler;
- local wear/polish;
- UV fading/chalking;
- contamination.

The texture should be physically scaled and attached to the moulded part.

## ABR-PLASTIC-005 — Piano black

Piano-black trim is typically a coated or highly polished plastic system.

Important consequences:

- extremely visible scratches;
- dust;
- fingerprints;
- swirls;
- strong reflection;
- edge bevel quality;
- substrate may become visible after deep damage.

It is not simply black Base Color at low Roughness.

## ABR-PLASTIC-006 — Painted flexible body parts

Painted bumper and mirror systems may differ from metal-body paint because of:

- substrate;
- primer;
- flex additives;
- cure temperature;
- separate line;
- basecoat rewetting;
- local geometry;
- colour matching.

The paint corpus already identifies body/trim mismatch. This chapter gives it a substrate/process owner.

## ABR-PLASTIC-007 — Glass-filled polymer

Visible texture can include:

- fibre-induced flow;
- anisotropic surface;
- mould finish;
- local whitening at stress;
- exposed fibres at wear.

Do not use woven carbon texture for short glass-filled polymer.

## ABR-PLASTIC-008 — Elastomers and weather seals

Key appearance:

- high absorption;
- low but non-zero dielectric reflection;
- mould/extrusion texture;
- seams;
- compression;
- dust;
- silicone dressing;
- UV oxidation;
- cracking;
- contact-polished regions.

Use geometry for seal compression and lip shape.

## ABR-PLASTIC-009 — Tyre rubber

Separate:

- sidewall compound;
- tread compound;
- lettering;
- mould texture;
- vent spews;
- seam;
- wear;
- embedded dirt;
- bloom/dressing;
- heated/abraded tread;
- wetness.

Carbon black affects material structure and appearance, but tyres are not perfect black diffuse objects.

## ABR-PLASTIC-010 — Node architecture

- `PLASTIC_BULK_B50`
- `PLASTIC_MOULD_TEXTURE_B50`
- `PLASTIC_COATING_B50`
- `PLASTIC_PROCESS_DEFECTS_B50`
- `ELASTOMER_B50`
- `TYRE_B50`
- `PLASTIC_AGE_B50`

## ABR-PLASTIC-011 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-019`: three mould textures with equal roughness.
- `EXP-ABR-020`: piano-black scratch hierarchy.
- `EXP-ABR-021`: painted bumper/body mismatch.
- `EXP-ABR-022`: PC-ABS etch/morphology-inspired fields.
- `EXP-ABR-023`: EPDM seal under compression.
- `EXP-ABR-024`: tyre new/dressed/worn/wet states.

---

# Part VI — Glazing and transparent body materials

## ABR-GLASS-001 — Automotive glazing stacks

### Windscreen

Common conceptual stack:

```text
air
→ outer glass
→ polymer interlayer
→ inner glass
→ cabin air
```

Optional features:

- acoustic interlayer;
- tint band;
- infrared/UV coatings;
- heating elements;
- antenna traces;
- HUD wedge;
- embedded sensors;
- ceramic frit;
- hydrophobic coating.

### Side/rear glazing

May be:

- tempered glass;
- laminated glass;
- privacy/tinted glass;
- heated;
- antenna-integrated.

## ABR-GLASS-002 — Geometry requirements

Hero glazing needs:

- physical thickness;
- correct inner/outer surfaces;
- edge thickness;
- no coincident shells;
- consistent normals;
- realistic seals;
- fit into body aperture;
- optional curvature variation;
- no smoothing kink across windscreen.

A single zero-thickness polygon is a preview representation.

## ABR-GLASS-003 — Interlayer

PVB is widely used in laminated automotive safety glass because of adhesion, optical clarity, toughness and flexibility. [REF-ABR-PVB]

CG implications:

- slight absorption/tint;
- internal reflection stack;
- crack retention;
- bubble/delamination defects;
- acoustic and HUD variants.

## ABR-GLASS-004 — Frit and dot matrix

Treat as:

- ceramic/opaque printed region;
- true placement on a glass surface;
- halftone transition;
- adhesive/edge concealment role;
- source-specific pattern.

Use texture/decal or real dot geometry for macro shots.

Do not scatter random dots without matching the vehicle pattern.

## ABR-GLASS-005 — Coatings and tints

Separate:

- bulk glass colour;
- interlayer colour;
- deposited solar-control coating;
- privacy tint;
- aftermarket film;
- contamination;
- hydrophobic layer.

## ABR-GLASS-006 — Defects

- stone chips;
- wiper scratches;
- pitting;
- delamination;
- bubbles;
- edge haze;
- water spots;
- washer residue;
- condensation;
- thermal wires;
- repair resin.

The 2024 safety-glass study links bubbles to trapped air, dissolved water, temperature and polymer relaxation. [REF-ABR-LSG-BUBBLE]

## ABR-GLASS-007 — Rendering concerns

- shadow transmission;
- caustics;
- screen-space refraction limits;
- multiple nested interfaces;
- reflection probes;
- depth sorting;
- denoising;
- thin versus solid glass;
- viewport/engine differences.

A Cycles research stack and an EEVEE production stack may differ while preserving identity.

## ABR-GLASS-008 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-025`: zero-thickness versus solid windscreen.
- `EXP-ABR-026`: clear/tinted/interlayer absorption.
- `EXP-ABR-027`: frit texture versus geometry macro.
- `EXP-ABR-028`: nested-interface reflection audit.
- `EXP-ABR-029`: delamination/bubble model.
- `EXP-ABR-030`: EEVEE/Cycles glazing parity.

---

# Part VII — Headlamp and taillamp optical assemblies

## ABR-LIGHT-001 — Assembly decomposition

A lamp is not an emissive texture.

Potential components:

- outer polycarbonate lens;
- hardcoat;
- black bezel;
- vacuum-metallised reflector;
- projector lens;
- LED package;
- light guide;
- diffuser;
- prism/micro-optic pattern;
- internal mask;
- seal;
- vent;
- wiring;
- condensation;
- dust.

## ABR-LIGHT-002 — Polycarbonate lens

Polycarbonate offers impact resistance but requires hardcoat/UV protection because of scratch susceptibility and weathering/yellowing risk. [REF-ABR-PC-LENS]

Represent:

- bulk transparent polymer;
- outer hardcoat;
- mould flow/stress only where visible;
- micro-scratches;
- pitting;
- yellowing/haze;
- inner/outer surface.

## ABR-LIGHT-003 — Vacuum-metallised reflector

Often a thin metal layer on moulded plastic.

The reflected form depends heavily on:

- mould geometry;
- metallisation smoothness;
- protective layer;
- oxidation/burning;
- dust;
- heat history.

A perfect mirror on crude game geometry will expose every polygon error.

## ABR-LIGHT-004 — Light guide

A light guide may use total internal reflection and extraction features.

Required CG components:

- transparent solid;
- internal geometry;
- LED injection region;
- extraction prisms/dots;
- absorption;
- emission transported or artistically approximated;
- bloom separated from material.

### Production options

1. true emissive geometry with transparent guide;
2. baked light distribution;
3. volume/emission approximation;
4. compositor bloom.

The option must declare whether it simulates transport or only the final appearance.

## ABR-LIGHT-005 — Projector optics

Hero representation may include:

- lens curvature;
- cutoff shield;
- reflector;
- bulb/LED source;
- internal glass;
- coating tint;
- dust/condensation.

Do not emit uniformly from the whole projector lens.

## ABR-LIGHT-006 — Micro-optics and prisms

At distance:

- normal/height or baked response.

At macro scale:

- real prism geometry;
- correct repetition;
- thickness;
- refraction/TIR.

## ABR-LIGHT-007 — Lamp state system

- off;
- DRL;
- position;
- low beam;
- high beam;
- indicator;
- brake;
- reverse;
- animation sequence.

The material and light objects should share a state controller.

## ABR-LIGHT-008 — Ageing

- lens hardcoat failure;
- haze/yellowing;
- pitting;
- reflector burn;
- seal failure;
- condensation;
- dead LED segment;
- dirt on inner surfaces.

## ABR-LIGHT-009 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-031`: emissive texture versus layered lamp.
- `EXP-ABR-032`: PC hardcoat ageing.
- `EXP-ABR-033`: light-guide transport tiers.
- `EXP-ABR-034`: reflector geometry validation.
- `EXP-ABR-035`: prism normal map versus geometry.
- `EXP-ABR-036`: lamp-state controller.
- `EXP-ABR-037`: condensation volume/surface model.

---

# Part VIII — Grilles, meshes and perforated accents

## ABR-MESH-001 — Representation classes

- open geometry;
- alpha texture;
- normal/displacement;
- procedural Geometry Nodes;
- layered hybrid.

## ABR-MESH-002 — When alpha is insufficient

Use geometry when:

- silhouette reveals thickness;
- camera enters grazing angle;
- shadows matter;
- reflections reveal wires;
- openings expose internal depth;
- macro shot;
- grille deforms across complex form.

## ABR-MESH-003 — Layered grille anatomy

A realistic grille may include:

- front patterned surface;
- wire/solid thickness;
- bevel;
- rear blackout panel;
- radiator;
- shutters;
- supports;
- sensors;
- badge;
- depth gap;
- dirt/insect accumulation.

## ABR-MESH-004 — Geometry Nodes reconstruction

Inputs:

- boundary surface;
- pattern type;
- cell dimensions;
- wire thickness;
- depth;
- bevel;
- orientation;
- deformation/projection;
- LOD.

Outputs:

- hero geometry;
- baked normal/opacity;
- proxy geometry.

## ABR-MESH-005 — Common patterns

- diamond expanded mesh;
- woven wire;
- perforated sheet;
- honeycomb;
- slats;
- chain/rectangular mesh;
- brand-specific parametric cells.

Expanded mesh is not identical to woven wire: strips have directional face orientation and thickness.

## ABR-MESH-006 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-038`: alpha/normal/geometry grille comparison.
- `EXP-ABR-039`: expanded mesh versus woven wire.
- `EXP-ABR-040`: three-depth grille stack.
- `EXP-ABR-041`: automatic bake from hero grille.
- `EXP-ABR-042`: LOD transition under motion.

---

# Part IX — Badges, decals, seals and details

## ABR-DETAIL-001 — Badge construction

Possible stacks:

- plated plastic emblem;
- enamel infill;
- painted badge;
- printed badge;
- clear-domed badge;
- carbon backing;
- adhesive foam;
- mechanical pins.

Geometry requirements:

- readable bevel;
- true thickness;
- backing gap;
- contact shadow;
- no floating surface.

## ABR-DETAIL-002 — Enamel and domed emblems

Separate:

- metal border;
- coloured enamel/resin;
- clear dome;
- printed layer;
- scratches/chips;
- edge meniscus.

## ABR-DETAIL-003 — Decals and painted graphics

Classify:

- sticker/vinyl;
- waterslide/transfer;
- printed under clearcoat;
- painted livery;
- hand-painted coachline;
- laser-etched mark.

The layer order determines:

- edge height;
- gloss;
- damage;
- dirt accumulation;
- reflection continuity.

## ABR-DETAIL-004 — Fasteners

Represent:

- head type;
- drive recess;
- material/coating;
- washer;
- seating;
- thread only when visible;
- orientation logic;
- repeated instances;
- tool marks;
- corrosion.

## ABR-DETAIL-005 — Seals and adhesive joints

Need:

- contact geometry;
- compression;
- rounded lip;
- matte/rubber response;
- dirt line;
- ageing;
- panel-gap interaction.

## ABR-DETAIL-006 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-043`: plated badge layer stack.
- `EXP-ABR-044`: enamel meniscus.
- `EXP-ABR-045`: decal above/below clearcoat.
- `EXP-ABR-046`: procedural fastener orientation.
- `EXP-ABR-047`: compressed weather seal.

---

# Part X — Wheels, brakes, hubs and tyres

## ABR-WHEEL-001 — Wheel manufacturing classes

- cast aluminium;
- flow-formed;
- forged;
- multi-piece forged;
- magnesium;
- carbon-composite wheel;
- steel;
- aero cover.

Manufacturing affects:

- spoke thickness;
- edge radii;
- machining;
- porosity/texture;
- finish;
- damage.

## ABR-WHEEL-002 — Wheel finish classes

- painted;
- powder-coated;
- clearcoated bare metal;
- polished;
- brushed;
- diamond-cut/machined face;
- anodised;
- chrome/PVD;
- matte;
- carbon fibre.

A wheel may combine several finishes in one part.

## ABR-WHEEL-003 — Diamond-cut wheel

Separate:

- machined aluminium face;
- concentric tool marks;
- painted recess;
- clearcoat;
- corrosion beneath damaged clear;
- curb rash.

## ABR-WHEEL-004 — Iron brake disc

Appearance fields:

- machined friction tracks;
- rust on non-contact regions;
- heat tint;
- pad transfer;
- drilled/slotted geometry;
- vent channels;
- hub coating;
- wear lip.

The friction track changes with use and should align with rotation.

## ABR-WHEEL-005 — Carbon-ceramic disc

Road-car carbon-ceramic discs are commonly C/SiC-type ceramic-matrix composites rather than cosmetic woven carbon.

Potential visible features:

- grey/brown composite matrix;
- fibre/bundle texture;
- holes/slots;
- friction layer;
- wear;
- temperature;
- different reflectance from iron.

Do not paste twill carbon on a brake disc.

## ABR-WHEEL-006 — Calipers

- cast/forged metal form;
- paint or anodised finish;
- printed logo;
- brake dust;
- heat state;
- chips;
- piston/boot details where visible.

## ABR-WHEEL-007 — Tyres and sidewalls

Geometry:

- real tread;
- sidewall bulge;
- lettering;
- bead;
- vent spews;
- contact flattening;
- wear.

Shader:

- sidewall/tread separation;
- mould texture;
- carbon-black bulk;
- dressing/wetness;
- dust;
- abrasion.

## ABR-WHEEL-008 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-048`: cast/forged finish family.
- `EXP-ABR-049`: diamond-cut groove LOD.
- `EXP-ABR-050`: iron disc wear/rust field.
- `EXP-ABR-051`: C/SiC disc appearance.
- `EXP-ABR-052`: brake-dust deposition.
- `EXP-ABR-053`: tyre load/contact patch.
- `EXP-ABR-054`: rotating wheel temporal aliasing.

---

# Part XI — Exhaust and heat-affected materials

## ABR-EXHAUST-001 — Material classes

- stainless steel;
- titanium;
- Inconel/nickel alloy;
- mild steel;
- chrome-plated tip;
- black-coated tip;
- carbon-fibre shroud;
- ceramic coating.

## ABR-EXHAUST-002 — Process fields

- weld;
- grinding;
- polishing;
- forming;
- heat-affected zone;
- soot deposition;
- water staining;
- oxidation;
- road contamination.

## ABR-EXHAUST-003 — Soot

Soot distribution depends on:

- flow direction;
- outlet shape;
- exhaust state;
- condensation;
- cleaning;
- geometry.

It should not be uniform black dirt around every tip.

## ABR-EXHAUST-004 — Thermal colour

Use a process field derived from:

- distance from heat source/weld;
- estimated temperature history;
- material class;
- oxidation state.

A thin-film approximation can supply colour, but the field must have a cause.

## ABR-EXHAUST-005 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-055`: stainless/titanium/Inconel family.
- `EXP-ABR-056`: heat tint around weld.
- `EXP-ABR-057`: soot transport mask.
- `EXP-ABR-058`: polished tip age progression.

---

# Part XII — Films, wraps and protective accents

## ABR-FILM-001 — Stack classes

- paint protection film;
- coloured vinyl wrap;
- printed livery wrap;
- chrome wrap;
- satin/matte wrap;
- window tint film;
- lamp-protection film;
- clear doming film.

## ABR-FILM-002 — Geometry signals

- film edge;
- seam;
- overlap;
- stretch;
- trapped air;
- lifting corner;
- cut line;
- adhesive;
- orange peel;
- contamination.

## ABR-FILM-003 — Stretch field

A wrap deformed around compound curvature may show:

- print distortion;
- gloss anisotropy;
- thickness change;
- whitening;
- edge tension.

Store strain or UV-distortion proxies from Geometry Nodes.

## ABR-FILM-004 — Chrome wrap is not plated chrome

Chrome film may have:

- polymer surface;
- metallised layer;
- adhesive;
- lower DOI;
- stretch distortion;
- orange peel;
- seam.

## ABR-FILM-005 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-059`: bare paint versus PPF.
- `EXP-ABR-060`: wrap stretch and print distortion.
- `EXP-ABR-061`: plated chrome versus chrome wrap.
- `EXP-ABR-062`: film edge at macro distance.

---

# Part XIII — Game-asset forensics

## ABR-GAME-001 — Preserve the original

The first imported asset is evidence.

Before repair:

- save a pristine `.blend`;
- preserve the original file;
- record checksum;
- record importer and settings;
- record Blender version;
- duplicate into a working collection;
- never destructively clean the only copy.

## ABR-GAME-002 — Legal and provenance record

Record:

- source;
- licence/permission;
- game/version;
- extraction/import path;
- original filename;
- file format;
- tool versions;
- redistribution restrictions.

Technical feasibility does not imply redistribution permission.

## ABR-GAME-003 — Initial forensic inventory

For every object:

- name;
- hierarchy;
- transform;
- dimensions;
- polygon count;
- triangles;
- vertices;
- edges;
- material slots;
- UV maps;
- colour attributes;
- generic attributes;
- custom-normal state;
- sharp edges;
- loose geometry;
- non-manifold edges;
- duplicate/near-duplicate objects;
- LOD label;
- collision/helper status;
- alpha material;
- negative scale;
- mirrored transform;
- animation/pivot;
- bounding box.

## ABR-GAME-004 — Do not join everything

Joining can destroy or obscure:

- panel identity;
- pivot;
- transform;
- material logic;
- LOD correspondence;
- smoothing boundaries;
- hierarchy;
- instancing;
- mirror information.

Join only with a declared reason.

## ABR-GAME-005 — Triangulation is evidence

The original triangulation may encode:

- intended runtime shading;
- quad diagonal choice;
- UV split;
- normal split;
- deformation;
- decimation history.

Do not dissolve triangles before comparing the original rendered surface.

## ABR-GAME-006 — Custom normals are evidence

Game assets may rely heavily on authored or baked normals.

Before recalculation:

- render with imported normals;
- dump normal data where available;
- duplicate the mesh;
- test recalculation on the duplicate;
- compare reflection lines.

Recalculate Normals is not a neutral cleanup.

## ABR-GAME-007 — UV and tangent evidence

Preserve:

- all UV sets;
- UV names/order;
- mirrored islands;
- stacked islands;
- texel density;
- tangent-basis assumptions;
- normal-map convention;
- material assignments.

A normal map can appear broken because of tangent/UV conversion rather than the texture itself.

## ABR-GAME-008 — Alpha geometry evidence

An alpha-masked part may represent:

- grille;
- cooling fin;
- badge detail;
- perforation;
- lamp pattern;
- hair-like fibre;
- decal;
- dirt.

Classify before replacing it.

## ABR-GAME-009 — LOD family

Different LODs can contain complementary information:

- LOD0 form;
- lower-LOD clean topology;
- alternate materials;
- collision volume;
- missing backside geometry;
- simpler panel segmentation.

Do not assume the highest polygon count is the best reconstruction source.

## ABR-GAME-010 — Game material archaeology

Record every original texture channel and shader parameter if accessible:

- base colour/albedo;
- normal;
- roughness/gloss;
- metallic/specular;
- AO;
- opacity;
- emissive;
- masks;
- detail normal;
- clearcoat;
- colour tint;
- material ID;
- vertex colour usage.

Do not blindly map a gloss texture to Blender Roughness without inversion/interpretation.

## ABR-GAME-011 — Coordinate and unit audit

Determine:

- up axis;
- forward axis;
- handedness;
- unit scale;
- scene scale;
- wheelbase;
- tyre diameter;
- known real dimension.

Use several known dimensions when possible.

## ABR-GAME-012 — Object-classification labels

- `SRC_RENDER`;
- `SRC_LOD`;
- `SRC_COLLISION`;
- `SRC_HELPER`;
- `SRC_ALPHA`;
- `SRC_DECAL`;
- `SRC_LIGHT`;
- `SRC_INTERIOR`;
- `SRC_UNKNOWN`.

## ABR-GAME-013 — Rehabilitation classes

### Class A — Preserve

Geometry and shading survive body-wide validation.

### Class B — Data repair

Form is acceptable; repair normals, UVs, materials, seams or transforms.

### Class C — Local reconstruction

Most form is usable; specific edges/panels/details are rebuilt.

### Class D — Retopology/projection

Source is used as a reference surface.

### Class E — Rebuild

Runtime representation is incomplete, overly simplified or fundamentally unsuitable.

## ABR-GAME-014 — Decision criteria

Classify using:

- silhouette;
- panel form;
- reflection-line continuity;
- topology;
- UV/tangent recoverability;
- material role;
- target camera distance;
- animation;
- time budget;
- accuracy requirement.

## ABR-GAME-015 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-063`: imported versus recalculated normals.
- `EXP-ABR-064`: LOD0 versus lower-LOD topology.
- `EXP-ABR-065`: original triangulation versus dissolved topology.
- `EXP-ABR-066`: normal-map tangent convention.
- `EXP-ABR-067`: alpha grille reconstruction.
- `EXP-ABR-068`: object hierarchy preservation.
- `EXP-ABR-069`: unit-scale reconstruction.

---

# Part XIV — Geometry rehabilitation

## ABR-REHAB-001 — Repair hierarchy

Use the least destructive method that passes validation:

1. transform/unit correction;
2. normal orientation correction;
3. duplicate-vertex/weld investigation;
4. shading-boundary repair;
5. custom-normal preservation/transfer;
6. local topology repair;
7. local reconstruction;
8. retopology;
9. complete rebuild.

## ABR-REHAB-002 — Geometry versus normal failure

### Geometry failure

Visible in:

- silhouette;
- mirror reflection;
- true-normal field;
- multiple light directions;
- subdivision/displacement.

### Normal-only failure

May disappear when:

- true geometric normals are used;
- custom normals are corrected;
- split boundaries are restored.

### Texture/tangent failure

May track:

- UV seams;
- mirrored islands;
- normal map;
- tangent basis.

The diagnostic must identify the domain before editing.

## ABR-REHAB-003 — Reflection-line priority

A vehicle body is evaluated by reflected environments.

Use:

- mirror;
- neutral clear dielectric;
- long strip lights;
- zebra/reflection-line shader;
- moving strip animation;
- silhouette.

Grey clay alone is insufficient.

## ABR-REHAB-004 — Local panel reconstruction

Preferred workflow:

1. isolate panel boundary;
2. preserve reference mesh;
3. place curves/edge landmarks;
4. build clean surface patch;
5. Shrinkwrap/project cautiously;
6. compare curvature and reflection;
7. transfer UV/material data if useful;
8. restore gap/edge thickness.

## ABR-REHAB-005 — Retopology over source

Use when:

- silhouette is trustworthy;
- source triangulation is dense/irregular;
- subdivision is required;
- reflections reveal facets;
- UVs can be rebaked/transferred.

Retopology should preserve measured/form landmarks rather than merely create all-quads.

## ABR-REHAB-006 — Subdivision suitability

Subdivision is suitable when the control mesh can represent the intended limit surface.

Warning signs:

- uncontrolled pinching;
- too many triangles/poles in highlight-critical zones;
- panel swelling;
- edge collapse;
- normal discontinuity;
- source already represents a tessellated CAD surface more accurately than a guessed SubD cage.

## ABR-REHAB-007 — CAD-like game mesh

A dense triangulated mesh may be a tessellated CAD/NURBS export.

Possible best strategy:

- preserve form;
- fix shading/normal data;
- local repairs;
- do not force into a low-poly SubD model unless needed.

## ABR-REHAB-008 — Gaps and shut lines

Panel gaps require:

- real separation or controlled recess;
- edge radius;
- depth;
- inner darkness/geometry;
- seal;
- no infinitely black painted line.

## ABR-REHAB-009 — Thin parts

For grilles, fins, splitters and lamp internals:

- determine actual thickness;
- avoid zero-thickness reflections;
- use Solidify only where offset direction and corner behaviour are controlled;
- inspect intersections.

## ABR-REHAB-010 — Symmetry

Mirror can help rebuild symmetric parts, but preserve asymmetries:

- fuel door;
- badges;
- exhaust;
- cameras/sensors;
- wipers;
- tow hooks;
- antenna;
- interior.

## ABR-REHAB-011 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-070`: preserve dense CAD-like mesh versus SubD rebuild.
- `EXP-ABR-071`: local patch projection.
- `EXP-ABR-072`: panel-gap geometry tiers.
- `EXP-ABR-073`: body reflection before/after repair.
- `EXP-ABR-074`: topology/normal/texture-domain diagnosis.
- `EXP-ABR-075`: fresh-file reconstruction test.

---

# Part XV — Normals, tangents and shading continuity

## ABR-NORMAL-001 — Normal types

- polygon/face normal;
- vertex normal;
- split/corner normal;
- custom normal;
- true geometric normal;
- shading normal;
- tangent-space normal map result.

Keep terminology explicit.

## ABR-NORMAL-002 — Smooth by Angle

Official Blender 5.0 documentation states that Smooth by Angle sets edge sharpness according to the angle between neighbouring faces and is a bundled Geometry Nodes asset. [REF-BL50-SMOOTH-ANGLE]

Use for:

- deliberate smoothing boundaries;
- mechanical parts;
- diagnostic classification.

It does not repair form.

## ABR-NORMAL-003 — Weighted Normal

Official Blender 5.0 documentation states that Weighted Normal changes custom normals and can weight by face area, corner angle or both. [REF-BL50-WEIGHTED-NORMAL]

Use when:

- large planar faces should dominate;
- bevelled hard-surface parts need controlled shading;
- vertex-group scope is explicit.

Do not use as proof that topology is correct.

## ABR-NORMAL-004 — Data Transfer

Official Blender 5.0 documentation states that Data Transfer can copy UV maps, colour attributes, custom normals and other data, with several mapping strategies. [REF-BL50-DATA-TRANSFER]

High-value uses:

- transfer source normals to rebuilt topology;
- transfer UVs/material masks;
- project data from a clean reference surface;
- preserve game data during local reconstruction.

## ABR-NORMAL-005 — Normal Edit

Official documentation describes radial/directional custom-normal generation and notes game/architecture uses. [REF-BL50-NORMAL-EDIT]

For automotive work it is a specialised cheat, not a default body repair.

## ABR-NORMAL-006 — Subdivision and normals

Blender’s documentation distinguishes subdivision from smooth shading, states modifier order matters, and warns that non-contiguous normals can prevent smooth subdivision in regions. [REF-BL50-SUBDIV]

## ABR-NORMAL-007 — Normal deviation metric

Use the paint diagnostic:

```text
1 - dot(normalize(N_true), normalize(N_shading))
```

Also record direction, not only magnitude.

## ABR-NORMAL-008 — Tangent-space repair

Check:

- UV map used to generate tangent;
- normal map colour space;
- handedness/green channel;
- mirrored UV islands;
- triangulation;
- transformed scale;
- tangent recomputation.

## ABR-NORMAL-009 — Inverted normals

Detect and fix only after:

- identifying deliberate internal/backface surfaces;
- checking two-sided materials;
- preserving a source copy.

## ABR-NORMAL-010 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-076`: custom/imported/weighted/transferred normal comparison.
- `EXP-ABR-077`: modifier-order normal audit.
- `EXP-ABR-078`: tangent/triangulation normal-map test.
- `EXP-ABR-079`: moving-strip detection threshold.
- `EXP-ABR-080`: carbon panel normal ownership.

---

# Part XVI — UV, texture and material recovery

## ABR-UV-001 — Preserve all maps

Rename only after recording original names/order.

Classify:

- body/effect UV;
- lightmap;
- decals;
- detail;
- condition;
- trim sheet;
- atlas;
- unknown.

## ABR-UV-002 — Texel density

Measure by material role.

A body paint procedural may not need the source UV, while:

- carbon weave;
- badges;
- lights;
- decals;
- mould texture;

often do.

## ABR-UV-003 — Mirroring

Mirrored UVs can break:

- text;
- carbon weave handedness;
- scratches;
- tangent normal maps;
- asymmetric dirt.

## ABR-UV-004 — Atlas archaeology

A game atlas can contain unrelated parts at different scales.

Before reusing:

- identify each island/object;
- map material channels;
- preserve alpha;
- inspect padding and mip bleed.

## ABR-UV-005 — Rebaking

Potential bakes:

- source normal to clean mesh;
- AO/cavity;
- material IDs;
- carbon direction;
- decals;
- height;
- curvature;
- thickness;
- original albedo details.

## ABR-UV-006 — Alpha replacement

When replacing alpha with geometry:

- preserve the alpha pattern as reference;
- reconstruct depth/thickness;
- bake proxy LOD;
- compare silhouette and shadow.

## ABR-UV-007 — Experiments

- `EXP-ABR-081`: source UV versus clean UV transfer.
- `EXP-ABR-082`: atlas extraction.
- `EXP-ABR-083`: mirrored tangent test.
- `EXP-ABR-084`: alpha-to-geometry bake pipeline.

---

# Part XVII — Blender modifier atlas for game-car rehabilitation

## ABR-MOD-001 — No universal stack

Modifier order and suitability depend on part class.

The correct question is:

> Which defect is this modifier intended to correct, and which valid source information might it destroy?

## ABR-MOD-002 — Modifier decision matrix

| Modifier | Strong use | Dangerous misuse | Required test |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smooth by Angle | edge smoothing classification | assumed geometry repair | mirror and sharp-boundary audit |
| Weighted Normal | scoped hard-surface shading | hiding body topology error | true-normal deviation and moving strip |
| Data Transfer | normals/UV/attributes from reference | nearest mapping across wrong surfaces | mapping-distance/visual diff |
| Normal Edit | deliberate radial/directional cheat | universal smooth-body fix | object rotation and silhouette |
| Weld | merge duplicate/near vertices | closing deliberate panel/UV seams | seam inventory and before/after counts |
| Triangulate | freeze diagonal/runtime parity | destructive early triangulation | normal-map and shading comparison |
| Subdivision Surface | clean controllable limit surface | applying to arbitrary game topology | limit-surface and panel-form comparison |
| Bevel | real edge radius or shading support | adding uniform toy-like edges | physical radius and silhouette |
| Mirror | controlled symmetry reconstruction | deleting intended asymmetry | asymmetry checklist |
| Shrinkwrap | project retopo/patch to reference | copying source faceting/noise | offset and reflection-line test |
| Solidify | thickness for sheet parts | self-intersections and wrong rim | section and silhouette |
| Decimate | proxy/LOD generation | hero form rehabilitation | Hausdorff/reflection/silhouette error |
| Remesh | topology reset for rough reference | destroying Class-A form/UVs | form-distance and reflection |
| Corrective Smooth | deformation cleanup | shape drift on static body | landmark distance |
| Laplacian Smooth | controlled noise reduction | shrinking/softening creases | volume/form preservation |
| Surface Deform | bind detail to repaired surface | hidden dependency | fresh-file portability |
| Boolean | openings/cutouts | messy coplanar topology | cleanup and reflection test |
| Geometry Nodes | repeatable reconstruction/tools | monolithic opaque procedural stack | named outputs and bake fallback |

## ABR-MOD-003 — Part-specific starting points

### Exterior body panel

Possible:

```text
local topology repair
→ optional Data Transfer
→ controlled Subdivision only if suitable
→ final normal strategy
```

### Badge/trim

Possible:

```text
Mirror
→ Bevel
→ Weighted Normal
```

### Thin splitter/fin

Possible:

```text
surface cleanup
→ Solidify
→ Bevel
→ normal strategy
```

### Retopology patch

Possible:

```text
Mirror if valid
→ Shrinkwrap/project
→ Subdivision
→ Data Transfer/bake
```

### Grille proxy

Possible:

```text
Geometry Nodes hero pattern
→ LOD/bake branch
```

These are hypotheses, not universal recipes.

# Part XIX — Python forensic audit

## ABR-AUTO-001 — Blender 5.0.1 mesh inventory script

The following script avoids destructive operations. It records object, mesh, material, UV, attribute and modifier information. It is a starting artifact and requires live verification in Blender 5.0.1.

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import bpy
import bmesh
import hashlib
import json
from pathlib import Path
from mathutils import Vector

REQUIRED_VERSION = (5, 0, 1)

def assert_version() -> None:
    running = tuple(bpy.app.version[:3])
    if running != REQUIRED_VERSION:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Expected Blender {REQUIRED_VERSION}; running {running}. "
            "Audit a copied file if intentionally testing another version."
        )

def matrix_to_rows(matrix) -> list[list[float]]:
    return [[float(value) for value in row] for row in matrix]

def vector_list(value) -> list[float]:
    return [float(component) for component in value]

def material_record(material) -> dict:
    if material is None:
        return {"name": None}

    return {
        "name": material.name,
        "use_nodes": bool(material.use_nodes),
        "blend_method": getattr(material, "surface_render_method", None),
        "node_count": len(material.node_tree.nodes) if material.use_nodes and material.node_tree else 0,
    }

def mesh_topology_record(mesh: bpy.types.Mesh) -> dict:
    bm = bmesh.new()
    try:
        bm.from_mesh(mesh)

        boundary_edges = sum(1 for edge in bm.edges if edge.is_boundary)
        manifold_edges = sum(1 for edge in bm.edges if edge.is_manifold)
        wire_edges = sum(1 for edge in bm.edges if edge.is_wire)
        loose_verts = sum(1 for vert in bm.verts if not vert.link_edges)

        zero_area_faces = 0
        for face in bm.faces:
            if face.calc_area() <= 1e-12:
                zero_area_faces += 1

        return {
            "boundary_edges": boundary_edges,
            "manifold_edges": manifold_edges,
            "wire_edges": wire_edges,
            "loose_vertices": loose_verts,
            "zero_area_faces": zero_area_faces,
        }
    finally:
        bm.free()

def object_record(obj: bpy.types.Object) -> dict:
    base = {
        "name": obj.name,
        "type": obj.type,
        "parent": obj.parent.name if obj.parent else None,
        "matrix_world": matrix_to_rows(obj.matrix_world),
        "location_local": vector_list(obj.location),
        "rotation_mode": obj.rotation_mode,
        "scale_local": vector_list(obj.scale),
        "dimensions_world_aligned": vector_list(obj.dimensions),
        "negative_determinant": obj.matrix_world.to_3x3().determinant() < 0.0,
        "modifiers": [
            {
                "name": modifier.name,
                "type": modifier.type,
                "show_viewport": bool(modifier.show_viewport),
                "show_render": bool(modifier.show_render),
            }
            for modifier in obj.modifiers
        ],
        "material_slots": [
            material_record(slot.material)
            for slot in obj.material_slots
        ],
    }

    if obj.type != "MESH":
        return base

    mesh = obj.data
    mesh.calc_loop_triangles()

    base["mesh"] = {
        "data_name": mesh.name,
        "vertices": len(mesh.vertices),
        "edges": len(mesh.edges),
        "polygons": len(mesh.polygons),
        "loop_triangles": len(mesh.loop_triangles),
        "loops": len(mesh.loops),
        "uv_layers": [
            {
                "name": layer.name,
                "active": layer == mesh.uv_layers.active,
                "active_render": bool(layer.active_render),
            }
            for layer in mesh.uv_layers
        ],
        "color_attributes": [
            {
                "name": attribute.name,
                "domain": attribute.domain,
                "data_type": attribute.data_type,
            }
            for attribute in mesh.color_attributes
        ],
        "attributes": [
            {
                "name": attribute.name,
                "domain": attribute.domain,
                "data_type": attribute.data_type,
                "is_internal": bool(getattr(attribute, "is_internal", False)),
            }
            for attribute in mesh.attributes
        ],
        "has_custom_normals": getattr(mesh, "has_custom_normals", None),
        "materials": [
            material.name if material else None
            for material in mesh.materials
        ],
        "topology": mesh_topology_record(mesh),
    }

    return base

def scene_record(scene: bpy.types.Scene) -> dict:
    return {
        "blender_version": bpy.app.version_string,
        "file": bpy.data.filepath,
        "unit_system": scene.unit_settings.system,
        "unit_scale_length": scene.unit_settings.scale_length,
        "engine": scene.render.engine,
        "objects": [object_record(obj) for obj in scene.objects],
    }

assert_version()

output = Path(bpy.path.abspath("//ABR_mesh_forensic_inventory.json"))
payload = scene_record(bpy.context.scene)
output.write_text(json.dumps(payload, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")
print(f"Wrote {output}")
```

## ABR-AUTO-002 — Audit limitations

The script does not prove:

- a boundary is wrong;
- a custom normal is correct;
- a mesh is watertight by design;
- an alpha part should become geometry;
- source scale is physically correct.

It generates evidence for classification.

## ABR-AUTO-003 — Suggested automated extensions

- image/texture inventory;
- missing-file report;
- duplicate mesh hash;
- near-duplicate geometry detection;
- material-channel inference;
- UV overlap/area statistics;
- world-space reference distance;
- normal-deviation bake;
- object naming classifier;
- LOD pairing;
- screenshot/render matrix;
- modifier ablation;
- checksum manifest.

---

# Part XXII — Contradictions and anti-patterns

## CTR-ABR-001 — Carbon is an anisotropic glossy checker

False as a complete model. It ignores resin, crimp, depth, clearcoat and drape.

## CTR-ABR-002 — Chrome equals Metallicity 1, Roughness 0

False as a material identity. Different chrome-like processes have distinct stacks and substrates.

## CTR-ABR-003 — Recalculate Normals is safe cleanup

False. Imported custom normals may be essential runtime data.

## CTR-ABR-004 — All triangles should become quads

False. Source triangulation may preserve form and shading; dense CAD-like triangulation may be preferable to a guessed SubD cage.

## CTR-ABR-005 — Weighted Normal repairs topology

False. It changes shading normals.

## CTR-ABR-006 — Subdivision always improves a vehicle model

False. It can distort form, panel edges and highlight flow.

## CTR-ABR-007 — Alpha grille is always bad

False. It may be the correct distant LOD. The error is using it outside its valid shot class.

## CTR-ABR-008 — More geometry is always more accurate

False. Bad reconstruction geometry can be worse than a filtered texture or preserved source mesh.

## CTR-ABR-009 — One material preset can represent all exterior plastic

False.

## CTR-ABR-010 — Lamp realism comes from stronger bloom

False. Geometry and optical assembly must be correct first.

- composites;
- metals;
- plastics;

## Phase 7 — Integration

- gaps;
- seals;
- contact;

## Phase 8 — Validation and packaging

- engine;
- performance;
- asset browser;
- manifest;
- accepted/rejected evidence.

# Part XXIV — Source register for the first automotive-body expansion

## Blender 5.0 official documentation

### REF-BL50-WEIGHTED-NORMAL

- **Title:** Weighted Normal Modifier
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/modifiers/normals/weighted_normal.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026
- **Supports:** custom-normal weighting by face area and/or corner angle; vertex-group scope; face influence
- **Does not establish:** corrected geometry

### REF-BL50-DATA-TRANSFER

- **Title:** Data Transfer Modifier
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/modifiers/modify/data_transfer.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026
- **Supports:** transferring/interpolating UVs, colour attributes, custom normals and other mesh data

### REF-BL50-SUBDIV

- **Title:** Subdivision Surface Modifier
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/modifiers/generate/subdivision_surface.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026
- **Supports:** Catmull–Clark/Simple modes, modifier-order importance, UV smoothing, custom-normal interpolation, non-contiguous-normal limitation

### REF-BL50-SMOOTH-ANGLE

- **Title:** Smooth By Angle Modifier
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/modifiers/normals/smooth_by_angle.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026
- **Supports:** edge sharpness from neighbouring-face angle; bundled Geometry Nodes asset

### REF-BL50-NORMAL-EDIT

- **Title:** Normal Edit Modifier
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/modifiers/normals/normal_edit.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026
- **Supports:** radial/directional generated custom normals and mixing

### REF-BL50-MESH-ANALYSIS

- **Title:** Mesh Analysis
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/meshes/mesh_analysis.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026
- **Supports:** built-in visual mesh-analysis categories after local confirmation

### REF-BL50-TRIANGULATE

- **Title:** Triangulate Modifier
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation
- **Version:** Blender 5.0
- **URL:** https://docs.blender.org/manual/en/5.0/modeling/modifiers/generate/triangulate.html
- **Audit:** opened 15 July 2026

## Composite research

### REF-ABR-CFRP-PRINT

- **Title:** Mitigating Print-Through Effects Through an Optimized Method for CFRP Mirror Production
- **Authors:** S. Castillo et al.
- **Year:** 2020
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.01562
- **Role:** primary research on lay-up and optical surface print-through

### REF-ABR-CFSMC-ORIENTATION

- **Title:** Quantitative Fiber Orientation Analysis of Carbon Fiber Sheet Molding Compounds using Polarization Imaging and X-Ray Computed Tomography
- **Authors:** M. Duhovic et al.
- **Year:** 2024
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.11597
- **Role:** fibre-orientation measurement in moulded chopped carbon

### REF-ABR-CFRP-REFLECTIVITY

- **Title:** Angle-dependent Reflectivity of Twill-Weave Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer for Millimetre Waves
- **Authors:** G. Artner et al.
- **Year:** 2017
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.03603
- **Scope warning:** millimetre-wave, not visible-light BRDF
- **Use:** evidence that weave orientation can create directional electromagnetic response; not direct visible-render calibration

## Plastics and glass

### REF-ABR-ABS-PCABS

- **Title:** Morphological Characterization of ABS and PC-ABS Surfaces for Automotive Industry
- **Authors:** G. Deninno et al.
- **Year:** 2022
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.12533
- **Role:** areal surface morphology and ISO 25178 parameters

### REF-ABR-LSG-BUBBLE

- **Title:** Why Does Safety Glass Bubble?
- **Authors:** C. Arauz-Moreno, K. Piroird, E. Lorenceau
- **Year:** 2024
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.04617
- **Role:** bubble formation in glass/PVB laminate

### REF-ABR-PVB

- **Title:** Polyvinyl Butyral — laminated-safety-glass role
- **Source type:** retained material reference; replace with manufacturer/standard data when specifying a real vehicle
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polyvinyl_butyral

### REF-ABR-PC-LENS

- **Title:** Polycarbonate — automotive lens and hardcoat context
- **Source type:** retained secondary material reference
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polycarbonate
- **Requirement:** replace with OEM/material-supplier data for target-specific calibration

## Surface reconstruction

### REF-ABR-SUBD-FIT

- **Title:** Differentiable Subdivision Surface Fitting
- **Author:** Tianhao Xie
- **Year:** 2022
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.01685
- **Role:** research route for compact subdivision fitting to dense surfaces

### REF-KHRONOS-GLTF

- **Title:** glTF 2.0 Specification
- **Publisher:** Khronos Group
- **URL:** https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/specification/2.0/Specification.adoc
- **Role:** open transmission-format semantics for attributes, primitives, materials and tangents
- **Note:** game assets may use proprietary formats instead

## Brakes and rubber

### REF-ABR-RUBBER-WEAR

- **Title:** Rubber Wear: Experiment and Theory
- **Authors:** B. N. J. Persson, R. Xu, N. Miyashita
- **Year:** 2024
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.07332
- **Role:** tread wear and particle-scale process research

### REF-ABR-CARBON-CERAMIC

- **Title:** Ceramic Matrix Composite — C/SiC brake-disc process context
- **Source type:** retained secondary locator
- **URL:** https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ceramic_matrix_composite
- **Requirement:** use Brembo/SGL/OEM technical data for a target disc

# Initial conclusion

This v1 expansion establishes the correct body-wide architecture and practical rehabilitation system.

It does not claim that carbon, chrome, glass, lighting and geometry have already reached the depth of the embedded paint V12. The next iterations must deepen each category with:

- wider primary literature;
- OEM/manufacturer process disclosures;
- exact Blender node cards;
- live Blender 5.0.1 modifier behavior;
- experiments;
- visual observations;
- performance data;
- accepted/rejected implementations.

The important standard has already been preserved:

> No category is simplified merely because the overall task has become larger.

# Part XXVI — v2 corrective expansion

## ABR2-CORRECTION-001 — Why v1 was insufficient

Automotive Body v1 added only about 72.6 KB and 3,091 lines beyond the embedded Paint V12 corpus.

That was an architecture draft, not a body-wide research compendium at the standard established for paint.

V2 corrects the scope-depth mismatch by adding:

- detailed material identity cards;
- manufacturing-process cards;
- explicit optical-layer ownership;
- exact geometry requirements;
- Blender node-group specifications;
- Geometry Nodes preprocessing routes;
- part-specific game-asset salvage playbooks;
- modifier implementation and failure cards;
- normal, tangent and UV recovery rules;
- quantitative mesh and appearance metrics;
- cross-material integration rules;
- experiments and contradiction records;
- a larger primary/official source registry.

The v1 text remains preserved because its architecture is useful. V2 supersedes its completeness claim.

## ABR2-GOVERNING-001 — No filler rule

The purpose of this expansion is not to reach a target byte count.

A new section must add at least one of:

- a distinct real material or layer stack;
- a manufacturing cause;
- a geometry requirement;
- a Blender implementation route;
- a diagnostic;
- a failure signature;
- a falsifiable experiment;
- an audited source;
- a salvage decision.

Repeated generic PBR advice does not count as depth.

## ABR2-GOVERNING-002 — Material identity chain

Every material card follows:

```text
real name
→ substrate
→ manufacturing process
→ layer stack
→ optical response
→ characteristic geometry
→ scale-dependent representation
→ Blender implementation
→ diagnostics
→ failure modes
→ validation
```

## ABR2-GOVERNING-003 — Geometry salvage chain

Every game-asset repair follows:

```text
immutable source
→ import audit
→ object/LOD/material classification
→ geometry-versus-normal-versus-texture diagnosis
→ least-destructive intervention
→ reflection/silhouette validation
→ data transfer or rebake
→ motion/performance test
→ accepted/rejected evidence
```

# Part XXVII — Detailed automotive-body material cards


## ABR2-MAT-CF-PLAIN — Plain-weave exposed CFRP

**Family:** continuous woven composite

**Substrate:** carbon-fibre tows in thermoset or thermoplastic resin

**Manufacturing route:** woven reinforcement, lay-up/drape, consolidation, cure, surface finishing and often clearcoat

### Layer stack
- air
- optional clearcoat
- resin-rich mould face
- plain-weave tow layer
- lower plies/substrate

### Optical/appearance behavior
- dielectric clear/resin reflection
- dark absorbing fibres
- orthogonal tow direction
- crimp-driven local normals
- possible print-through

### Geometry requirements
- correct panel curvature
- stable warp/weft directions
- real cut edges for macro views
- seams and ply boundaries where present

### Blender representation
- CF_COORDINATE_STATE
- CF_WEAVE_TOPOLOGY plain mode
- CF_FIBRE_RESPONSE
- CF_RESIN
- CF_CLEARCOAT
- CF_LOD

### Common failure signatures
- flat checkerboard
- metallic carbon
- no over-under topology
- identical pitch across differently scaled objects
- camera-dependent weave

### Validation
- moving strip light
- object rotation
- macro pullback
- drape-direction AOV
- coat-on/coat-off ablation

### Reference links
- [REF-ABR-CFRP-PRINT]
- [REF-CG-FABRIC-CAPTURE-2024]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-TWILL2 — 2×2 twill exposed CFRP

**Family:** continuous woven composite

**Substrate:** carbon-fibre tows in resin

**Manufacturing route:** 2×2 twill weave, drape, consolidation and cosmetic finishing

### Layer stack
- clearcoat
- resin surface
- diagonal twill top ply
- lower plies

### Optical/appearance behavior
- diagonal repeating tow progression
- lower crimp than plain weave
- strong diagonal reading at medium distance
- resin/fibre contrast

### Geometry requirements
- weave must shear over compound curvature
- diagonal phase must remain continuous across intended cosmetic ply
- seams need deliberate placement

### Blender representation
- twill draft/index logic
- direction-field shear
- separate resin and fibre responses
- filtered pattern LOD

### Common failure signatures
- checkerboard instead of twill
- perfectly straight diagonals over compound form
- tow pattern too high contrast
- visible texture tiling

### Validation
- diagonal phase continuity
- curved coupon
- macro-to-full-car transition
- strip reflection across weave

### Reference links
- [REF-CG-FABRIC-CAPTURE-2024]
- [REF-CG-WOVEN-REALTIME-2024]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-TWILL4 — 4×4 twill exposed CFRP

**Family:** continuous woven composite

**Substrate:** wider/longer-float carbon tows in resin

**Manufacturing route:** long-float twill weave with cosmetic surface finishing

### Layer stack
- clearcoat
- resin
- 4×4 twill top ply
- support plies

### Optical/appearance behavior
- larger diagonal motifs
- longer floats
- different crimp and highlight breakup than 2×2
- higher risk of obvious repetition

### Geometry requirements
- pattern scale verified from reference
- local distortion and nesting
- real seam logic

### Blender representation
- parameterized weave draft
- tow-width and float-length controls
- macro pattern randomization constrained by weave rules

### Common failure signatures
- 2×2 pattern mislabeled 4×4
- generic noise replacing weave
- unphysical random over-under states

### Validation
- draft validation
- pattern period measurement
- LOD and aliasing ladder

### Reference links
- [REF-CG-FABRIC-CAPTURE-2024]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-SPREAD — Spread-tow carbon composite

**Family:** continuous woven composite

**Substrate:** flattened wide carbon tows with reduced gaps

**Manufacturing route:** spread-tow textile or tape lay-up, consolidation and clear finishing

### Layer stack
- clearcoat
- thin resin-rich surface
- wide flattened tow pattern
- lower plies

### Optical/appearance behavior
- broad relatively flat fibre bands
- reduced crimp
- fine filament direction within large cells
- subtle gap structure

### Geometry requirements
- wide cell dimensions
- low crimp height
- sharp but not perfect tow boundaries

### Blender representation
- large weave topology
- fine directional Gabor inside each tow
- low-amplitude crimp normal
- moment-filtered LOD

### Common failure signatures
- scaled-up twill
- thick rope-like tows
- excessive parallax
- equal resin gaps everywhere

### Validation
- broad-light banding
- compact-light filament cue
- macro reference comparison

### Reference links
- [REF-CG-WOVEN-REALTIME-2024]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-UD — Unidirectional exposed carbon laminate

**Family:** continuous unidirectional composite

**Substrate:** parallel carbon fibres in resin

**Manufacturing route:** UD tape or prepreg lay-up, consolidation and surface finishing

### Layer stack
- clearcoat
- resin
- parallel fibre layer
- cross-oriented structural plies

### Optical/appearance behavior
- strong one-axis fibre structure
- resin interface
- banding from tape edges or tow groups
- possible print-through of lower plies

### Geometry requirements
- continuous direction field
- tape boundaries
- ply drop-offs where visible

### Blender representation
- tangent-driven fibre response
- directional microstructure
- optional tape seam masks
- coat separation

### Common failure signatures
- woven checker on UD part
- anisotropic metal response without fibre/resin separation
- direction changing with camera

### Validation
- rotate object relative to strip
- direction AOV
- tape-edge inspection

### Reference links
- [REF-ABR-CFRP-PRINT]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-FORGED — Forged/chopped carbon composite

**Family:** discontinuous tow composite

**Substrate:** chopped carbon tow bundles in moulding resin

**Manufacturing route:** charge placement and compression moulding with flow-driven orientation

### Layer stack
- optional clearcoat
- resin-rich mould face
- overlapping chopped bundles
- bulk composite

### Optical/appearance behavior
- irregular elongated dark/grey bundles
- orientation-dependent local response
- resin pockets
- overlap and burial depth
- marbled rather than woven appearance

### Geometry requirements
- moulded part form
- bundle field attached to part
- cut/edge cross-section for macro shots

### Blender representation
- stable bundle IDs
- elliptical/local bundle masks
- orientation per bundle
- depth/coverage populations
- filtered homogeneous LOD

### Common failure signatures
- Voronoi stone pattern
- random white shards
- woven carbon normal map
- all bundles identical length

### Validation
- polarization-inspired orientation AOV
- bundle-size histogram
- compression-flow direction field
- distance filtering

### Reference links
- [REF-ABR-CFSMC-ORIENTATION]
- [REF-ABR-CFSMC-WIKI]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-SMC — Carbon-fibre sheet moulding compound body surface

**Family:** discontinuous composite

**Substrate:** chopped carbon tows in thermoset SMC matrix

**Manufacturing route:** sheet charge placement, compression flow, cure and mould-surface finishing

### Layer stack
- paint or clearcoat if specified
- moulded resin surface
- flow-oriented chopped reinforcement
- bulk SMC

### Optical/appearance behavior
- surface fibre orientation may correlate with bulk orientation
- weld-line and resin-pocket signatures
- mould texture

### Geometry requirements
- mould seams
- ribs/bosses on hidden side
- flow-related distortion
- parting lines

### Blender representation
- GN process-flow field
- bundle orientation
- weld-line masks
- mould texture
- paint/clear variants

### Common failure signatures
- purely random isotropic fragments on a strongly flowed part
- ignoring weld lines
- structural weave pattern

### Validation
- flow-field rotation
- weld-front collision
- surface-orientation statistics

### Reference links
- [REF-ABR-CFSMC-ORIENTATION]
- [REF-ABR-CFSMC-WIKI]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-CF-PAINTED — Painted carbon-composite panel

**Family:** coated composite

**Substrate:** CFRP/SMC panel beneath primer and automotive paint

**Manufacturing route:** composite manufacture, surface preparation, primer, basecoat and clearcoat

### Layer stack
- clearcoat
- paint layers
- primer/surfacer
- composite substrate

### Optical/appearance behavior
- paint dominates colour
- substrate may reveal print-through or sink at larger wavelengths
- edge chips expose composite

### Geometry requirements
- panel form
- print-through height
- different chip fracture than metal
- bonded seams

### Blender representation
- reuse Paint V12
- add composite substrate and print-through
- damage layer exposes fibres/resin

### Common failure signatures
- visible weave through intact opaque paint without evidence
- metallic chip substrate
- no substrate-specific edge damage

### Validation
- strip reflection
- chip cross-section
- print-through amplitude ladder

### Reference links
- [REF-ABR-CFRP-PRINT]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-ARAMID — Aramid/Kevlar exposed composite

**Family:** continuous fibre composite

**Substrate:** aramid fibres in resin

**Manufacturing route:** woven/UD reinforcement, lay-up and cure

### Layer stack
- clear/resin surface
- yellow/gold or dark aramid fibre
- lower plies

### Optical/appearance behavior
- different fibre colour from carbon
- fuzzy cut edges
- resin interface
- directional fibre cues

### Geometry requirements
- weave or UD direction
- frayed/cut edges if damaged
- seams

### Blender representation
- reuse weave topology
- aramid fibre colour/roughness
- fuzz only at valid damage/cut edges

- carbon shader recoloured yellow
- uniform fabric diffuse
- metallicity

### Validation

- fibre/resin ablation
- woven direction

### Reference links
- [REF-CG-FABRIC-CAPTURE-2024]

**Initial status:** `HYP` unless an existing embedded source gives a higher evidence state.


## ABR2-MAT-GLASSFIBRE — Glass-fibre composite

**Family:** continuous or chopped glass composite

**Substrate:** glass reinforcement in polymer matrix

**Manufacturing route:** SMC, RTM, hand lay-up, prepreg or thermoplastic forming

### Layer stack
- gelcoat/paint/clear where present
- resin
- glass reinforcement

### Optical/appearance behavior
- glass fibres can be lighter/translucent
- matrix often dominates
- print-through and fibre exposure vary

### Geometry requirements
- mould surface
- chopped or woven pattern only when exposed
- damage whitening

- dielectric fibre/resin model

- excessive transparency
- generic fibreglass noise

- damage whitening
- backlighting

- substrate

## ABR2-MAT-CHROME-METAL — Decorative chromium on metal

**Family:** electroplated metal trim

**Substrate:** steel, brass, zinc alloy, aluminium or other prepared metal

**Manufacturing route:** polishing/preparation, copper where used, bright/semi-bright nickel, thin decorative chromium

### Layer stack
- air
- chromium
- nickel system
- optional copper
- metal substrate

### Optical/appearance behavior
- mirror-like conductor stack
- surface finish inherited from preparation/nickel
- very high reflected-image sensitivity

### Geometry requirements
- real edge radius
- seams/fasteners
- plating-safe part form
- pits and peel at damage

### Blender representation
- conductor baseline
- micro-roughness
- optional thin tint
- scratch/pit/corrosion layers

### Common failure signatures
- Roughness 0 everywhere
- plastic edge geometry on metal part
- grey diffuse tint
- no reflected environment

### Validation
- neutral reflection cards
- DOI/roughness ladder
- damage cross-section

- [REF-ABR-CHROME-PLATING]
- [REF-ABR-NICKEL-PLATING]
