```json
}
```


# v4 conclusion

V4 closes the largest conceptual gaps left after the material and part atlases.

The master now treats an automotive body as a coupled system of:

- designed and assembled geometry;
- manufactured material stacks;
- joining and sealing;
- visible and sensor-wavelength optics;
- weather, water, contamination and cleaning;
- corrosion and repair history;
- source acquisition, uncertainty and data fusion;
- legal/production markings;
- thermal and mechanical state;
- OEM configuration.

The next bottleneck is no longer category coverage. It is experimental promotion: running the proposed diagnostics in Blender 5.0.1, capturing controlled samples and applying the source hierarchy to an actual vehicle asset.

# Part LXVI — Reflection shoulder, multi-scale roughness and the user-provided metal lead

## ABR5-LEAD-001 — Source intake

The user supplied a seven-page NoteGPT summary of a YouTube video titled:

> Rediscovering “Reflection Tailoff” and Forgotten Metal Workflows Inspired by Iron Man (2008)

The summary describes:

- a sharp primary reflected image;
- a broader soft halo or shoulder around it;
- multi-scale scratches and surface variation;
- a proposed dual-specular or multi-roughness implementation;
- claimed historical use of multiple specular lobes in the Iron Man suit shader;
- clearcoat and GGX-tail controls as alternatives.

### Evidence classification

- `LEAD`: useful observation and terminology lead;
- `SECONDARY`: generated summary of a video, not the original video or primary paper;
- `UNVERIFIED`: historical ILM implementation, expert correspondence and survey claims;
- `HYPOTHESIS`: universal claims about prevalence and cause;
- `ACTIONABLE`: controlled core-plus-shoulder experiments and Blender implementation.

The source is retained as:

`REF-ABR5-NOTEGPT-METAL-TAILOFF`

## ABR5-LEAD-002 — Internal contradiction in the summary

The summary states in one place that the two additive lobes were energy-conserving, then later states that the original additive reflections could break energy balance and that modern mixing is preferable.

Both claims cannot be accepted simultaneously without additional detail.

Possible reconciliations include:

- the historical implementation scaled or normalized the added lobes;
- the summary merged statements from different speakers or renderers;
- “additive” was used informally;
- the historical shader was intentionally non-conserving;
- the NoteGPT summary is inaccurate.

### Decision

Do not cite the summary as proof of the Iron Man shader architecture.

The historical claim remains:

`UNRESOLVED-HISTORICAL`

## ABR5-LEAD-003 — Terminology correction

“Reflection tailoff” is not treated as a stable scientific term in this master.

Use one of these terms according to the evidence:

- **reflection shoulder** — elevated energy around a sharp reflected core;
- **core-plus-halo profile** — descriptive image-space observation;
- **multi-lobe BRDF fit** — measured angular response requires several lobes;
- **mixed-NDF model** — several microfacet distributions are combined;
- **spatial roughness mixture** — smooth and rough regions coexist;
- **reflection haze** — small-angle scattered reflection around specular;
- **reduced distinctness of image** — reflected imagery becomes less sharp;
- **camera or post halo** — bloom, flare, clipping or denoising effect.

A heavy-tailed single GGX lobe and a true two-population roughness mixture are not the same model.

## ABR5-LEAD-004 — What is physically plausible

A sharp reflected core with a broader shoulder can plausibly arise from one or more of:

1. unresolved spatial mixture of smoother and rougher surface regions;
2. multiple microfacet populations;
3. directional scratch populations;
4. surface height and slope power across several spatial bands;
5. real coating over a metal substrate;
6. diffraction from sufficiently fine scratches;
7. camera point-spread, bloom, flare or clipping;
8. denoising and reconstruction filters;
9. a large or structured light source;
10. geometry and normal variation.

The existence of the appearance does not uniquely identify the cause.

## ABR5-LEAD-005 — Why a single roughness value can fail

A single roughness parameter compresses the surface into one microfacet distribution.

It may fail when the target contains:

- a narrow high-energy core;
- a broad lower-energy shoulder;
- different anisotropy in core and shoulder;
- spatially mixed intact and scratched regions;
- several process scales;
- a response that changes differently with source size or rotation.

The failure must be demonstrated by a controlled profile or held-out render, not assumed from appearance alone.

## ABR5-LEAD-006 — Why the clearcoat shortcut is often wrong

For bare or brushed metal, an artificial Principled coat creates a dielectric interface over the conductor.

That can introduce:

- dielectric Fresnel behavior;
- coat tint and absorption;
- substrate attenuation;
- a separate coat normal and roughness;
- a true vertical layer.

Use it only when the real part contains a coating, lacquer, oxide or film consistent with that architecture.

Do not use a dielectric coat merely because two reflected widths are visible.

## ABR5-LEAD-007 — Why unrestricted Add Shader is dangerous

Blender’s Add Shader adds two shader closures.

If two full-strength conductor shaders are added without explicit normalization, reflected energy can exceed the intended amount.

Default project rule:

- use a weighted Mix Shader for a spatial/statistical mixture;
- keep weights in `[0, 1]`;
- ensure `W_core + W_tail = 1`;
- reserve additive construction for a controlled closure model whose energy has been independently verified.

## ABR5-LEAD-008 — Spatial mixture versus vertical layer

### Spatial/statistical mixture

Different surface fractions have different roughness or anisotropy.

Conceptual form:

```text
f = (1 - w) f_core + w f_tail
```

This is the preferred interpretation for unresolved intact and scratched micro-regions.

### Vertical layer

Light crosses one interface and reaches another layer beneath it.

Examples:

- clearcoat over aluminium;
- lacquer over brass;
- protective polymer over vacuum metallisation.

These require layer transport and cannot be replaced automatically by a spatial Mix Shader.

## ABR5-LEAD-009 — Scratch diffraction is a separate branch

Fine scratches can create wavelength-dependent diffraction and iridescence.

This is different from a broad achromatic reflection shoulder.

The existing scratch-iridescence source demonstrates a scale transition from individually resolved scratch effects to a smoother aggregate response.

Project rule:

- ordinary multiscale roughness branch for broad reflection structure;
- wave-optical scratch branch only when coloured diffraction is actually present;
- do not generate rainbow scratches as generic metal wear.

---

# Part LXVII — Automotive relevance of multi-scale reflection structure

## ABR5-AUTO-001 — High-value automotive applications

### Brushed stainless brightwork

Likely useful for:

- window surrounds;
- sill plates;
- roof rails;
- grille strips;
- exhaust trim.

Expected structure:

- sharp conductor core from smoother regions;
- broad anisotropic shoulder from abrasive grooves;
- sparse deeper scratches;
- fingerprints and water spots as separate condition layers.

### Brushed or satin aluminium

Likely useful for:

- window trim;
- mirror accents;
- wheel details;
- interior-visible exterior trim;
- roof rails;
- technical aero hardware.

The broad component may be more isotropic after blasting or more anisotropic after brushing.

### Diamond-cut and machined wheel faces

Likely useful because:

- clearcoat may form a true dielectric lobe;
- machined aluminium supplies the conductor response;
- tool grooves create directional or radial scattering;
- damage can remove clearcoat and expose oxidizing metal.

This may require both:

- actual coat layering;
- multiscale metal roughness beneath it.

### Polished exhaust tips

Useful for:

- sharp retained reflections;
- polishing haze;
- circumferential or axial scratches;
- soot and oxide contamination.

### Satin or black PVD trim

Potentially useful, but the real stack must be identified.

Possible sources of shoulder:

- PVD film microfinish;
- topcoat;
- substrate preparation;
- scratches;
- deposited-layer roughness.

### Plated ABS and chrome-like trim

Potentially useful when:

- mould and plating preserve a sharp core;
- substrate preparation, topcoat or wear create haze;
- the target is not a perfect mirror finish.

The correct substrate and damage behavior remain mandatory.

## ABR5-AUTO-002 — Related non-metal applications

The underlying **core-plus-shoulder observation** also applies beyond metals, but the BSDF model changes.

### Automotive clearcoat

Swirls and micro-scratches can produce:

- sharp reflected-image core;
- broader reflection haze;
- reduced DOI.

Both responses belong to the dielectric coat or its spatial variation.

### Piano black

A high-gloss dielectric coating can show:

- sharp core;
- broad micro-scratch shoulder;
- dust and fingerprints;
- substrate distortion.

Use two dielectric roughness populations or resolved coat damage, not two metallic lobes.

### Clearcoated carbon

The sharp core and haze belong primarily to:

- outer clearcoat;
- resin surface;
- print-through and weave beneath.

The fibre/resin response must not be duplicated as a generic metal tail.

### Glass and hard-coated polycarbonate

Small-angle scatter can lower reflected-image quality.

Use a transparent layered model or roughness mixture consistent with the surface, not a metal workflow.

## ABR5-AUTO-003 — Low-value or inappropriate uses

Avoid the new metal mixture by default for:

- perfectly polished mirror chrome;
- extremely rough cast metal with no narrow core;
- ordinary metallic automotive paint flakes;
- passive red reflectors;
- tyre rubber;
- uncoated textured plastic;
- diffuse corrosion;
- genuine dielectric clearcoat already represented physically;
- camera bloom falsely attributed to the material.

## ABR5-AUTO-004 — Part applicability matrix

| Part/material | Core-plus-shoulder value | Preferred first model |
|---|---:|---|
| brushed stainless window trim | high | resolved scratches, then weighted conductor mixture |
| satin aluminium trim | high | measured-scale roughness bands or conductor mixture |
| diamond-cut wheel under clear | high | dielectric coat over machined metal plus metal microstructure |
| polished exhaust tip | high | conductor core/tail plus condition |
| chrome-plated trim | medium | plating stack first; mixture only after evidence |
| black PVD trim | medium | deposited-layer stack plus measured/fitted roughness |
| piano-black trim | high | dielectric coat roughness mixture |
| scratched clearcoat | high | coat-domain mixture and DOI/haze test |
| carbon clearcoat | medium | coat haze plus composite layers |
| raw cast rotor | low | broad process roughness and state, usually no sharp core |
| metallic paint | low for this model | Paint V12 effect-pigment architecture |
| grille opacity card | none | geometry/LOD problem |

---

# Part LXVIII — Blender 5.0.1 node practicality

## ABR5-NODE-001 — `METAL_CORE_SHOULDER_B50`

**Status:** `SRC-V`, `HYP`  
**Purpose:** Energy-bounded statistical mixture of two conductor responses.

### Inputs

- `Metal Color`
- `Core Roughness`
- `Shoulder Roughness`
- `Core Anisotropic`
- `Shoulder Anisotropic`
- `Core Rotation`
- `Shoulder Rotation`
- `Tangent`
- `Shoulder Coverage`
- `Normal`
- `Enabled`

### Outputs

- `BSDF`
- `Core Weight`
- `Shoulder Weight`
- `Core Roughness AOV`
- `Shoulder Roughness AOV`
- `Coverage AOV`
- `Anisotropy Direction AOV`

### Stock-node construction

```text
Principled Core:
    Base Color = Metal Color
    Metallic = 1
    Roughness = Core Roughness
    Anisotropic = Core Anisotropic
    Anisotropic Rotation = Core Rotation
    Tangent = Tangent
    Normal = Normal

Principled Shoulder:
    Base Color = Metal Color
    Metallic = 1
    Roughness = Shoulder Roughness
    Anisotropic = Shoulder Anisotropic
    Anisotropic Rotation = Shoulder Rotation
    Tangent = Tangent
    Normal = Normal

Mix Shader:
    Shader 1 = Principled Core
    Shader 2 = Principled Shoulder
    Factor = clamp(Shoulder Coverage, 0, 1)
```

### Governing constraints

```text
Shoulder Roughness > Core Roughness
Core Weight = 1 - Shoulder Coverage
Shoulder Weight = Shoulder Coverage
Core Weight + Shoulder Weight = 1
```

These are model constraints, not universal measured values.

### Prohibitions

- Add Shader with two unrestricted full-strength Principled nodes;
- coat used as the shoulder unless a real coating exists;
- different metal colours without target evidence;
- camera-facing masks;
- Light Path used to fabricate the direct reflection;
- shoulder factor derived from AO.

### Engine scope

- Cycles: primary research and hero path;
- EEVEE: visually comparable branch must be tested;
- MaterialX/export: mixture semantics require export verification;
- OSL: optional research path for fitted distributions.

## ABR5-NODE-002 — `METAL_RESOLVED_MICROFINISH_B50`

**Purpose:** Close-range representation where scratches and process texture remain spatially resolved.

### Inputs

- physical position;
- process tangent;
- fine scratch spectrum;
- medium scratch spectrum;
- sparse deep scratches;
- base roughness;
- scratch roughness increase;
- scratch normal amplitude;
- anisotropy;
- LOD.

### Active model

One primary conductor BSDF receives:

- physically scaled roughness variation;
- tangent-aligned anisotropy;
- filtered normal or bump;
- sparse condition scratches.

This is preferred over duplicated lobes while the microstructure is resolvable.

### Failure checks

- white scratch colour;
- visible procedural stripes;
- roughness and normal driven by identical high-contrast field;
- pattern swims under rotation;
- denoiser erases the whole process;
- feature scale changes by object bounds.

## ABR5-NODE-003 — `METAL_MICROFINISH_LOD_B50`

### Representation tiers

#### Tier A — Resolved

- explicit height/normal;
- roughness map;
- directional process field;
- one conductor BSDF.

#### Tier B — Transitional

- lower-frequency resolved process;
- filtered fine normal;
- partial weighted shoulder.

#### Tier C — Statistical mixture

- core-plus-shoulder BSDF mixture;
- no visible micro-scratch pattern;
- preserved mean and anisotropy.

#### Tier D — Single-lobe fallback

- only after the shoulder is below the shot’s significance threshold.

### Weight condition

```text
W_A + W_B + W_C + W_D = 1
```

Use overlapping smooth transitions based on projected feature size and shot tier.

## ABR5-NODE-004 — `DIELECTRIC_HAZE_SHOULDER_B50`

**Applications:**

- scratched clearcoat;
- piano black;
- clearcoated carbon;
- hard-coated transparent polymer.

Use two dielectric surface populations or a resolved roughness field.

Do not set Metallic to one.

If a true base substrate lies beneath the dielectric surface, use proper vertical layering first.

## ABR5-NODE-005 — `REFLECTION_PROFILE_DIAGNOSTIC_B50`

### Outputs

- core/shoulder coverage;
- roughness values;
- anisotropy values;
- tangent encoding;
- resolved scratch bands;
- LOD weights;
- coat presence;
- material family ID.

### Contribution isolation limitation

Stock shader AOVs can record values and colours from the graph, but they do not automatically provide independent radiance contributions for arbitrary internal BSDF branches.

For reliable contribution comparison, render controlled variants:

1. core only;
2. shoulder only;
3. combined mixture;
4. resolved microfinish;
5. actual coat if present;
6. single-lobe baseline.

## ABR5-NODE-006 — `METAL_SHOULDER_CAPTURE_RIG`

**Domain:** scene asset, not material node group.

### Required components

- flat metal coupon;
- gently curved coupon;
- cylinder;
- long narrow strip light;
- compact rectangular light;
- broad softbox;
- black and white reflection cards;
- fixed orthographic or long-lens diagnostic camera;
- unclamped raw EXR output;
- post-processing disabled;
- deterministic render settings.

### Camera states

- reflection profile across the process direction;
- profile along the process direction;
- normal incidence;
- grazing incidence;
- rotating coupon;
- fixed light with rotating tangent.

## ABR5-NODE-007 — Blender API socket guard

Source-verified Blender 5.0 Principled sockets relevant to this model include:

- `Base Color`
- `Metallic`
- `Roughness`
- `Normal`
- `Anisotropic`
- `Anisotropic Rotation`
- `Tangent`

The Mix Shader declares a factor and two shader inputs.

The Add Shader declares two shader inputs and directly adds them.

Scripts must still inspect the running Blender 5.0.1 node sockets before constructing or modifying the graph.

## ABR5-NODE-008 — Practical graph-construction template

```python
from __future__ import annotations

import bpy

REQUIRED_VERSION = (5, 0, 1)

def require_version() -> None:
    running = tuple(bpy.app.version[:3])
    if running != REQUIRED_VERSION:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Expected Blender {REQUIRED_VERSION}, running {running}."
        )

def find_input(node, *candidates):
    by_name = {socket.name: socket for socket in node.inputs}
    by_identifier = {
        getattr(socket, "identifier", ""): socket
        for socket in node.inputs
    }

    for candidate in candidates:
        if candidate in by_name:
            return by_name[candidate]
        if candidate in by_identifier:
            return by_identifier[candidate]

    raise KeyError(
        f"{node.bl_idname} lacks expected input {candidates}. "
        f"Available names: {sorted(by_name)}"
    )

def set_value(node, value, *socket_names) -> None:
    socket = find_input(node, *socket_names)
    socket.default_value = value

def build_core_shoulder_material(
    name: str = "ABR_Metal_Core_Shoulder",
    metal_color=(0.6, 0.62, 0.65, 1.0),
    core_roughness: float = 0.08,
    shoulder_roughness: float = 0.32,
    shoulder_coverage: float = 0.22,
):
    require_version()

    if not 0.0 <= shoulder_coverage <= 1.0:
        raise ValueError("shoulder_coverage must be in [0, 1].")
    if not 0.0 <= core_roughness <= 1.0:
        raise ValueError("core_roughness must be in [0, 1].")
    if not 0.0 <= shoulder_roughness <= 1.0:
        raise ValueError("shoulder_roughness must be in [0, 1].")
    if shoulder_roughness <= core_roughness:
        raise ValueError(
            "Shoulder roughness should exceed core roughness "
            "for this diagnostic model."
        )

    material = bpy.data.materials.get(name) or bpy.data.materials.new(name)
    material.use_nodes = True
    tree = material.node_tree
    tree.nodes.clear()

    output = tree.nodes.new("ShaderNodeOutputMaterial")
    output.name = "ABR_Output"

    core = tree.nodes.new("ShaderNodeBsdfPrincipled")
    core.name = "ABR_Core"
    set_value(core, metal_color, "Base Color")
    set_value(core, 1.0, "Metallic")
    set_value(core, core_roughness, "Roughness")

    shoulder = tree.nodes.new("ShaderNodeBsdfPrincipled")
    shoulder.name = "ABR_Shoulder"
    set_value(shoulder, metal_color, "Base Color")
    set_value(shoulder, 1.0, "Metallic")
    set_value(shoulder, shoulder_roughness, "Roughness")

    mix = tree.nodes.new("ShaderNodeMixShader")
    mix.name = "ABR_Core_Shoulder_Mix"
    set_value(mix, shoulder_coverage, "Factor", "Fac")

    tree.links.new(core.outputs["BSDF"], mix.inputs[1])
    tree.links.new(shoulder.outputs["BSDF"], mix.inputs[2])
    tree.links.new(mix.outputs["Shader"], output.inputs["Surface"])

    core.location = (-420, 100)
    shoulder.location = (-420, -120)
    mix.location = (-120, 20)
    output.location = (160, 20)

    return material
```

### Template limitations

- not executed in this environment;
- no tangent or anisotropy wiring yet;
- no diagnostic AOVs;
- no material-group interface;
- no proof of physical fit;
- defaults are test values, not automotive constants.

---

# Part LXIX — Observation and fitting workflow

## ABR5-OBS-001 — Observation must precede shader selection

Record the raw observation before naming the cause.

Example:

```text
OBSERVATION:
A narrow reflected strip has a sharp centre and a softer wider shoulder.

NOT YET ESTABLISHED:
The material has two physical layers.
```

## ABR5-OBS-002 — Observation ledger schema

```json
{
  "observation_id": null,
  "asset_or_sample": null,
  "part": null,
  "material_candidate": null,
  "date": null,
  "observer": null,
  "capture_or_render": {},
  "raw_observation": null,
  "image_regions": [],
  "measured_profile": null,
  "candidate_causes": [],
  "discriminating_tests": [],
  "current_interpretation": null,
  "confidence": null,
  "contradictions": [],
  "decision": "OPEN"
}
```

## ABR5-OBS-003 — Reflection profile measurement

Use a linear RAW or EXR image.

Across a reflected strip:

1. choose a region free from silhouette and panel curvature changes;
2. sample luminance perpendicular to the reflection;
3. record exposure and light geometry;
4. normalize only with a declared method;
5. retain unclipped values;
6. compare several positions and frames.

Useful profile observations:

- core full width at half maximum;
- shoulder width;
- shoulder energy fraction;
- left/right asymmetry;
- anisotropy ratio;
- far-tail slope;
- clipping;
- noise floor.

## ABR5-OBS-004 — Candidate model comparison

Compare at least:

- single Principled conductor;
- spatial roughness map on one conductor;
- two-conductor weighted Mix Shader;
- actual clearcoat or coating stack;
- resolved scratch geometry or bump;
- camera/post-only halo.

A model is accepted only if it explains held-out conditions.

## ABR5-OBS-005 — Discriminating tests

### Source-size test

Vary light size while keeping incident exposure controlled.

Purpose:

- distinguish intrinsic angular scattering from the convolution with the light source.

### Process-direction test

Rotate the surface or tangent direction relative to the light.

Purpose:

- reveal anisotropic shoulder structure.

### Resolution test

Render several output resolutions and distances.

Purpose:

- detect unresolved explicit scratches and unstable LOD.

### Post-processing test

Disable:

- bloom;
- glare;
- lens flare;
- clipping;
- denoising;
- temporal accumulation;
- sharpening.

Purpose:

- establish whether the halo is in the material transport or display pipeline.

### Coating test

Compare:

- bare metal;
- coated metal;
- scratched coating;
- scratched substrate.

Purpose:

- separate spatial roughness mixture from true vertical layering.

### Polarisation test

Useful only with careful interpretation.

Metals and coatings have polarization-dependent reflection. Cross-polarization is not assumed to cleanly separate “diffuse” and “specular” metal components.

## ABR5-OBS-006 — Held-out validation

Fit the material under one or two controlled conditions.

Validate under:

- different light size;
- different light orientation;
- different camera angle;
- grazing view;
- motion;
- different resolution;
- a second physical sample region.

A shader that matches only the fitting frame remains `CONTEXT-DEPENDENT`.

## ABR5-OBS-007 — Practical stop rule

Do not add a second lobe when:

- a single BSDF plus correctly scaled roughness/normal map matches the target;
- the halo disappears when bloom is disabled;
- geometry or normals explain the broad reflection;
- the target has a real coating already handled by the stack;
- the improvement is below delivery significance;
- performance cost is not justified.

---

# Part LXX — Knowledge-base practicality upgrades

## ABR5-KB-001 — Research-lead intake gate

Every new video, forum post, AI summary or anecdote receives:

1. source identity;
2. original source availability;
3. claims extracted;
4. internal contradictions;
5. primary-source search;
6. automotive applicability;
7. Blender implementation candidate;
8. falsifiable experiment;
9. confidence;
10. decision.

No interesting claim goes directly into the active technical doctrine.

## ABR5-KB-002 — Observation versus interpretation

The master must separate:

- what was seen;
- what was measured;
- what is inferred;
- what was implemented;
- what passed;
- what remains unresolved.

Required vocabulary:

- `OBS-RAW`
- `OBS-MEASURED`
- `INTERPRETATION`
- `IMPLEMENTED`
- `RENDER-OBSERVED`
- `MOTION-VALIDATED`
- `ASSET-QUALIFIED`
- `REJECTED`

## ABR5-KB-003 — Node-card practicality contract

Every node group must state:

- exact Blender baseline;
- exact node types;
- exact socket names or runtime discovery;
- input and output data types;
- units;
- coordinate spaces;
- attribute domains;
- engine support;
- lobe/material ownership;
- dependencies;
- safe parameter relations;
- diagnostics;
- AOV limitations;
- performance;
- fallback;
- experiment IDs;
- failure signatures;
- migration notes.

A conceptual diagram without these fields is not an implementation card.

## ABR5-KB-004 — Minimal work package

Every implementation begins as a small work package:

```text
one phenomenon
one test object
one controlled rig
one node group
one baseline
one diagnostic set
one experiment
one decision
```

Do not build the complete vehicle material before the smallest test explains the effect.

## ABR5-KB-005 — Node graph ablation

For each meaningful branch:

- branch off;
- branch only;
- combined;
- exaggerated;
- target value;
- distance/LOD variant.

If a branch cannot be isolated, it is difficult to debug and should be redesigned.

## ABR5-KB-006 — Value AOV versus contribution render

### Value AOV

Use for:

- masks;
- roughness;
- orientation;
- weights;
- LOD;
- coordinates;
- process fields.

### Contribution render

Use controlled material variants for:

- core-only reflected radiance;
- shoulder-only reflected radiance;
- coat-only appearance;
- effect-only appearance.

Do not claim that a scalar AOV is the radiance contribution.

## ABR5-KB-007 — Practicality score

Score every proposed system from zero to five.

### P0 — Idea

No graph or experiment.

### P1 — Specified

Inputs, outputs and logic exist.

### P2 — Buildable

Exact Blender nodes/sockets and dependencies identified.

### P3 — Executed

Runs in Blender 5.0.1.

### P4 — Observed

Controlled still evidence and diagnostics exist.

### P5 — Production qualified

Motion, performance, fresh-file and target-asset validation pass.

Research depth does not imply practicality grade.

## ABR5-KB-008 — Cost ledger

Record:

- node count;
- texture count and memory;
- procedural frequency;
- generated geometry;
- shader compile time;
- render time;
- sample requirement;
- denoiser dependence;
- EEVEE/Cycles parity;
- AOV overhead;
- quality-tier cost.

## ABR5-KB-009 — Dependency and ownership graph

Every parameter has one primary owner.

Examples:

- metal microfinish owns metal roughness structure;
- coating owns coat roughness and absorption;
- geometry owns silhouette and macro form;
- camera owns bloom and flare;
- condition owns later scratches and contamination;
- LOD owns representation weights.

A second node may modulate an owned value only through an explicit connection.

## ABR5-KB-010 — Assumption register

Every experiment lists assumptions such as:

- isotropic or anisotropic;
- distant or near-field light;
- RGB or spectral;
- resolved or subpixel structure;
- rigid or deforming geometry;
- clearcoat present or absent;
- clean or aged state.

A result cannot be generalized outside its assumptions without a new test.

## ABR5-KB-011 — Blender source and runtime verification

Verification order:

1. pinned Blender source or official manual;
2. runtime socket dump in Blender 5.0.1;
3. generated test graph;
4. still render;
5. motion;
6. target asset;
7. migration test.

Source code proves availability and declared semantics, not rendered correctness.

## ABR5-KB-012 — Work-priority rule

Prioritize by:

```text
visual impact
× uncertainty
× reuse across parts
× ease of falsification
÷ implementation cost
```

This prevents rare exotic effects from displacing basic geometry, scale and material identity.

## ABR5-KB-013 — Project observation appendix

The master should contain a dedicated append-only area for actual project observations.

Each observation must include:

- screenshot/render filename;
- scene and material version;
- light/camera;
- visible symptom;
- suspected domain;
- test performed;
- outcome;
- next decision.

No observation is promoted into universal doctrine from one vehicle or shot.

## ABR5-KB-014 — Failure-to-action mapping

Every failure record should identify the next smallest action.

Example:

```text
symptom: reflected strip has unexplained halo
next action: disable post and render single-BSDF baseline
not: rebuild the entire material
```

## ABR5-KB-015 — Search completeness check

Before declaring a category exhausted, ask:

- primary optical literature searched?
- manufacturing/process sources searched?
- OEM or supplier disclosures searched?
- measurement standards searched?
- Blender implementation checked?
- alternative physical causes retained?
- negative results recorded?
- one practical experiment specified?

---

# Part LXXI — New experiments and contradiction records

## EXP-ABR5-001 — Single lobe versus weighted mixture

**Hypothesis:** A weighted mixture is justified only when one Principled conductor cannot fit both reflected core and shoulder.

### Setup

- flat brushed-metal coupon;
- narrow strip light;
- raw EXR;
- single-lobe baseline;
- weighted two-conductor mixture;
- matched mean reflected energy.

### Decision

Accept mixture only if it improves the profile and held-out views materially.

## EXP-ABR5-002 — Explicit scratch field versus statistical mixture

**Hypothesis:** Resolved scratch fields are superior at macro range; the mixture is more stable once scratches become subpixel.

### Matrix

- camera distance;
- resolution;
- sample count;
- denoiser;
- scratch scale;
- mixture weight.

## EXP-ABR5-003 — Add Shader energy test

Compare:

- unrestricted Add Shader;
- Mix Shader;
- explicitly scaled Add construction;
- single-lobe baseline.

Use a controlled white environment and record average reflected output.

## EXP-ABR5-004 — Clearcoat false-positive test

Fit the same core-plus-shoulder target using:

- bare-metal mixture;
- dielectric coat over metal;
- roughness texture;
- camera bloom.

Validate under grazing angle and changed environment colour.

## EXP-ABR5-005 — Light-source-size discrimination

Hold exposure approximately constant while varying source width.

Record core and shoulder widths.

## EXP-ABR5-006 — Anisotropy rotation

Rotate tangent direction while holding geometry and light fixed.

A brushed-metal shoulder should rotate with the process frame.

## EXP-ABR5-007 — Post-processing separation

Render:

- raw;
- denoised;
- bloom;
- glare;
- clipped display;
- compressed delivery.

Record which pipeline stage creates or alters the halo.

## EXP-ABR5-008 — Diamond-cut wheel model

Compare:

- single rough conductor;
- radial grooves;
- conductor mixture;
- clearcoat over radial machined metal;
- production LOD.

Run wheel rotation and shutter-duration matrix.

## EXP-ABR5-009 — Chrome versus plated-plastic shoulder

Use identical visible colour but different:

- substrate geometry;
- coating stack;
- microfinish;
- damage.

## EXP-ABR5-010 — Piano-black dielectric version

Compare one dielectric roughness map with a weighted dielectric core/shoulder mixture.

Do not use metallic lobes.

## EXP-ABR5-011 — Clearcoat swirl haze

Use a clean automotive clearcoat and a swirl-conditioned state.

Measure reflected strip profile and DOI proxy.

## EXP-ABR5-012 — Carbon clearcoat shoulder

Hold carbon fibre/resin response fixed and vary only outer-coat condition.

## EXP-ABR5-013 — Scratch-diffraction boundary

Compare ordinary achromatic scratch roughness against a wave-optical reference or approved approximation.

Only retain diffraction where coloured behavior is observed.

## EXP-ABR5-014 — Profile fitting holdout

Fit one light/view pair and validate on at least three held-out conditions.

## EXP-ABR5-015 — Tangent-frame error

Deliberately misalign the tangent and record how the shoulder orientation fails.

## EXP-ABR5-016 — LOD conservation

Test resolved, transitional, statistical and single-lobe tiers through a camera pullback.

Record mean energy, anisotropy, reflection width and temporal derivative.

## EXP-ABR5-017 — EEVEE and Cycles comparison

Use the same node graph and controlled rig.

Record:

- reflected profile;
- shadow/reflection differences;
- performance;
- instability;
- engine-specific fallback.

## EXP-ABR5-018 — Denoiser threshold

Find the sample and feature-scale region where the denoiser removes or merges the shoulder.

## EXP-ABR5-019 — Material family blind test

Render brushed stainless, satin aluminium, chrome, PVD and piano black without labels.

Determine whether the model preserves material identity rather than producing the same two-lobe look.

## EXP-ABR5-020 — Fresh-file node reconstruction

Run the guarded Blender script in a clean 5.0.1 file, dump sockets and render the diagnostic rig.

## CTR-ABR5-001 — “Reflection tailoff” is a universal metal law

**Status:** rejected as an unqualified universal statement.

It is a useful observed appearance family whose prevalence and cause depend on material and process.

## CTR-ABR5-002 — Two visible widths prove two physical layers

**Status:** rejected.

Possible causes include spatial mixture, several NDF populations, coating, geometry, camera/post and light structure.

## CTR-ABR5-003 — Additive lobes are automatically energy-conserving

**Status:** rejected.

Normalization or constrained weights are required.

## CTR-ABR5-004 — Mix Shader is always physical layering

**Status:** rejected.

It can represent a weighted material or spatial mixture; physical layer transport requires a layer model.

## CTR-ABR5-005 — Clearcoat is a cheap universal second lobe

**Status:** rejected for bare-metal microfinish.

## CTR-ABR5-006 — Fine scratches always mean higher roughness

**Status:** unresolved/context-dependent.

Depth, spacing, orientation, wavelength and PSD determine the angular response.

## CTR-ABR5-007 — One successful beauty render validates the model

**Status:** rejected.

Held-out light, view, motion, resolution and post tests are mandatory.

---

# Part LXXII — v5 reference registry

## REF-ABR5-NOTEGPT-METAL-TAILOFF

- **Title:** Rediscovering “Reflection Tailoff” and Forgotten Metal Workflows Inspired by Iron Man (2008)
- **Source:** user-provided NoteGPT PDF summary of a YouTube video
- **Date received:** 15 July 2026
- **Type:** secondary AI-generated summary / research lead
- **Supports:** discovery of the core-plus-shoulder appearance and proposed multi-roughness workflow
- **Does not establish:** historical ILM implementation, expert correspondence, survey data, universality, physical cause or best practice
- **Audit:** all seven rendered pages inspected
- **Known contradiction:** additive lobes described as both energy-conserving and potentially energy-breaking

## REF-ABR5-BL-PRINCIPLED-SOURCE

- **Title:** `node_shader_bsdf_principled.cc`
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation source repository
- **Pinned commit:** `861ef025be6a6bc5985e857f3d74a1dbad5b2b23`
- **URL:** https://github.com/blender/blender/blob/861ef025be6a6bc5985e857f3d74a1dbad5b2b23/source/blender/nodes/shader/nodes/node_shader_bsdf_principled.cc
- **Supports:** Blender 5.0 socket declarations for Base Color, Metallic, Roughness, Normal, Anisotropic, Anisotropic Rotation, Tangent and Coat
- **Does not establish:** suitability or visual correctness of the proposed mixture

## REF-ABR5-BL-MIX-SOURCE

- **Title:** `node_shader_mix_shader.cc`
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation source repository
- **Pinned commit:** `861ef025be6a6bc5985e857f3d74a1dbad5b2b23`
- **URL:** https://github.com/blender/blender/blob/861ef025be6a6bc5985e857f3d74a1dbad5b2b23/source/blender/nodes/shader/nodes/node_shader_mix_shader.cc
- **Supports:** factor-bounded mixing between two shader inputs

## REF-ABR5-BL-ADD-SOURCE

- **Title:** `node_shader_add_shader.cc`
- **Publisher:** Blender Foundation source repository
- **Pinned commit:** `861ef025be6a6bc5985e857f3d74a1dbad5b2b23`
- **URL:** https://github.com/blender/blender/blob/861ef025be6a6bc5985e857f3d74a1dbad5b2b23/source/blender/nodes/shader/nodes/node_shader_add_shader.cc
- **Supports:** direct addition of two shader inputs
- **Boundary:** no automatic user-visible normalization control is declared by the node

## REF-ABR5-LAFORTUNE-1997

- **Title:** Non-Linear Approximation of Reflectance Functions
- **Authors:** Eric P. F. Lafortune, Sing-Choong Foo, Kenneth E. Torrance, Donald P. Greenberg
- **Year:** 1997
- **Type:** peer-reviewed SIGGRAPH research
- **Supports:** fitted reflectance models using multiple specular lobes
- **Boundary:** a general reflectance fit does not identify the manufacturing cause

## REF-ABR5-SCRATCH-IRID-2017

- **Title:** Scratch Iridescence: Wave-Optical Rendering of Diffractive Surface Structure
- **Authors:** Sebastian Werner, Zdravko Velinov, Wenzel Jakob, Matthias B. Hullin
- **Year:** 2017
- **URL:** https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.06086
- **Supports:** scratch-scale wave-optical effects and transition from resolved scratch response to smoother aggregate appearance
- **Boundary:** diffraction is not the default explanation for an achromatic reflection shoulder

## REF-ABR5-NDFF-2016

- **Title:** Filtering Distributions of Normals for Shading Antialiasing
- **Authors:** Anton S. Kaplanyan, Stephen Hill, Anjul Patney, Aaron Lefohn
- **Year:** 2016
- **URL:** https://research.nvidia.com/sites/default/files/pubs/2016-06_Filtering-Distributions-of/NDFFiltering.pdf
- **Supports:** filtering normal distributions over pixel footprints and temporal stability
- **Link:** already retained in the embedded Paint V12 source

## REF-ABR5-OPENPBR

- **Title:** OpenPBR Surface Specification
- **Publisher:** Academy Software Foundation
- **Version:** 1.1.1
- **URL:** https://academysoftwarefoundation.github.io/OpenPBR/
- **Supports:** distinction between vertical layer architecture and material mixing

## REF-ABR5-ASTM-E430

- **Title:** Standard Test Methods for Measurement of Gloss of High-Gloss Surfaces by Goniophotometry
- **Publisher:** ASTM International
- **Identifier:** ASTM E430
- **Type:** measurement standard
- **Supports:** gloss, reflection haze and distinctness-of-image measurement context
- **Access:** full current text may require purchase or subscription

## REF-ABR5-IRONMAN-HISTORICAL

- **Claim:** Iron Man production shader used a specific dual-lobe implementation and expert correspondence confirmed it
- **Status:** unresolved
- **Reason:** the current audit did not locate a sufficiently direct primary source matching the summary’s exact claims
- **Rule:** retain as a search lead; do not present as established history

# v5 conclusion

The user-provided video summary was useful because it exposed a missing practical category:

> materials whose reflected image contains a sharp core and a broader process-dependent shoulder that one roughness value may not reproduce.

The contribution has been retained without accepting unsupported history or universal claims.

The broader knowledge base is now stricter about:

- raw observations;
- source leads;
- internal contradictions;
- exact Blender sockets;
- energy-bounded graph construction;
- AOV limitations;
- minimal experiments;
- performance;
- held-out validation;
- implementation grades;
- actual project evidence.
